=== Page 109 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Town Clerk and Board of Registrars
Department Mission
The Town Clerk’s Office fulfills mandates of local, state, and federal governments as well as the
needs of the general public for a variety of services. The Town Clerk is the chief election officer
and is responsible for all election-related activities for Town elections and certain election-related
activities for State and Federal elections. In addition to elections, the Town Clerk’s office is a busy
office on a daily basis: receiving and certifying nominations and petitions (which continue in off-
election years); issuing and recording vital records (burial permits, marriage licenses, and birth,
marriage and death certificates); issuing and receiving the Annual Town Census (updating the
street list, following up with voter registration); updating voter registration; issuing and
maintaining dog licenses and rabies certificates; issuing business certificates and storage of
flammable licenses; certifying grants of location; posting meeting notices and reviewing them for
Open Meeting Law compliance, and receiving public meeting minutes; receiving and maintaining
records of planning and zoning decision and appeals for proper recording with the Registry of
Deeds; and issuing auction and raffle permits. The Town Clerk is the primary Records Access
Officer, receiving, assigning, and responding to public record requests. The Town Clerk swears in
and orients all elected and appointed officials and provides Open Meeting Law materials, monitors
and enforces Conflict of Interest and Ethics training and mandates, and monitors and enforces
campaign finance reporting. The Town Clerk is also part of the Town’s finance team and maintains
the Department of Revenue Division of Local Services financial gateway and ensures procedural
compliance with State finance law requirements. The Needham Town Clerk’s Office is one of the
few in the State that also has a sufficient number of staff members qualified to administer oaths,
resulting in officials (primarily Notary Public) choosing to get sworn in Needham. In addition, the
Town Clerk’s Office provides notary services to residents and businesses.
On the Horizon
FY2027 has one scheduled State primary election, one scheduled State (including certain Federal
offices) election, and one scheduled Town Election. In preparation for the State elections that will
take place in the fall of 2027, the Office is already busy with certifying the 40 initiative petitions
that could become ballot questions. During elections, the complex operational mandates of
elections, following changes to State law in June 2022, require that most staff hours be prioritized
to address those mandates. Each election requires between 4 and 8 weeks of additional staffing,
and over 80 temporary employees are called upon and must be coordinated to complete all
necessary tasks. The Town Clerk is purchasing 2 new high speed vote tabulators in FY2026, which
will be used during the Annual Town Election in April 2026, allowing the Town Clerk’s Office to
become familiar with the machines. In addition, the Town Clerk has worked with the Secretary of
the Commonwealth’s Office on implementation of a new census, voter registration, and election
system. Based on feedback received, the Secretary of the Commonwealth’s Office has revised the
program and will launch a second round of testing. Implementation of the new system will further
challenge the Office during the FY2027 elections.
During FY2025, the Town Clerk was responsible for 4 elections: the State and Federal primary
election, the State and Federal Presidential election, a special Town election, and the annual Town
election. This left little time to focus on other aspects of the office. Nevertheless, some new
processes were implemented for dog licensing, and an audit and update of membership of boards,
commissions, and committees, and census data verification were started. FY2026 has one
scheduled election, which is allowing time for the Office to focus on its other functions and continue
the work started in FY2025.
The Town Clerk has been working on streamlining the processes in the Office, focusing on
Section 3 - 41

=== Page 110 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Town Clerk and Board of Registrars
mechanical and digital automation, as appropriate.
During FY2025 and FY2026, attention has been given to the maintenance of the more than 50
boards and committees. For the Town Clerk, this means ensuring membership composition and
terms are correct, and that the statutory requirements for oaths, Open Meeting Law
acknowledgements, and Conflict of Interest and Ethics training have been completed. We started
with elected boards and committees, then boards and committees created through the Town’s
Bylaws, newly created boards and committees, and are now working on the remaining existing
committees. We anticipate that the review will not be completed until the early part of FY2027.
With respect to the Conflict of Interest and Ethics training, the Town Clerk is also the administrator
for all Town employees, including the School Department. This means that the system has to be
maintained to remove any departing employee, questions have to answered from existing
employees, and new employees need to be added to the system. This also means certifying
compliance when departments, such as the School Department or the Police Department, perform
their own required statutory filings. During the remainder of FY2026, the Office will focus on
meeting with all departments and bringing all departments to full compliance with Conflict of
Interest and Ethics training requirements
The Town Clerk’s Office, was also busy at the end of FY2025 and the start of FY2026 with the State
implementation of a new Vitals Record Information System for burial permits and death
certifications. The new State system means that the Town has 3 distinct State and 1 local
electronic system for vital records, as well as vital record cards and books for each of the vital
records. The systems, while quirky at times, provide the Office with the necessary information
and means to record vital records so that we can meet State mandates and customer requests in
a timely manner. The State has stated that new Vitals Record Information System(s) will be
implemented in the future but there is no existing timeline.
The census for calendar year 2025 got started late due to the demands on the Town Clerk’s Office
of a referendum resulting in a Special Town Election in January. The time constraint did have
certain benefits. The Town Clerk’s Office reviewed the manner in which census forms were sent
to each household. In the past, each of the 15,000 census forms was manually folded, paired with
a return envelope, both of which were inserted into an outside envelope. The envelopes were then
transported to the Post Office for bulk mailing. The Town Clerk’s Office tried to utilize the Town’s
existing very old letter folding and envelope stuffing machine, which is located in the mailroom.
The Town’s old machine had not been used in a very long time. The Town Clerk’s Office purchased
training on it and obtained repair services. The machine, while very efficient, when working
properly, requires a staff person to be present during every phase of operation and requires
constant adjusting. In addition, the machine broke down a number of times for extended periods
of time while we waited for parts, which are difficult to find due to the machine’s age. Currently
the machine does not work because of a software issue that has yet to be solved. Replacing the
machine may be costly but should be considered in the next couple of years, since it could reduce
the total time required to fold letters and stuff envelopes from weeks to days. During the census
preparation period, the Town Clerk’s Office purchased a smaller letter folding machine with
operating budget funds. The machine can fold several hundreds of letters in the course of one
day, although not the volume required for the census. Also, envelope stuffing is still required.
This machine did cut down the amount of time required for census preparation, dog licenses,
flammables and gasoline licenses. The Town Clerk’s machine was also used by the Assessor’s
Office and the Retirement Board. Another office that has expressed interest is the Town
Accountant.
Section 3 - 42

=== Page 111 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Town Clerk and Board of Registrars
The Town Clerk oversees the public records requests for the Town and serves as the statutory
Records Access Officer. The Town uses an online system for public records requests, but still
receives numerous requests by email, mail, and in person. For calendar year 2025, the total
number of requests is estimated to exceed 1,000, which is similar to 2024. Each request needs
to be reviewed to ensure that the appropriate town official or officials are assigned to gathering
the documents and that responses are timely provided. While the Town has an online system, it
has numerous deficiencies, including not allowing requests to be closed after resolution that does
not result in any documents being sent. The Town Clerk’s Office is working with the Information
Technology Department to acquire a better software product.
Records Management System –The centralization of town records under the care and custody of
the Town Clerk has progressed little in the last year. We have implemented receipt of minutes
from Boards and Committees. So far, we regularly receive minutes from a dozen committees. We
had intended it to become a focus during Fiscal Year 2026 but now anticipate working on the
project in FY2027.
Preservation of old town records was also paused during the heavy election seasons in Fiscal Year
2024 and Fiscal Year 2025. Preservation of records will be a project in FY2027. The Town Clerk
will apply for an archivist grant from the State. If obtained, the grant will fund an archivist to
inventory Town Clerk records and to recommend next steps for their preservation.
Notary services are another set of services that the Town Clerk offers and that draw on the Office’s
resources. Notary requests vary from CORI checks to business certificates and documents, dual
citizenship certifications, probate documents, and construction-related affidavit. Many residents
appreciate the service as it is either difficult or expensive to obtain notary services. Banks, for
instance, only notarize certain documents, and others charge a significant fee per transaction. The
practice in the Office had been to collect voluntary donations to the Needham Community Council
for the services. While certainly laudable, the services draw upon taxpayer funded resources. The
number of notarial acts has also increased as others are reducing their services. A fee schedule
for certain transactions, including probate and real estate documents would be appropriate and
could be set at an amount less than the amounts charges by private notary service providers.
Budget Statement
The Town Clerk’s budget request reflects an increase of $118,338, or 18,9%. The portion of the
increase attributable to 2 additional elections in FY2027 compared to FY2026 is $109,050. The
portion attributable to responsibility for the mailroom is $4,053.
The Budget Statement is organized to address 1. changes in the budget due to 3 elections in
FY2027, 2. changes due to becoming responsible for the mailroom, and 3. other budget changes
in each budget category.
Salary and Wage:
The 3 elections in FY2027 versus 1 election in FY2026 results in an increase of $51,789 to the for
temporary and seasonal positions to reflect 2 additional elections in FY2027: the State Primary
and the State/Federal Election. The Town Clerk portion of the Salary and Wage Line is increasing
by $10,835 due to contractual raises.

Repairs and Maintenance:
The additional maintenance to prepare the election tabulator and the check-in poll pads accounts
for an increase of $20,050; the mailroom equipment maintenance accounts for $1,835; the balance
of the increase is $450 for maintenance costs for the Town Clerk’s Office for the time clock and
the typewriters. In FY 2027, the net costs of repairs and maintenance services reflects a reduction
of $9,550 to move the dog licensing software, the public records request tracking software and
the board and committee database licenses to Professional and Technical Services, where they
should be budgeted with other software license expenses.
Rentals and Leases:
The Golf Club lease for an additional 2 elections accounts for the increase of $1,700.
Professional and Technical Services:
Professional and Technical Services for programming the memory cards for the election tabulators
and for printing elections ballots for 2 additional elections is $19,000; the Pitney Bowes postal
machine service contract accounts for a $718 increase; and the balance of the increase of $10,550
reflects an additional $1,000 for the cost of the Laserfiche license for digitizing Town Clerk records,
and the $9,550 for the budgeting of software previously budgeted in Repairs and Maintenance
Services for dog licensing software, public record request software, and boards and committee
database licenses.

Overall, the Communications line is decreasing by $2,290. The additional 2 elections result in a
$16,600 increase for vote by mail postage (including additional mailing and a postage increase).
The net increase to Communications due to printing and postage for the elections, however, is
$3,600 reflecting a $13,000 reduction thanks to a favorable quote for vote by mail printing On
the Town Clerk side, The Town Clerk Communications line is increasing by $6,000 due to the
increase in postage of .05 for non-election mailings (approximately 22,000 items). Overall, the
Communications line is decreasing due to the budgeting of $10,500 in office supplies and
miscellaneous supplies in the correct budget lines rather than the Communications line. The
budget was further decreased by $1,000 for printing maps and bylaws. We no longer send them
out to be printing. Instead, we print them in-house and maintain a small supply and print them
as needed. We also reduced the amount budgeted for confirmation notices by $1,200 to reflect
better what we are actually sending out. A new census mailing and reminder system will be
implemented in FY2026. This may result in further adjustments in coming budgets to reflect how
census is actually handled. Finally, the Town Clerk’s Office is not sending out census forms or dog
license forms for printing. Instead, printing all census and dog licensing inhouse, reducing the
budget by $2,500. There were a number of other adjustments to budgeted amounts for posting
of warrants and by laws)
Office Supplies:
Office supplies are increasing by $5,050 to reflect the budgeting of envelopes and miscellaneous
other supplies from Communications. There is no increase to the budget for these supplies.
Instead, some lines have been reduced to reflect the actual expenditures for supplies, including a

Accomplishments and Activities
All dog licensing was transferred to a previously purchased dog licensing system, eliminating the
index card system that existed. Previously, dog owners were required to come in person or mail
a check payment to the Town Clerk with a copy of a valid rabies certificate. The online system
allows dog owners to apply for a license and pay the fee by While not completely meeting the
needs of the Office, the online dog licensing provides a complete database of dogs licenses, dog
rabies certificates, and kennel licenses. With changes in the kennel licensing laws, we are working
with the vendor to enable the Office to maintain kennel licenses in the same database. The Town
Clerk would prefer a different software platform and has been reviewing options. To date, no
software platform is able to provide all the requirements for licensing in Massachusetts. The Town
Clerk’s Office will continue to work with members of the Information Technology Department on
software options. The streamlining of the dog licensing has freed up the time of one staff member
to focus on other tasks that required attention.
As reported last year, during the last quarter of FY2024 and the first half of FY2025, the Town’s
Conflict of Interest and Ethics compliance level increased from 11% to 45%. As of the end of the
first quarter of FY2026, the Town has a 66.2% compliance status.
The Town Clerk’s Office audited the Calendar Year 2025 census returns. Any active or inactive
resident and voter who had not responded to a census prior to 2020 and had failed to vote in the
last 2 State elections was deleted from the Town’s resident and voter rolls. All returned mail from
the Post Office was followed up. A census form was sent to all addresses that appeared vacant in
the State census database and as voters were removed who had moved, census forms were sent
to the address. This is a new procedure. Prior to 2025, blank census forms were not sent to
addresses after voters moved and the Town was notified of the move. Blank census forms were

=== Page 114 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information

Department Town Clerk and Board of Registrars

=== Page 123 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Legal Services
Purpose
This budget supports legal services for the Town government and School Administration, including
securing special outside counsel as needed. Town Counsel attends all Town Meetings and public
body meetings when warranted; represents the Town before courts and administrative agencies;
and drafts and reviews contracts, bylaws, and regulations.
Budget Statement
In October 2022, the Select Board appointed Miyares-Harrington (now Harrington-Heep) as Town
Counsel. This codified an arrangement that had been operated under since February 2020. This
budget funds Town Counsel and special counsel costs for activities to include, but not limited to:
land acquisition, building projects, cable licensing, ongoing legal issues, complex licensing matters,
human resources issues, contract disputes, environmental issues, title searches, Town Meeting
article research, and zoning issues. The Town anticipates services supporting ongoing capital
projects, cable grant accounting, and license renewal, and activity related to ongoing litigation.
Budget Changes
There are no changes to the Legal Services proposed budget for FY2027.

=== Page 126 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Finance Department
Department Mission
The Finance Department by its primary functions, which include accounting, assessing, budget and
finance, collections, parking appeals, procurement, risk management, and treasury; endeavors to
provide professional and responsive services to the Town, its Boards, Committees, Departments,
and the community at large. The Finance Department’s primary goals are to ensure that the Town’s
financial assets are protected and managed, provide financial information to facilitate policy
deliberation and formulation, coordinate the development and preparation of the annual capital
improvement plan and operating budget requests, and monitor implementation and compliance.
Monitors purchasing activities for goods and services by all Town departments, to ensure
procurements meet legal requirements, whether it is an informal quote, a written quote, a sealed
bid or a response to a request for proposal. In addition, the department periodically issues
guidelines with respect to best practices and local procedures. We endeavor to provide high quality
customer service to the public and our internal customers.
On the Horizon
Many activities of the department are highly regulated and are required by statute. Other
department activities that are not required by law but have arisen from other efficiency or cost-
saving efforts, or are just in keeping with good business practices, have transferred work from
other departments to the Finance Department. The Department is responsible for the oversight of
fiscal management functions, the establishment of accounting policies and practices, and the
publication of official financial reports. With virtually no exceptions, expenditures made by any
department within the fiscal year cannot exceed the appropriation authorized by Town Meeting. In
limited cases, such as debt service where the Town is required to pay debt service regardless of
the annual appropriation, or snow and ice removal expenses, provided that the current year
appropriation is at least the same dollar amount or more than the previous year’s appropriation,
spending may not exceed what is appropriated by Town Meeting.
The Finance Department develops, updates, explains, and puts internal controls into effect. The
Deputy Town Manager/Director of Finance, in conjunction with the Assistant Finance Director,
Town Accountant, Treasurer/Collector, and others, establishes fiscal internal control policies and
procedures in accordance with state finance law. These policies require all departments to develop
and maintain an internal control plan. All departments, boards, and committees (except the School
Committee) are required to adhere to such policies and procedures.
At the Annual Town Meeting in May 2025, funding was appropriated to procure and implement a
new comprehensive financial enterprise resource planning (ERP) system. As of October 2025, the
Finance Department in conjunction with IT and the School Department, are in the process of
procuring a new financial ERP system, with the goal to start the implementation process by January
2026. This will be a multi-year process that will involve all divisions within Finance, with a focus
on Accounting and Collector/Treasurer’s Office.
Accounting: At the time of the budget submission, the Accounting office was fully staffed. Looking
ahead to FY2026/FY2027 we will be going through another software conversion that consists of
revenue, payroll, accounts payable, fixed assets and the general ledger. As we go through the
conversion, the division plans to continue to cross train employees in the department so that the
day-to-day activities are done without interruption. A primary department goal is to complete
reconciliations monthly so that any adjustments and corrections will be identified and done timely.
The Accounting staff will be attending user group conferences and webinars to gain additional
knowledge and to keep current with the new software. Due to software concerns, the Accounting
division undertook additional reconciliations to confirm data accuracy and manually entered
Section 3 - 58

=== Page 127 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Finance Department
commitments into the general ledger. The division also provided additional assistance to other
Finance department divisions which were short staffed due to vacancies.
Assessing: The Assessing department is committed to delivering the highest level of customer
service while maintaining the quality and integrity of its work. This past year, the department
experienced a transition in leadership while undergoing the state-mandated five-year revaluation
year. This presented a unique opportunity to conduct a comprehensive review of all property
parcels, resulting in an approximate 40% adjustment in land values. While this increase may have
caused an initial sticker shock, it brought property valuations within Town in line with the fair
market value.
Looking ahead, the Assessing department is aiming to continue capturing new growth and ensure
that land valuations accurately reflect the ongoing development and construction trends in
Needham. Additionally, the department is planning to cross train all department staff to ensure
everyone is well-equipped to handle various assessment functions, fostering greater efficiency and
service continuity.
Collector/Treasurer: A primary focus of the office staff has been on the ongoing issues with the
current billing and collections software. The office strives to provide excellent customer service
despite system challenges that have occurred. The Collector/Treasurer’s office recently changed
lockbox providers. Additionally, the office has streamlined its cash deposit process with various
departments in Town and is looking to improve efficiencies in this area. At the time of this
submission, the Treasurer/Collector position was vacant and the department is looking to fil this
position in the near future.
Parking Clerk: The Office of the Parking Clerk processes appeal requests of parking tickets online,
in person, and by mail. Appeal statements are reviewed and investigated promptly, and appellants
are provided with written notification of appeal results. The office also prepare and processes
handwritten tickets so that the information will be available to the collection office. Ongoing liaison
is maintained with the Treasurer’s office staff, Police Department, and the Highway Department.
Enforcement issues, signage, and meter problems are identified and resolved through inspections
and interdepartmental communications. The Town is continuing with the ongoing implementation
of a mobile parking application to allow people to pay for parking. A coin option will still be
available.
Purchasing: The Purchasing division has been updating its guidance and instructions on
purchasing to the other departments in Town. The division looks to continue to update and improve
its procurement guidance in order to help departments more efficiently purchase goods and
services. The Purchasing division has seen an increase in the complexity of software contracts,
requiring a significant amount of resources to ensure the Town’s protection.
Budget Statement
The FY2027 base budget submission is $2,607,746 which is a change of $85,421 (3.39%)
over the current budget of $2,522,325. The current budget is inclusive of $20,072 transferred by
the Town Manager from the Classification, Performance, and Settlements reserve to the Finance
Department for compensation adjustments for managers and Schedule C employees.
Although there may be some individual line-item changes in the different divisions, the changes
are reviewed below on a department wide level. Some changes are due to a reallocation of existing

Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1

funds from one object line to another, so the expense is linked to the account that best describes
its purpose. There is also an additional $157,415 requested through three DSR4 submissions for
replacement mail processing equipment, an Administrative Analyst position for the Purchasing
division to begin the process of implementing a centralized procurement office and for an additional
Field Assessor position for the Assessing division to allow for increased cyclical inspections,
respectively. Including the three DSR4 requests, the department budget submission is $242,836
over the current budget of $2,522,325 or 9.63% higher. Below is a summary of the changes.
Salary and Wages
The fiscal year 2027 base budget submission of $1,981,532 is $43,449 higher than the current
budget of $1,938,083, an increase of 2.24%, after accounting for the transfer from the
Classification, Performance, and Settlements budget line by the Town Manager to reflect the
changes to Schedule C and managers’ salary schedules. The number of benefit eligible FTE’s for
FY2027 is 18, which is the same as FY2026. The department also relies on the services of two
part-time positions which are year-round, but are not eligible for Town benefits; one is the Parking
Clerk, and the other is an administrative position which supports the Assessor’s functions of the
department.
As stated previously, there are two DSR4 requests for additional positions, one in the Purchasing
division and one in the Assessing division. If the request for the FTE in the Assessing division is
approved, this would replace the current part-time year-round position. If the requests were
approved, the permanent staff FTE would increase by two, but the part-time positions would
decrease to one. Including the DSR4 requests, the total increase for personnel is $171,447 or
8.85% over the current year.
The submission includes step and longevity increases for the eight (8) employees who are
members of the Needham Independent Town Workers’ Association (ITWA) union which are based
on the collective bargaining agreement with this group, which expires June 30, 2027.
The request also includes step and longevity increases, if applicable, for the ten (10) non-
represented positions. The wages are based on the FY2026 classification and compensation plan.
Any base salary increases approved for the non-represented positions would be funded through
the salary reserve funded by Town Meeting and transferred by written approval of the Town
Manager. Merit increases for managers, if any, for FY2027 would also be funded through the salary
reserve.
The temporary and part-time wage expense is funded at $73,002 for FY2027. This is $3 higher
than the FY2026 budget. If the DSR4 for the Assessing division is funded, the temporary and part-
time wage expense would be $37,300, as the position would decrease part-time wages and
increase base wages.
The $29,938 allocation for overtime is $1,014 higher than FY2026 which represents the
adjustments to the base wages for the different divisions in FY2027. The overtime allowances for
the Accounting, Assessing, Collector, Finance, Parking Clerk, and Treasurer offices have been
submitted at $10,500, $0, $4,000, $5,875, $6,909, and $2,654 respectively. Other salary and
wage expenses include tuition reimbursement, payment in lieu of vacation, and stipends, are
budgeted at $27,714, and increase of $3,380 (13.89%)

=== Page 129 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Finance Department
Services, Supplies, and other Expenses
The fiscal year 2027 submission of $626,214 is $41,972 higher than the current appropriation of
$584,242, 7.18% higher. The major drivers for this increase are contracted services ($40,889),
including a $17,000 increase for the annual audit and a $10,000 increase for an increase in the
new contract for parking enforcement fees, higher banking fees, and software subscriptions. The
various changes are shown on the upcoming table.
The Finance Department always seeks ways to hold down costs by regular examination of its
expenses as well as trying to anticipate future costs. There are various expenses lines that contain
decreases based on spending history, where appropriate, to help offset the increases in other lines.
There is also a decrease of $4,122 over multiple lines that is due to a transfer of those budget line
items for mail room equipment and maintenance over to the Town Clerk’s office.
Operational Capital
The Finance Department is submitting a DSR4 for operational capital. This request is for
replacement mail processing equipment in the amount of $27,192. This equipment would replace
the current postage meter and mail processing equipment which folds, inserts, and seals mail.
Other Requests for Funding (DSR4)
The Finance Department is submitting three DSR4 forms for FY2027. The first request is for
replacement mail processing equipment that folds, inserts, and seals mail (as previously
mentioned). The second request is to increase a part-time position in the Assessing division to a
full time position in order to provide the additional resources necessary to comply with the cyclical
residential inspections. The DSR4 request does not come with an increase in expenses, as the
required tools for this position are already budgeted with the existing part-time position. The third
request is for an additional position for the Purchasing division to move the Town towards
centralized purchasing. The DSR4 request comes with an increase in expenses of $4,463, however
$1,580 of the amount is for one year, and the balance ($2,883) would be ongoing. The $1,580 is
for a laptop and a new Zoom phone. More information and justification for both requests are
included on the DSR4 forms.
Other Funding Resources
Although the Finance Department must track and account for numerous revolving funds and
grants, it does not have any for its own operations. All monies generated by the department go to
the general treasury. No position in the Finance Department is funded by a revolving fund.

7. Number of adjustments/changes in property valuation after the issuance of the actual tax bills.
Measure: Number of changes (corrections) per year as a percentage of the total number of parcels.
Results:
Of the 10,799 Real and Personal property accounts billed in FY2021, the value of 21 of those
properties, or 0.19% saw changes in their value through the abatement process vs 0.21% in
FY2020. The average adjustment in tax dollars for FY2021 was $12,903 vs. $5,174 for FY2020.
Of the 10,836 Real and Personal property accounts billed in FY2022, the value of 21 of those
properties, or 0.19% saw changes in their value through the abatement process vs 0.19% in
FY2021. The average adjustment in tax dollars for FY2022 was $7,230 vs. $12,903 for FY2021.
Of the 10,827 Real and Personal property accounts billed in FY2023, the value of 16 of those
properties, or 0.15% saw changes in their value through the abatement process vs 0.19% in
FY2022. The average adjustment in tax dollars for FY2023 was $8,059 vs. $7,230 for FY2022.
Of the 10,888 Real and Personal property accounts billed in FY2024, the value of 13 of those
properties, or 0.12% saw changes in their value through the abatement process vs 0.15% in
FY2023. The average adjustment in tax dollars for FY2024 was $1,328.13 vs $8,059 for FY2023.
Of the 10,898 Real and Personal property accounts billed in FY2025, the value of 172 of those
properties, or 44.22% saw changes in their value through the abatement process vs 0.12% in
FY2024. The average adjustment in tax dollars for FY2025 was $12,640.73 vs $1,328.13 for
FY2024. This significant increase in average adjustment is due to a number of commercial property
abatements that were granted.

Budgetary Considerations Yes No
Does this request address a goal of the Select Board or other Board or Committee? X
If yes, which Board or Committee?
Has this request been submitted in the last three fiscal years and not funded? X
Are there additional costs to implement this request (except for future year operating

if the request is approved?
Does the request support activities which produce revenue for the Town? X
If the request is not approved, will current Town revenues be negatively impacted? X
Is there an increased exposure for the Town if the request is not approved? X
Is specialized training or licensing required (beyond the initial purchase)? X
Does this request address a documented health or safety issue? X
All “YES” responses above must be explained in the narrative below
Description and Explanation
The Town’s current postage meter and mail processing equipment (folding, insertion, sealing)
is need of replacement. The Town currently owns a Pitney Bowes SendPro P2000 MSF5 which
is an outdated, obsolete, and we have been informed that it is a non-compliant machine which
must be replaced with a compliant model. The postage meter unit is rented (a postage meter
cannot be owned). The USPS requires all postage meters to use the new Intelligent Mail Indicia
(IMI) technology. The older Connect+ and P-Series meters, which include the P2000, do not
meet this standard. The new equipment would be procured through a state or cooperative
purchasing group contract in accordance with MGL c. 30B. There would be annual maintenance
and support costs which are currently budgeted for the existing equipment.
The equipment is needed to process the issuance of various bills. When the equipment is down
it takes longer to prepare and send invoices, in a much more manual process. The longer it
takes to get bills out, the longer it takes to receive payment. Many bills processed at Town Hall
for direct mailing are not tax related, but rather are other receivables, which if not sent timely
could result in less revenue. If the Town didn’t have a postage meter, we would need to revert
to postage stamps which are not as efficient, may cost more, and harder to track use. If the
equipment is not operational the Town could be delayed in getting required information mailed
timely. There are correspondences that must be sent via USPS within certain time frames.

Budgetary Considerations Yes No
Does this request address a goal of the Select Board or other Board or Committee? X

if the request is approved?
Does the request support activities which produce revenue for the Town? X
If the request is not approved, will current Town revenues be negatively impacted? X
Is there an increased exposure for the Town if the request is not approved? X
Is specialized training or licensing required (beyond the initial purchase)? X
Does this request address a documented health or safety issue? X
All “YES” responses above must be explained in the narrative below
Description and Explanation
This request is for a full-time Field Assessor for the Assessing division. In the FY2027 standard
budget request (DSR2), the division has $35,702 budgeted for a part-time
Professional/Technical Support Specialist (Schedule C) employee. This request removes
$35,702 budgeted under the part-time/seasonal staff line and adds $80,867 to the regular
salaries budget, which is a net increase of $45,165.
As part of the directives issued to the Assessor’s Office by the Department of Local Services
(DLS), the Town is required to complete its cyclical inspections within the next four years.
These inspections are a critical form of data maintenance, ensuring that all residential
properties are inspected at least once every ten years. Each inspection includes a thorough
exterior review and an attempted interior inspection to verify and update property data for
assessment accuracy.
Currently, there are over 5,000 parcels requiring inspection during this cycle. The Town
currently contracts with an outside vendor for data collection services. The contracted vendor
provided a quote of approximately an additional $175,000 a year over the next four years in
order to complete these inspections.
Section 3 - 75

=== Page 144 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Fiscal
Department Finance Department 2027
Year
Title Field Assessor Priority 2
Given this significant expense, it would be more cost effective and operationally beneficial to
expand the department’s internal capacity for converting the existing part-time position in the
Assessor’s Office to a full-time role. This change would:
• Provide the necessary staffing hours to manage the cyclical inspections in-house
• Reduce or eliminate the need for third-party inspection costs
• Enable timely follow-ups, permit inspections, and other critical fieldwork
• Ensure continuous progress and oversight of the cyclical project, preventing it from
being delayed or overlooked in the future
• Maximize the efficiency and responsiveness of the Assessor’s Office as a whole
This proposed staffing enhancement aligns with our long-term goals of fiscal responsibility,
data accuracy, and operational self-sufficiency. By investing in a full-time position, we can
retain control over the inspection process while delivering better value to the Town over the
duration of the cyclical inspection mandate.
This requirement to inspect every property at least every ten years is an ongoing requirement
that the Town must continue to meet even after we complete the current directive.
This request supports the Select Board Goal #6: Responsibly Governed – “models stewardship
and sustainability of the Town’s financial, human, information, and physical assets”. This
request also supports the Board of Assessors’ goal to comply with directives issued by the
Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
The request would support the accuracy of the Town’s property assessments, which could
produce additional revenue in the form of property taxes, for the Town.
If this request is not approved, the Assessing division will need to rely on outside consultants
at a much higher cost to ensure compliance with state guidelines.
V2027

Description and Explanation
The Field Assessor is classified as a Grade I-06 and the new position would be budgeted at a
Step 7, for a salary of $80,867 a year. The existing part-time Professional/Technical Support
Specialist is currently budgeted within the Assessing budget at $35,702 a year. This DSR4
request is to budget for the additional $45,165 in order to hire a full-time Field Assessor.
While this position does require a workstation, computer, software, and Zoom phone/license,
the Town already budgets for these items for the part-time Professional/Technical Support
Specialist.

All “YES” responses above must be explained in the narrative below
Description and Explanation
This request is for an Administrative Analyst in the Procurement Division. This position would
focus on the administrative procurement functions in order to assist in the creation of a
centralized procurement office. This position would report to the Assistant Director of Finance.
The creation of a centralized procurement office would allow for centralized oversight of all
non-school purchasing, including construction projects, which previously have been
decentralized. This would allow for improved consistency and efficiency with regards to
contract language, bidding procedures, and documentation. It would also allow for improved
interdepartmental collaboration on purchasing, which could lead to better pricing. A
centralized procurement office would also promote more regular review and updating of the
Town’s standard contracts, standard bid documents, policies, procedures, and templates.
Currently, no department has a position solely dedicated to procurement and therefore, it is
often not the most efficient process. This position would be able to focus on the intricacies of
procurement and would instead rely on departments for their subject matter expertise.
Procurement is rarely at the forefront of departments’ priorities. This position would provide
more proactive guidance to departments, including evaluation of the procurement options that
would better suite the needs of the department and issuing bids in a more timely fashion. The
Section 3 - 78

=== Page 147 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Fiscal
Department Finance Department 2027
Year
Title Administrative Analyst Priority 3
addition of this position would also allow for the creation of a town-wide procurement
calendar. Many of the procurements conducted by the Town are cyclical, but currently the
departments are responsible for kicking off each new procurement process. Instead, this
position would be responsible for proactively reaching out to the departments in advance,
based off of the centralized procurement calendar.
The Administrative Analyst will have the following responsibilities:
• Perform administrative aspects of purchasing, including preparing specifications,
coordination of bidding and contracting documentation, maintaining contract
schedules, and drafting of bids and contracts
• Conduct necessary research on purchasing options to adhere to state procurement law
on behalf of Town departments
• Submit required bid advertisements
• Assist departments with pre-bid meetings and assist with drafting and issuing addenda
• Ensure compliance with MGL Chapters 30B; 30,39M; 149; 7C; and 25A
• Attend bid openings and record responses
• Assist the Assistant Director of Finance with the management of the Town’s online
procurement webpage
• Review contract spending, monitor contract progress, and close out contracts
• Conduct regular procurement training to Town staff
• Conduct trainings on Commbuys for the Town
• Serve as a resource to Town staff on procurement questions
• Assist the Assistant Director of Finance with drafting and reviewing procurement
documentation such as the procurement manual, procurement templates, and internal
procurement guidance
• Communicate externally with vendors on the status of bids, contracts, and other
procurement needs
• Maintain procurement records for the Town
• Consolidate purchasing across multiple departments
• Conduct outreach to potential vendors to diversify bidders
This position would work towards the Select Board goal of “evaluat[ing] ways to increase
minority and women-owned business participation in construction, building maintenance
projects, and other Town programs.”

=== Page 156 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Finance Committee
Purpose
The Finance Committee has up to 9 members who are appointed by the Town Moderator. The
Committee is responsible for recommending a balanced budget for the following fiscal year for
Town Meeting to consider and vote. The Finance Committee also makes recommendations to Town
Meeting on capital requests and all other issues with financial implications. The Finance Committee
oversees the Reserve Fund and is responsible for authorizing any transfers needed to other lines
in the operating budget to cover unforeseen or extraordinary expenses.
Budget Statement
The Finance Committee budget request for FY2027 is flat to last year. Salary and wages are flat
to last year. The expense line is flat to last year, and allows for 3-5 members and/or staff to
attend yearly professional conferences.
Budget Changes
There are no changes to last year.

=== Page 161 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
Department Mission
The Planning and Community Development Department includes three divisions, namely, Planning,
Conservation, and the Board of Appeals under a single Planning and Community Development
budget. The Department is tasked with protecting and advancing the quality of life in Needham
through the application of sound land use planning principles and development review standards;
facilitating informed land use decisions through community participation and a collaborative
process; protecting and enhancing safe, livable neighborhoods which reflect and preserve
Needham’s unique and historic character; supporting and encouraging healthy reinvestment in the
community to provide for a strong local economy; and promoting smart planning and a sustainable
future for generations to come.
On the Horizon
The key economic development and planning challenges facing the Planning Board and the
Planning and Community Development Division over the course of the next five years will be
securing the successful implementation of the Needham Center Plan and the Land Use and Zoning
Plan for the Needham Crossing area. Unlocking the economic potential of both Needham Center
and the Needham Crossing area remains an overarching goal of the Board and Department.
Additionally, implementing the recommendations of the Large House Review Study Committee to
ensure that new residential construction in the Single Residence B and General Residence Districts
will complement existing buildings, settings and neighborhood character and the development of
recommendations for improvements to Needham’s parking-related standards in alignment with
Low Impact Development (LID) parking practices are key priorities.
Needham Crossing Business Center Planning
The Planning and Community Development Division has focused on the recommendations outlined
in the Needham Crossing Business Center Planning study completed in the fall of 2011 and its
implementation at the Center 128 project site. Most specifically, the Planning and Community
Development Division continues to oversee the permitting and build-out which is occurring within
the New England Business Center and the Center 128 project. Plan review and permitting for
Center 128 comprising three components was conducted by the Division as follows: Center 128
West, Center 128 East, and the 2nd Avenue Residences.
The Center 128 West Development, approved by the Planning Board in April of 2013, consists of:
(a) four office/research and development buildings with a total combined square footage of
approximately 740,000 square feet; (b) two free standing parking garages (to contain a combined
3,525 parking spaces); (c) 117 surface parking spaces; (d) a hotel comprising approximately
89,740 square feet and containing 128 guest units, together with (e) associated driveways,
landscaping and other associated site improvements. A portion of the development has been
constructed, which includes the hotel, building 3 (currently occupied by TripAdvisor, Inc.), a portion
of Garage B (2,070 spaces) and 153 interim surface parking spaces. Accordingly, three (3) office
buildings (Buildings 1, 2 and 4) with an aggregate of approximately 452,000 square feet, Garage
A, a portion of Garage B, and the remaining associated landscaping, driveways and other site
improvements have yet to be developed.
In October 2020 Boston Children’s Hospital filed a special permit application with the Planning
Board seeking to amend the approval for Center 128 West. Boston Children’s Hospital is seeking
to construct Building 1 as an approximately 224,000 square foot Pediatric Medical Facility, to
complete the construction of Garage B by adding 530 parking spaces and to construct an interim
surface parking lot with 105 spaces at 37 A Street. Building 2 and Building 4 are expected to be
Section 3 - 93

=== Page 162 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
constructed later and the approved use is currently expected to remain office as set forth in the
existing special permit. The special permit amendment for this project amendment was issued by
the Planning Board in January 2021. The building permit for the project was issued in February
2023. The Certificate of Occupancy for the Pediatric Medical Facility building and Garage B
expansion of 530 parking spaces was issued in September of 2025.
The Center 128 East Development, approved by the Planning Board in November of 2015, includes
420,429 square feet of office space, 19,000 square feet of retail space, a 128-room hotel and
surface parking area. Components of the Center 128 East completed project include: the expansion
of Parking Garage B at Center 128 West to accommodate the redevelopment planned at Center
128 East, the renovation and occupancy of the building located at 77A Street to accommodate the
SharkNinja headquarters and the renovation of the building located at 189 B Street to
accommodate the new NBC Universal headquarters. Construction of the hotel and retail space
remains to be completed under the project.
In summary, Center 128 at completion (with the Boston Children’s Hospital amendment) will
include a Pediatric Medical Facility, three new office buildings and two renovated buildings totaling
1,160,400 square feet, two 128-room hotels, and 19,000 square feet of retail space. Parking for
4,100 cars distributed across 3 structured parking garages along with surface parking for 778 cars
is also planned. The combination of elements will represent a major step forward in the
implementation of the vision developed for the district. Lastly, the construction of the 2nd Avenue
Residences comprising 390 residential housing units and associated structured parking at 2nd
Avenue was completed in the spring of 2018 with full rent-up completed in the fall of 2019.
Needham Center Planning
The Planning and Community Development Division further continued with implementation of the
Needham Center Planning effort. The Planning Board has initiated a review of the land use plan
which was established for Needham Center and the Lower Chestnut Street area through the
Overlay District to determine if it is meeting its stated objective of establishing a pedestrian friendly
streetscape with multi-use development in the form of a traditional New England Village.
Interviews with property owners, tenants and developers across the noted districts to identify
regulatory barriers and opportunities for redevelopment were initiated by the Board in September
of 2025.
Large House Review Study Committee
In response to concerns expressed at the May 2024 Annual Town Meeting as to the impact new or
expanded homes are having on the character of the surrounding residential neighborhood and
specifically the action taken under Article 44 to refer the issue to the Planning Board for further
study, the Planning Board appointed the Large House Review Study Committee. The Committee
is charged with developing recommendations on how best to ensure that new residential
construction in the Single Residence B and General Residence Districts will complement existing
buildings, settings and neighborhood character. The Committee will also explore how the updating
and upgrading of structures in such neighborhoods can and should be done, while at the same
time conserving the neighborhood’s distinctive qualities as change occurs. The Planning Board is
taking this action as directed by the Town Meeting and with the support of the Select Board.
The Large House Review Study Committee will consider the impact new or expanded homes are
Section 3 - 94

=== Page 163 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
having on the character of the neighborhoods within the Single Residence B and General Residence
Districts and will develop recommendations consistent with the overall purpose for the Study
Committee as noted above. The work of the Large House Review Study Committee will include:
(1) Review of past reports, plans and maps prepared by town committees, town officials, state
agencies and consultants including the previous Large House Study Committee. (2) Seeking the
input of neighborhood residents, builders, contractors, real estate agents, property owners and
others as required. It is also expected that the Large House Review Study Committee will hold 3
citizen information meetings to elicit public comments and input. (3) Review and analyze the
current Zoning By-Law and Planning Board Regulations and consideration of amendments to each.
(4)Analyze the impact of recent planned and potential new housing constructed in the past 5 years
in the Single Residence B and General Residence Districts. (5) Review and analyze alternative
zoning dimensions, restrictions or limitations that may address neighborhood concerns. (6) Review
the current FAR definition to determine whether it is too permissive and if so, how it should be
revised including whether the floor area designed for human occupancy on the third floor or
basement level of a house should be included in the FAR calculation. (7) Prepare recommendations
to amend the Zoning By-Law or propose other regulatory strategies that will protect the
characteristics valued by residents in the Single Residence B and General Residence Districts. (8)
Generally, identify key issues and needs, analyze alternative solutions, and make
recommendations to the Planning Board, both short and long term, within the overall purpose of
the Large House Review Study Committee. (9) Prepare Fiscal Impact Analysis to accompany
recommendations of Committee. (10) Coordinate with current efforts around the Stormwater By-
Law and Tree By-Law.
To date a town-wide workshop has been held and a consultant selected to perform 3D house
modeling of the various housing massing options under review by the committee. A value analysis
to understand how potential changes in the zoning code affect the anticipated selling prices of
existing smaller homes is also underway. This includes a review of smaller homes that were sold
to a developer and torn down as well as smaller homes sold to homeowners who have remained
in the current structure. The intent is to understand the impact to prospective sellers if developers
are restricted on the proposed house size they are permitted to build and to what magnitude, if
any, that changes the current market value of the house. Additionally, a fiscal impact analysis to
understand the fiscal impact on the Town of a reduction in permitted house size on municipal
revenues within the Single Residence B zoning district is planned. It is anticipated that the
Committee’s work will conclude in late fall with a set of recommendations to the Planning Board
for zoning action at the May 2026 Annual Town Meeting.
Parking Study
In July of 2024 a grant request was submitted to the Executive Office of Energy and Environmental
Affairs to fund a study of Needham’s parking-related standards. The study seeks to review,
identify, and develop recommendations for improvements to Needham’s parking-related standards
in alignment with Low Impact Development (LID) parking practices. Off-street parking
requirements will be the focus of this effort, with a particular emphasis on the benefits and
implications of reducing parking requirements and introducing parking maximums. Shared parking
mechanisms and in-lieu fees will also be evaluated, in addition to transportation demand
management (TDM) mechanisms. Section 5.1 of Needham’s Zoning By-Law, Off-Street Parking
Requirements, will be the primary focus for this project. A decision on this grant request by the
Executive Office of Energy and Environmental Affairs was delayed for close to one year. In April of

Housing Office
The Housing Office, which is part of the Planning Division, supports the Town's efforts to promote
and maintain affordable housing opportunities. The Community Housing Specialist, who staffs the
office, works closely with the Needham Housing Authority (NHA), non-profit and for-profit
developers and service providers, and other Town departments as well as regional, state and
federal agencies. The Housing Specialist enforces affordability requirements of several subsidized
housing developments to ensure compliance with funding sources and agreements with the Town,
addresses inquiries from the public related to housing issues, monitors funding opportunities and
ensures the Town’s compliance with housing grants, collects and analyzes demographic and
housing-related data, and is involved in affordable housing planning initiatives.
Another important activity involves providing staff support to the Needham Affordable Housing
Trust (NAHT), which serves as the Town’s entity for overseeing housing policies and programs.
NAHT manages a dedicated fund in support of affordable housing programs and projects and
receives fees from developers and residential management firms for monitoring performed by the
Housing Specialist, including those related to resales, refinancing, and capital improvements
associated with affordable homeownership units.
A major NAHT responsibility is the Small Repair Grant Program (SRGP), administered by the
Housing Office on behalf of the Housing Trust. The SRGP provides financial assistance to income-
eligible Needham homeowners to make repairs and alterations to their homes for health and safety
reasons. The program was modified during fiscal years 2025 and 26 to make it more user friendly
and to increase the maximum grant available per household from $5,000 to $7,000. An eligible
household must include at least one member who is 60 years or older or has a disability and have
a combined income at or below 80% of Area Median Income (AMI—established annually by the
federal Department of Housing and Urban Development—HUD). Eligible work items include minor
plumbing or electrical work; light carpentry, window or door repairs or replacements; railings,
broken or clogged gutters or downspouts repair; step or porch improvements; and locks,
smoke/CO2 detectors, weather stripping, bathroom grab bars, raised toilets and hand-held shower
heads improvements.
The Community Housing Specialist represents the Town in the WestMetro HOME Consortium, a
group of 13 municipalities that receives federal HOME Investment Partnerships Program (HOME)
funds from HUD. Administered by the City of Newton on behalf of the member communities, the
HOME Consortium provides limited administrative funds to the Town to compensate for the
Community Housing Specialist’s work on HOME-related activities including preparing HUD-
mandated plans and documenting the Town’s efforts to advance affordable housing. Membership
in the Consortium also provides a mechanism for specified Needham entities to apply for HOME
funds to maintain, improve and create affordable housing. The Consortium awarded the Needham
Housing Authority a $182,000 HOME grant during FY26 to support the preservation and
modernization of Seabeds Way and Captain Cook Drive.
The Community Housing Specialist and Director of Planning and Community Development will be
providing staff support to the Stephen Palmer Development Review Committee, which is

Conservation Division
The Conservation Division has identified the following primary goals for FY2026: (1) Fulfilling
ongoing obligations for technical support of the Conservation Commission through processing of
permit applications under the MA Wetlands Protection Act and the Town of Needham Wetlands
Protection Bylaw; (2) The Conservation Department has hired a new Conservation Specialist who
is also acting as the Trails Coordinator; (3) The Conservation Department and Commission have
taken over the management and the implementation of the Comprehensive Trails Master Plan, and
will be overseeing the Needham Trails updates and initiatives with collaboration from other Town
Boards and Commissions that oversee properties that include trails; (4) The Conservation
Department continues to solicit volunteers for the Trail Steward Program and also for special
projects; getting the Trail Steward Program active including soliciting volunteers; (5) The
Conservation Commission and staff received funding from the Community Preservation Committee
(CPC) for implementation of the signage initiative to improve consistency and quality of signage
at conservation lands across town; (6) The Trail Signage Initiative Phase 1 is underway and sign
posts have been installed at the Towns two largest open space parcels, Ridge Hill and the Town
Forest. The next Phase is to finalize the Trailhead signage designs and have the signage produced
and organize volunteers to install the signage; (7) Once the signage is installed, the focus will
move to either re-furbishing or constructing new kiosks as well as bird boxes for various properties;
(8) The Conservation Commission and staff will continue assisting various Town departments in
assessing compliance with the Town’s NPDES permit and other initiatives; (9) As part of the NPDES
permit public education compliance piece, the Conservation staff will continue to schedule
presentations for the public with our interactive Stormwater Model; (10) Conservation staff will
continue to promote the use of pollinator seed mixes as opposed to lawn seed to the public and
on various Town projects where applicable; (11) The Conservation Commission and staff will
organize themed trail walks with the public and Conservation Commissioners on Conservation
lands; and (12) The Conservation Commission and staff will continue to facilitate presentations to
the public on interesting, timely topics.

FY 2025 Budget Return
The Planning and Community Development Department had a 15% overall turnback rate for FY
2025 with a salary and wages turnback rate of 11.3% and an expense turnback rate of 68.7%.
As relates the salary and wage turnback, this return of funds was attributed to a position vacancy
in the Conservation Division. In that Division the position of Conservation Specialist remained
unfilled due to staff turnover for a period of 9 months. As relates to the expense turnback, this
return of funds was in large part attributed to a reduction in the Professional and Technical
Services line item. Funds anticipated to fund new Planning Studies to support the Planning
Department’s planning and zoning initiatives were delayed as staff time remained focused on
securing Town Compliance with the requirements of the MBTA Communities Act.

Planning and Community Development Division
The Planning Board is charged with broad statutory responsibilities to guide the physical growth
and development of Needham in a coordinated and comprehensive manner. Specifically, the
Section 3 - 100

=== Page 169 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
Planning Board is legally mandated to carry out certain provisions of the Subdivision Control Law
(M.G.L., Chapter 41, Section 81-K to 81-GG) and of the Zoning Act (M.G.L., Chapter 40A). These
legal responsibilities are reflected locally in the Subdivision Rules and Regulations and Procedural
Rules of the Planning Board, and in the Town’s Zoning By-Law. The specific services that the
Planning Board provides are as follows:
Review and Approval/Disapproval of:
• Approval-Not-Required (ANR) Plan
• Preliminary Subdivision Plans
• Definitive Site Plans for certain larger developments (major projects)*
• Subdivision Plans*, including ongoing administration
• Residential Compounds (RC's)*
• Scenic Road Applications
• Outdoor Restaurant Seating Applications
* This includes Special Permit Decisions, with legal notices, public hearings, and written decisions.
• Site Plans for certain smaller developments (minor projects)
• Applications to the Board of Appeals for variances and special permits
• Petitions for acceptance/discontinuance of public ways
Initiation, Development, and Presentation of Proposed Zoning Amendments to Town Meeting
Reprinting of Town Zoning By-Laws and Zoning Map.
Preparation and Maintenance of a Master Plan and related planning studies to guide future physical
growth and development in Needham (including studies referred to the Board by Town Meeting).
Revisions to “Subdivision Regulations and Procedural Rules of the Planning Board” and printing of
the same.
Provision of Information on Planning, Zoning and Development matters to the public (including
residents, developers, and other government agencies).
In its capacity as a special permit granting authority, the Planning Board in FY2025 processed nine
(9) applications as “Major Projects” under the Site Plan Review By-Law. In addition, the Board
reviewed and approved one (1) subdivision plan, and five (5) plans were endorsed
“Approval-Not-Required (ANR)” under the Subdivision Control Law, meaning that the lots created
or altered on such plan’s met minimum frontage requirements.
The Board of Appeals referred nineteen (19) applications for variances, special permits,
comprehensive permits, and administrative appeals to the Planning Board last year, and as
required by the Zoning By-Law, the Board reviewed each application and submitted its
recommendations in writing to the Board of Appeals.
Fiscal Year 2025 Achievements
In January 2021, Governor Baker signed Chapter 358 of the Acts of 2020 into law, which requires
each MBTA community to have “a zoning ordinance or by-law that provides for at least 1 district
of reasonable size in which multi-family housing is permitted as of right.” The statute requires
that: (1) the housing cannot have age-restrictions and must be suitable for families with children;
(2) the zoning must allow for at least 15 units per acre; (3) the district must be within 0.5 miles
from a commuter rail station, subway station, ferry terminal or bus station; and (4) non-compliant
communities will be ineligible to receive state funds from the Housing Choice Initiative, the Local
Capital Projects Fund, and the MassWorks infrastructure program. This legislation is part of a
broader effort to encourage transit-oriented development and to address the state’s housing
shortage, in terms of both the number of units and types of housing stock available.
Section 3 - 101

=== Page 170 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
In August 2023 the Executive Office of Housing and Livable Communities (EOHLC) released a set
of guidelines for how communities can comply with this new multi-family zoning requirement. The
EOHLC guidelines classify Needham as a commuter rail community which requires that the Town
implement zoning to permit a housing unit capacity equivalent to 15% of the Town’s total number
of existing housing units. As the Town’s 2020 Census count was 11,891 housing units, Needham’s
minimum unit capacity of 15% is 1,784 units.
In response to the MBTA Communities Law, a compliant zoning option was presented to and
adopted by the May 2025 Annual Town Meeting. The adopted scenario is referred to as the “Base
Compliance Plan”. The Base Compliance Plan (presented under Articles 18 and 19) adheres very
closely to the underlying zoning district boundaries and dimensional standards found in Needham’s
current Zoning By-Law. This zoning scenario is intended to meet the minimum compliance
requirements of the MBTA Communities Law and proposes zoning that would allow for a total of
1,870 units. On June 13, 2025, Needham submitted its district compliance application for the Town
of Needham’s Multi-family Overlay District to the EOHLC seeking approval of the Base Compliance
Plan adopted at the May 2025 Annual Town Meeting. EOHLC has now reviewed the district
compliance application for the Town of Needham’s Multi-family Overlay District and on September
2, 2025, determined that Needham was compliant with the MBTA Communities Act (Section 3A
and 760 CMR 72.00). This determination of compliance by EOHLC now qualifies Needham for
eligibility under the Housing Choice Initiative, the Local Capital Projects Fund, and the MassWorks
infrastructure program.
Fiscal Year 2025 Zoning Initiatives
In Fiscal Year 2025 two zoning articles responsive to the MBTA Communities Law were submitted
to and approved by the Town Meeting as detailed above. In Fiscal Year 2025 two zoning articles
responsive to the National Flood Insurance Program’s minimum requirements for flood plain
management were prepared and presented to the May 2025 Special Town Meeting as noted below.
Both flood plain district zoning articles were adopted as detailed below.
Article 5: Amend Zoning By-Law, Flood Plain District. This article amends the flood plain district to
reference the new Norfolk County Flood Insurance Rate Maps which become effective July 8, 2025.
Additionally, the article updates Needham’s flood plain zoning regulations to assure compliance
with mandatory National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) requirements. For residents of a
community to be eligible to receive flood insurance, the community must agree to abide by the
National Flood Insurance Program’s minimum requirements for flood plain management. These
modifications have been prepared to ensure compliance with the requirements of that program.
Failure to adopt these revisions will result in Needham’s suspension from the National Flood
Insurance Program.
Article 6: Amend Zoning By-Law, Map Change to Flood Plain District This article describes the
geographical boundary of the Town’s flood plain zoning district as it will be reflected on a revised
zoning map. Said area comprises the geographical area that the Federal Emergency Management
Agency (FEMA) has defined as having a 1% chance of flooding in a given year. The exact
boundaries of the district are defined by the 100-year base flood elevations shown on the Norfolk
County Flood Insurance Rate Map (FIRM) and further defined by the Norfolk County Flood
Insurance Study (FIS) report dated July 8, 2025.
Following adoption of the above-named Flood Plain zoning articles, a certification of the Town

Performance Factors
During the previous fiscal year, the Planning Department had committed to tracking the turnaround
time required on its Major Project Site Plan Special Permits and Subdivision applications.
Monitoring of the timeline between application filing and the scheduling of the public hearing,
issuance of the Decision following the close of the public hearing and filing of the written decision
with the Town Clerk following Board action were tracked. The goal was to schedule the public
hearing within 5 weeks of application receipt, to issue the Special Permit Decision or Subdivision
Decision within two weeks of the close of the public hearing, and to file the written decision within
5 business days of permit issuance by the Board. The articulated goals were met in one of the
three studied criteria in FY2025. During the affected timeline 9 new Special Permit applications
and 1 Subdivision application were processed. Public hearings were held on average within 29
days of application receipt, decisions were issued within 20 days of the close of the public hearing,
with written decisions filed with the Town Clerk within 9 days of permit issuance. The total average
time required to process an application was 69 days with a minimum of 38 days and a maximum
of 73 days. The Department plans to continue to track these items and will submit a similar report
with its FY2028 budget request.
Conservation Division
The Needham Conservation Division is primarily tasked with providing dedicated professional,
administrative, and technical assistance to the Town and Conservation Commission in accordance
with the MA Wetlands Protection Act (M.G.L. Chapter 131 Section 40) and the Needham Wetlands
Protection Bylaw (Article 6), and their respective regulations, guidelines, and policies.
Additionally, the Division provides support in office administration and organization, public
assistance, grant administration, committee and community support, and land conservation and
management, including oversight of the Town Trails Network.
The Needham Conservation Commission is comprised of seven volunteer members appointed by
the Select Board to staggered three-year terms. The Commission’s primary responsibility involves
ensuring compliance with the Massachusetts Wetlands Protection Act (M.G.L. Chapter 131 Section
40) and the local Wetlands Protection Bylaw (Needham General Bylaws Article 6). As the
administrative and technical support agency to the Commission, the Conservation Division
undertakes the following tasks pertaining to wetland permitting:
• Reviews Notice of Intent applications and other filings for completeness and compliance
with wetland statutes and associated regulations.
• Assures legal requirements are met including postings, hearing and permit timeframes,
minutes, and other records.
• Schedules and attends twice monthly meetings of the Conservation Commission and
transcribes meeting minutes.
• Maintains case files and materials including computer databases.
• Arranges and conducts on-site inspections related to filings.
• Reviews wetland delineations.
• Drafts permits with associated conditions and other documents.
• Monitors construction to ensure compliance with permits.
Section 3 - 103

=== Page 172 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
• Reviews building permits for projects occurring within the Commission’s jurisdiction to
reduce the amount of Enforcement Orders issued and provide better public outreach.
• Responds to complaints and investigates potential violations.
• Keeps regular office hours to provide assistance to the regulated community.
• Collaborates with other Town Departments, Boards and Committees on matters pertaining
to permitting municipal projects and activities.
In addition to wetland permitting, the Conservation Division undertakes broader environmental
and land management functions including the following:
• Oversees/carries out management tasks such as trail building and maintenance, signage,
and cleanup.
• The Conservation Specialist/Trails Coordinator organizes and manages the Trail Steward
Program, as well as reports to the Conservation Commission on Trail initiatives.
• Participates in various committees, including the Stormwater Bylaw Working Group.
• Assures compliance with rules and regulations for conservation lands; issues needed
permits; and addresses user problems.
• Assists the Commission in planning, acquisition, administration, and management of
municipal conservation land.
• Assists in preparation of Open Space and Recreation Plans to meet criteria for approval.
• Researches and conducts on-site evaluations for parcels under consideration for acquisition,
donation, conservation, or agricultural preservation restrictions.
• Researches, pursues, and coordinates grant and other funding opportunities; writes
proposals and manages grants.
• Collaborates with other Town Departments, Boards and Committees on matters pertaining
to the use, management and protection of the Town’s natural resources and open space.
• Educates the public about the importance of protecting wetlands and other open space.
Under both the state and local statutes, the Conservation Commission has a maximum of 21 days
from receipt of a completed application to open a public hearing on that application. A hearing
may be continued with the consent of the Applicant to allow for the submittal of additional
information. After closing the public hearing, the Commission has 21 days to issue an Order of
Conditions. In FY2025, all Orders of Conditions were completed within the noted timeline with no
requests for additional time requested.
The Conservation Division makes every effort to assist Applicants with the filing process. The
Division’s staff meets frequently with property owners and potential applicants to explain the filing
process or to provide input on which application would be most appropriate (for a particular site
or project). The Conservation Division conducts well over one hundred site visits each year to
review projects associated with both the MA Wetlands Protection Act and the Needham Wetlands
Protection Bylaw.
The collective knowledge and experience of the Conservation Division serves to benefit the
regulated community, including the Town of Needham as it provides professional expertise in a
growing and more restrictive environmental regulatory time period.
