Zoning Board of Appeals Division
The Zoning Board of Appeals is a quasi-judicial body that hears and renders decisions on: 1)
Appeals from Building Inspector administrative decisions; 2) Applications for Special Permits or
Variances under M.G.L. Chapter 40A and the Needham Zoning By-Laws; and 3) Requests for
Comprehensive Permits under M.G.L. Chapter 40B (affordable housing) as well as hearing
amendments and de minimis change requests. The Board of Appeals consists of three regular
members and two associate members appointed by the Select Board as authorized and established
by General Laws, Chapter 40A, the Home Rule Charter Act and Article VIII of the General By-Laws.
The Division provides guidance to applicants on all zoning matters, including assistance with the
application and hearing process; reviews and analyzes applications for completeness and
compliance to established zoning guidelines; coordinates the interdepartmental administrative
review process, conducts site reviews; organizes the production of Special Permits and Board
determinations post-public hearings; provides professional staffing to Board members, provides
management and coordination of Comprehensive Permits projects, designs procedures for any new
zoning review processes, produces technical reports, conducts research, and maintains and
manages all Board data bases, communications and legal documents. Virtually all matters that
come before the Board are initiated by residents or businesses seeking relief from the Zoning By-
Law. Each application is processed in accordance with the legal requirements and timetables
established under the Massachusetts Zoning Act, the Town of Needham Zoning By-Law, and Zoning
Board of Appeals Rules and Regulations.
Performance Factors
The Board holds monthly in-person meetings with livestream broadcast on Zoom and on The
Needham Channel.
In FY2025, the Board held thirty-six (36) public hearings and one (1) informal matter (refer to
Table 4). The informal matters consisted of permit renewals, document reviews, project updates,
plan substitutions or corrections. The Board of Appeals received fourteen (14) applications relating
to Special Permits, three (3) Variances, one (1) Comprehensive Permit and two (2) Appeal of a
Building Inspector Decision. Four (4) were withdrawn (refer to Table 7). Ten (10) applications
were for residential use; seven (7) were for commercial use; and two (2) for education use.
The Board issued fifteen (15) Decisions in FY2025.
The Division continues to coordinate the conditions associated with the 40B Comprehensive

Permits issued by the ZBA –
• 339 Chestnut Street, a development of 6 rental units housing complex, consisting of one

consisting of an 86 rental unit development comprised of studios, 1 bedroom and 2-

Design Review Board
The Design Review Board (DRB) consists of five regular members and two alternate
members. Three regular members are appointed by the Planning Board where one such regular
member is either qualified in the art or design profession, and the second regular member is either
qualified in fine arts or landscape design. Two regular members and two alternate members are
appointed by the Select Board where one such regular member is either qualified in the art or
design profession, and the second regular member has a retail business in the town. The Board
is required to meet monthly and generally meets two times a month using the zoom format. In
FY2025 the Select Board appointed a new Design Review Board member who is a landscape
architect. At this point the Board consists of six members, with one vacancy remaining.
The Design Review Board serves the community by reviewing the design of all new commercial
structures and outdoor uses, exterior additions, exterior alterations, and exterior changes in all
areas as specified in section 7.7.2.2 of the Zoning By-Laws. They review requests for all sign
permits, as required under Article XIX of the Needham General By-Laws. Applications that are
submitted to the Board are generally initiated by businesses, developers and sign companies.
Projects under review by the Planning Board such as major site plan projects, and minor site
plan projects are also reviewed by the Design Review Board. They provide comments and
recommendations for consideration by the Planning Board in their decisions. The DRB reviews
applications for retaining walls, for example, under review by the Zoning Board of Appeals and
provides comments and recommendations. Appeals to the Sign-By-Law and special permits as
allowed in the General By-Laws are reviewed with a public hearing before the Design Review Board.
Section 3 - 107

=== Page 176 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Planning and Community Development
Performance Factors
In FY2025, the DRB met formally a total of fourteen (14) times and held a total of six (6) public
hearings for special permits (refer to Table 5). The Board conducted site plan reviews for five (5)
projects that were being heard by the Planning Board or Zoning Board of Appeals (refer to Table
8). The DRB provided their comments and recommendations to the specific Board for
consideration. The site plan reviews that were presented and discussed at the DRB meetings
included: 1) Dedham Savings Bank renovating a bank building for their new location at 695
Highland Avenue, 2) renovations to the office building at 63 Kendrick Street, 3) 339 Chestnut
Street Comprehensive Permit for a six unit residential project, 4) an additional building constructed
at 1407 Central Avenue, the Jack Cogswell Building for a vehicle maintenance garage, and 5)
Wingate Development revisions to their previous site plan for recreational amenities and EV
parking spaces.
A total of thirty-four (34) sign applications were reviewed during the FY 2025 period, and seven
(7) exterior alterations were submitted for approval. Several new businesses to Needham sought
signage/awnings/etc. and several existing businesses relocated within Needham, refreshed their
exterior appearances, changed their business name and/or redeveloped and expanded their
footprint. In addition, other various applications were reviewed. Like past years, a public art
proposal was submitted and approved by the Design Review Board. The Needham Council for Arts
and Culture proposed a painted mural in the walkway at 91 Chapel Street. The mural, entitled
“Tight Knit,” pays homage to the women of the 1800s who worked in the knitting mills in Needham.
The women contributed to the growing economy and identity of Needham as a textile mill town.

Description and Explanation
The Department of Planning and Community Development seeks the approval of the Town Manager
to hire a full-time Planner. The Planner would provide a variety of technical and professional work
in the current and long-range planning of the Town. Working under the general supervision of the
Director of Planning and Community Development and Assistant Town Planner the essential
functions of the position would be as follows:
1. Review applications and development plans submitted to the Planning Board for compliance
with Zoning Bylaw and Development Regulations and evaluate site design and landscape
components. Conduct research and site assessments and refer to other Town departments for
review. Preparation of background and analysis of plans and submittal of recommendations to
the Assistant Planner for review.
2. Prepare drafts of recommendations or decisions for action by the Planning Board and prepare
reports or exhibits to support Board recommendations. With Assistant Town Planner
supervision, oversee subdivision and special permit administration and prepare drafts of the
Certificate of Action and the Special Permit. Monitor construction to ensure developer’s
compliance and enforce remedial action if needed.
Section 3 - 113

=== Page 182 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Planning and Community Fiscal
Department 2027
Development Year
Title Planner Priority 1
3. Conduct research into relevant legal issues, gather and analyze data, and prepare studies and
policy actions related to current Town planning issues, Planning Board policies, regulations and
by-laws, and general planning.
4. Attend occasional Planning Board meetings to report on planning issues and to present research
findings through oral and/or visual presentations. Provide technical information and support
to the Board, perform follow-up action, and conduct additional research as directed.
5. Work with administrative support people to help organize and maintain all Planning Board
records. Assemble materials and documents as required for recording in Registry of Deeds or
Land Court.
6. Provide technical support to the Director of Planning and Community Development and assist
the Assistant Town Planner in various planning functions.
7. Under the direction of the Director of Planning and Community Development, respond to public
inquiries on zoning, subdivision, and planning activities of the Planning Board via phone and
electronic mail. Educate the public through correspondence and through presentations at
meetings.
8. Communicate with consultants under contract with the Town and make occasional site visits to
verify compliance with permit conditions.
9. Attend various other departments, committees, and board meetings as assigned to provide
technical and staff support. Review and provide feedback on documents and reports.
10. Perform special projects and other related duties as required, directed, or as the situation
dictates.
Minimum education and experience required for the position: Bachelor’s degree in urban planning,
or related field, from an accredited college or university and two to five years of increasingly
responsible, planning-related experience. Master’s Degree preferred.
Required position qualifications to include knowledge of the: (1) Principles and practices of urban
planning and development; (2) Site planning techniques and methods; (3) Federal, State, and
local laws, codes and regulations related to planning, zoning, and land divisions; (4) Geographic
Information Systems; and (5) Current literature, information sources, and research techniques in
the field of urban planning.
Required skills to include ability to: (1) Interpret and explain planning and zoning activities to the
general public; (2) Read and interpret comprehensive building and land development plans; (3)
Analyze and compile technical and statistical information and write technical reports; (3) Operate
computer and various software necessary for performing assigned planning duties; and (4)
Communicate clearly and concisely, both orally and in writing, and maintain effective working
relationships.
Funding the position will enable the Department to better meet the permit-granting and land-

Planning studies and zoning initiatives identified by the Planning Board include the following items:
(1) Review and analysis of the Town’s Zoning By-Law parking standards; (2) Review and analysis
of the goals of the 2009 Needham Center Plan and the steps completed to date to meet those
goals to determine if adjustments to the zoning for Needham Center and Chestnut Street are
warranted; (3) Review of the land use and policy goals of the Business District located along
Highland Avenue between May and Rosemary Street; (4) Review of the land use and policy goals
of the Town’s Industrial Districts, particularly the Industrial District on Hillside Avenue; (5)
Development of a Town-wide Inclusionary Zoning By-law provision; and (6) Development of
Design Guidelines for the subdistricts of the Mult-family Overlay District as adopted under the
MBTA Communities Act.
Finally, should the Planning and Community Development Department be successful in this effort,
the costs associated with this additional staff position would be added to the Department’s FY 2027
salary account. It is anticipated that $88,000.00 additional dollars would be required to fund the
new position during the first year. Additional costs would include a projected annual benefit cost
of $31,988 and a one-time computer purchase and phone connection cost of $1,580.

Description and Explanation
Description and Explanation
The Department is seeking funding for a new part-time, non-benefitted position of
Administrative/Office Support Specialist to serve the Department. The position is requested to be
funded on an hourly basis at a rate of 10 hours per week of staff time. Presently, the Department
has a part-time Administrative Specialist who provides 27.5 hours of administrative support to the
Department. With this change the Department would be provided with administrative support on
a full-time basis. It is anticipated that $14,500 additional dollars would be required to fund the
new position during the first year. Additional costs would include a projected annual benefit cost
of $1,337 and a one-time computer purchase and phone extension cost of $1,580.

Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Planning Consulting Assistance
Planning and
Fiscal Year 2027 Department Community
Development
Funding Amount $125,000.00 Funding Source Tax Levy
Article Information
The Planning and Community Development Department is requesting an appropriation of
$125,000.00 for Planning Consulting Assistance. These funds would provide support to the
Planning and Community Development Department, enabling the Director to engage professional
services from time to time to support two of its functional areas:
(1) the regulatory functions of the Planning and Community Development Department, which
oversees permit granting for the Planning Board, the Zoning Board of Appeals, and the
Conservation Commission; and
(2) the land use and town planning initiatives of the Planning Department.
Funding for this activity was previously approved in FY2015 in the amount of $45,000.00, in
FY2022 in the amount of $60,000.00, and in FY 2025 in the amount of $80,000. Examples of
projects completed under the FY2015 appropriation include a build-out and traffic analysis for the
Gould Street Industrial 1 and Reservoir Street Industrial Districts; 3D modeling and presentation
drawings for the proposed Highway Commercial 1 zoning district at the Muzi Ford and Channel 5
site; and a fiscal and traffic impact analysis for the proposed Highway Commercial 1 zoning district
at the Muzi Ford and Channel 5 site. Funding under the FY2022 appropriation was dedicated solely
to the planning work required to assist the Town in developing compliant zoning under the MBTA
Communities Act. This work included the development of 2 compliant zoning scenarios, a build-
out analysis, a fiscal impact analysis, 3-D modeling, and a traffic study. Funding under the FY 2025
appropriation is currently underway and is being used to support the work of the Large House
Review Study Committee. This work has included the use of 3D modeling to assist the Committee
in preparing 3D eye-level views of three Committee selected sample house construction drawing
sets where such house construction drawing sets are further modeled reflective of Committee
identified parameters. Additionally, a value analysis/fiscal impact study has been procured to
attempt to quantify the impact of a more restrictive FAR zoning bylaw on a home’s value for a
developer/builder seeking to maximize profit on a larger new home and on future Town tax revenue
growth. Finally, the funds have been allocated as a partial match to an Executive Office of Energy
and Environmental Affairs grant which currently funds a study of Needham’s parking-related
standards.
The need for such professional services in both function areas is noted below.
Regulatory Functions:
The permit-granting and land-development plan review function of the boards that the Planning
and Community Development Department supports has called for contracted professional services
over the last decade, and this is expected to continue over the next few years. The Director
anticipates that professional services in such areas as traffic/transportation engineering and fiscal
impact analysis to complement the expertise of Town staff would be procured. Having access to
professional expertise across multiple land use disciplines in a complex regulatory environment
has proven essential to allowing the Department to effectively address the permitting issues
Section 3 - 124

=== Page 193 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Planning Consulting Assistance
coming before it. In addition, the funds would be used to help the Department research and advise
other appropriate regulatory Boards when presented with complex development projects.
Land Use and Planning Initiatives:
The use of contracted services is also anticipated to support the Planning Department’s planning
and zoning initiatives, and in some cases this funding would supplement funding from other
sources. Several planning projects which the Planning Board is considering are as follows.
• The Planning Board wants to review the goals of the 2009 Needham Center Plan and the
steps completed to date to meet those goals to determine if adjustments to the zoning for
Needham Center and Chestnut Street are warranted. The Board anticipates that this effort
will include a workshop to present accomplishments to date and to identify any constraints
to redevelopment not anticipated in the 2009 Needham Center Plan. The 2009 Plan detailed
the Village Concept that called for “diverse, mixed-use districts combining residential,
commercial and civic uses in a compact area” and proposed new zoning regulations to
“encourage massing that helps define the street edge and that serves as a backdrop to the
streetscape.” Some projects carrying out this vision have occurred over the past decade -
the mixed-use building at 50 Dedham Avenue, the Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital’s new
facilities, the new Needham Public Safety building, and a new mixed-use building at 15-17
Oak Street, but most of the under-developed areas identified in the Plan have yet to fulfill
their full potential. The purpose of this review would be to examine current impediments
to redevelopment and to make the warranted adjustments. Suggestions from the Needham
2025 plan will be examined along with other identified constraints. The Board anticipates
that this planning effort will provide a framework for informing adjustments to both the
zoning and implementation plan for Needham Center and the Chestnut Street corridor
moving forward.
• The Board wants to review the land use and policy goals of the Business District located
along Highland Avenue between May and Rosemary Street. The land use and dimensional
regulations for this district have not been updated in over 50 years. Prior to 1989, all the
Town’s commercial areas were zoned under a single “Business District” designation.
Recognizing that each commercial area had unique attributes and land use objectives,
beginning in 1989 the Town began the process of studying each area to establish a more
tailored regulatory framework for the studied area consistent with the Town’s land use
objectives. This subsequently led to the creation of the Needham Center Business District,
Chestnut Street Business District, Avery Square Business District, Commercial 128 Business
District, the Hillside Avenue Business District, and the Neighborhood Business District. The
Business District located along Highland Avenue between May and Rosemary Street is the
only remaining Business District on which the land use and regulatory profile have not yet
been updated.
• The Board also wants to review the land use and policy goals of the Town’s Industrial
Districts, particularly the Industrial District on Hillside Avenue. Additionally, a review of the
land use and policy goals of the Town’s Mixed-Use 128 District at Reservoir Street is
planned.
Section 3 - 125

=== Page 194 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Planning Consulting Assistance
• An important goal for this coming year is to review and consider amending the Inclusionary
Zoning provisions of our Zoning By-Law. The Town has incorporated inclusionary zoning
mandates into its Zoning By-Law for several Overlay Districts, including the independent
living units in the Elder Services Zoning District, as well as zoning for the Needham Center,
Lower Chestnut Street, and Garden Street Overlay Districts. In these areas at least 10%
of the units must be affordable to those earning at or below 80% of area median income
and meeting all other state requirements for inclusion in the Subsidized Housing Inventory.
More recent zoning as part of the Mixed-Use Overlay District, in the Highland Avenue/128
area, as well as changes to the Neighborhood Business District increased the affordability
requirement to 12.5% with the option of a payment in-lieu of unit’s provision in the case of
the Neighborhood Business zoning. New zoning for the MBTA Overlay District also included
a 12.5% affordability requirement. More than one-third of the municipalities in the state
have such inclusionary zoning in place with percentage of affordable units in multi-family
rental or condominium developments required at a range of between 10% and 15% or even
up to 20% of the units. The level of income to be reached is also a factor to be considered.
Not having such inclusionary zoning in place for all our zoning districts at a consistent
standard is causing the Town to miss opportunities for new affordable units.
• Another goal for the coming year is to develop Design Guidelines for the recently adopted
MBTA Overlay Districts.
In summation, the above noted items provide an overview of potential areas of planning initiatives
in which professional services might be required.

Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Small Repair Grant Program
Planning and
Fiscal Year 2027 Department Community
Development
Funding Amount $50,000 Funding Source Tax Levy
Article Information
The Small Repair Grant Program provides financial assistance to senior and disabled low-and
moderate-income Needham homeowners to make repairs and alterations to their homes for health
and safety reasons. Up to $7,000 in grant funding is currently available per eligible household.
Applicants must be 60 years or older or have a disability with incomes at or below 80% of Area
Median Income (established annually by the federal Department of Housing and Urban
Development—HUD). Subject to funding availability, grants are awarded on a first-come, first-
served basis with no submission deadlines to eligible property owners proposing health and safety
improvements. This request seeks to provide funding for the program in the 2027 fiscal year to
maintain this successful program. Program funding in the amount of $50,000 was previously
appropriated in the 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025 fiscal years.
Eligible work items include minor plumbing or electrical work, light carpentry, doorbell switches,
window or door repairs or replacements, railing repairs, broken or clogged gutters or downspouts,
step or porch improvements, work on locks, smoke/CO2 detectors, weather stripping, bathroom
grab board, raised toilets, hand-held shower heads, among others.
The Small Repair Grant Program responds to findings and recommendations in the Council on
Aging and Needham Public Health Division’s “Assessment of Housing and Transit Options for
Needham Seniors” which indicated that one of the major barriers to aging in place involves the
costliness of modifying existing homes to increase accessibility. Since the start of the Small Repair
Grant Program, the Town has awarded 54 grants, most typically for window replacement, electrical
upgrades, stair and railing replacements, and plumbing improvements. Recent modifications to
the program have made the entire process more user-friendly, provided access to grants on a
rolling basis, increased the amount of grant funds available per eligible household, and improved
financial oversight.

Department Information
DSR1
Department Police
Department Mission
The Needham Police Department is committed to excellence in everything we do. We pledge to
maintain a close working relationship with the community to protect life and property and maintain
a safe and peaceful environment for all. We will perform our sworn duties ethically, maintaining
respect for individual rights, human dignity, cultural diversity, and community values.
On the Horizon
On February 12, 2025, The Needham Police Department was awarded full accreditation from the
Massachusetts Police Accreditation Program. It should be noted that the Department must be re-
accredited every three years to maintain its accreditation status.
We have made progress to bring our staffing levels to full complement. We are at 46 Officers with
one recruit graduating in November and 4 more attending police recruit academies late fall or early
next year. We plan to start the hiring process in January 2026 to fill additional vacancies.
Budget Statement
The FY2027 budget request is $10,181,469 which is $ 416,158 above FY2026 budget.
The salary and wage amount requested is $9,132,636 which is an increase of $146,438. The
increase is a result of a new collective bargaining agreement for The Police Union and Superior
Unions which will expire June 30, 2026. The other increases include step increases for the officers,
and COLA for the civilians and administrative staff.
The operating capital request is $318,948.00 Which is $255,523 above the FY2026 request. This
is a result of requesting four vehicles in FY27 compared to one in FY26.
We did turn back salary because of four open vacancies and three retirements in FY25. We have
made progress to bring our staffing levels to full complement. We currently have 46 Officers with
one recruit graduating in November. We have four more new hires that will attend a Police Basic
Recruit Academy late this fall or early next year. We plan to start the hiring process in January
2026 to fill additional vacancies.
Our FY2027 Expense Request is $729,885 which is a $14,197 increase over FY2026.Changes are
highlighted in the table

Section 3 - 144

=== Page 213 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Fire Department
Department Mission
To provide the Town of Needham with an effective, well-trained team of professionals to protect
the lives and properties of its residents. This mission is achieved through providing fire
suppression, emergency medical services, emergency disaster preparedness, fire inspections and
fire safety education in the most cost-effective manner possible.
On the Horizon
The Fire Department patch has been completed and approved. This brings the Department in line
with the Town’s new branding initiative. This patch will represent the community of Needham
better and will be seen added to uniforms as replacements take place.
The Fire Department is working with the Treasurer’s office to begin accepting online payments to
streamline the fire-permitting process. The goal is to offer easy access to all residents and
businesses by having easy online payments. This process will be completely rolled out in the
coming months.
In an effort for more transparency, Ambulance rates will be posted online.
The Fire Department has established a group of Firefighters who are particularly interested in
community outreach. They have been doing a great job making connections and building
relationships with community members. This program will be developed even more in the coming
months as a growing desire from the community is evident for such engagement.
Among other things they are planning to coordinate a career day for high school students to
educate on the work of a firefighter/paramedic. The hope is to show young residents how to access
work in public safety as an alternative when graduating high school. When high school graduates
are hired, there are education incentives offered while actively employed here.
The hope is that this program will lead to interest in careers in public safety and attract a diverse
workforce.
A test will be offered in the Spring of 2026 for the rank of Deputy Fire Chiefs; this is part of the
promotional process used by this department since leaving the Civil Service system.
The EMS Administrator, along with the Fire Chief, will develop a plan to reduce workplace injuries.
The project for removal of 13 circuits of fire alarm wire throughout Town is well underway and due
to be completed by Spring 2026.
The Fire Department is working to update the fleet to have ALS engines for early medical
intervention. This will be invaluable in giving the capability of responding with advanced life support
when the ambulances are committed to other emergencies.
The Emergency Management Administrator has plans in FY 26 and FY 27 to continue all staff
training in each Town building, as well as update the emergency procedure guides and update
the Hazard Mitigation Plan working with DPW.
The Fire Department is due to take delivery of the new Fire Engine in 2027. This will replace the
department’s front line Fire Engine 1.
Much work has been completed for the long-term initiative to have the Fire Department Policies
and Procedures updated and available electronically for all members. This will provide a consistent
Section 3 - 145

=== Page 214 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Fire Department
and clear framework for operations, ensuring compliance, safety and improving efficiency. This
platform will be ready to roll out by Spring 2026.
As a department, we look forward to continuing to build relationships with all members of the
community and offering the highest level of service we can.
Budget Statement
The Fire Department has three (3) main sources of revenue for the Town. In FY2025 we collected
Ambulance Services $1,506,711 (a decrease of $54,857 over FY2024)
Inspection Permits $34,615 (a decrease of $5,895 over FY2024)
Wireless Fire Alarm Box monitoring $19,590 (an increase of $4,710 over FY2024)
These three (3) revenue sources represent an 3% decrease over FY2024.
DSR2 Salary and Wages
The Salary and Wage Permanent Positions are based on FY2026 salaries as Local 1706 had a
contract through FY2026. There is no information yet available for FY2027 salaries as negotiations
have not yet begun at the time of the writing of this statement. Included in these numbers are
many step and longevity raises along with additional members attaining higher education
compensation.
The Scheduled Overtime line on the DSR2 includes overtime necessary to cover all vacation and
¾ of all personal shifts. We also project the number of $784,639 to cover the estimated cost of
coverage for sick, military tours, mutual aid, callbacks, holdovers, storm coverage, 111F,
mechanics, training, special events, community outreach, court and staff meetings for members
of Units A & C. Training and Development increased slightly to adjust for contractual increases in
overtime rates. Dispatchers overtime includes desk coverage for use of vacation time, personal
time and all other types of leave. Training is state mandated for all Emergency Medical Dispatchers.
Working out of grade is based on the average usage over the past three years.
The Temp PT Special Assignment Admin position is not being funded in FY2027 in recognition of
the admin office being fully staffed and cross-trained for coverage during absences.
Grants:
The Emergency Management Performance Grant (EMPG) is a non-competitive grant from
FEMA that is administered by MEMA. Needham has been eligible for $8,500 in recent years.
Uses:
FY25 – EOC team workshop and table top exercise
FY24 – Back to Basics, ICS Overview
FY23 – Emergency Shelter Plan
The Assistance to Firefighters Grant (AFG), through a regional program, offered the chance
to receive reimbursements for a train the trainer for an ICS system - Blue Card. The Town of
Needham was eligible for $10,000 from this grant.
Uses:
This was utilized to train 4 staff members who in turn trained the rest of the Department in this
new system.
The Firefighter Safety Equipment Grant is a competitive grant offered through the Department

of Fire Services to offset some of the costs of equipment needs.
Uses: Needham Fire Department was awarded $29,125 out of a maximum of $30,000 and with
this were able to replace three thermal imaging cameras ($23,997) with round one funds. Round
two funds provided $5,127 towards the cost of a Face Fit Machine for Personal Protective
Equipment. This is a service that was provided by a third party in the past and now can be done
in house.
Hazardous Materials Emergency Response Grant
The Needham Fire Department participates in this program and has a technician assigned to the
regional hazardous materials response team managed by the grantor. The technician must meet
all membership requirements for this award.
Uses:
A $5,000 stipend is paid in two installments throughout the year to the technician for their
membership of the regional team.
There is a monthly training that the member must attend. The time and the time spent at call outs
for responses to incidents in the region, is reimbursed to the Department through this grant from
the State.

Accomplishments and Activities
The Fire Department has moved away from Civil Service and is using the testing practices
developed by the Town with Local 1706 for hiring and promotions. The hope is to reach a wider
pool of candidates with this approach to align with the DEI goals of the Town. Two new recruits
have been hired so far using this process.
The Tower truck has arrived and is currently housed at Station 2 where there is daily training on
this new piece of equipment, before it is put in service in Town.
Section 3 - 148

=== Page 217 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Fire Department
Each Town building along with Town staff is participating in active threat drills and trainings. The
department is also reviewing and updating our policies and procedures for active shooter responses
out in the field. ASHER training was completed with the Police Department.
The Incident Command System (ICS) has been enhanced and expanded through the introduction
of Blue Card training. This system is used widely throughout the country to keep personnel better
trained in decision making processes at the time of an incident.
Emergency Management conducted a workshop and tabletop exercise with the EOC team, to
understand what areas need improvement when it comes to emergencies in Town. The EM Policy
Group has also been meeting quarterly to continue these improvements and communication.
Cancer screening was conducted and offered to all members in the on-going effort to keep the
personnel healthy.
The Fire Department continues to remove all the outdated fire alarm wire around Town. This means
utility companies can move equipment when necessary. This task was made possible with funding
by the passing of the special warrant article at Town Meeting in May 2024.
The Fire Department has implemented a Paramedic Training Officer (PTO) program. The paramedic
training officers mentor new hires and train them to the highest standards. The PTO remains with
them throughout their time and ensures the highest standards of care to the residents in their
charge.
The Fire Department had three long serving members retire. Shawn Donovan retired in July 2024
with 32 years of service. Ronnie Gavel retired in July 2025 with almost 31 years of service. Al
Bulger retired in September 2025 after 26 years.
Back to basics training has been something that is continuously focused on. This is vital when we
do not see a large number of structural fires on a regular basis. The Fire Department carries out
daily trainings on skills that can become rusty if not used repetitively.

Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Fire Department Long Range Master Plan
Fiscal Year FY2027 Department Fire Department
Funding Amount $51,338 Funding Source General Fund
Article Information
This request is to fund a master plan study to assist with future planning needs of the Fire
Department. The study will include analysis of several strategic, foundational and operational
components to include:
• Mission, vision and values
• Community risk assessment
• SWOT analysis, goals and objectives
• Resource assessment and historic system performance
• Services provided
• Training and professional development
• Equipment and technology
• Emergency management and preparedness
• Mutual aid and collaboration with other departments and agencies.
With the population and responses to emergency calls on the rise, we are looking to the future to
meet the needs of the community. We feel this is necessary to continue the high level of
response and service that the Town of Needham is accustomed to.
There are several community expectations and factors that are going to change the needs of the
Town including, but not limited to:
• The opening of the Children’s Hospital.
• The addition of housing from the MBTA requirement.
• The MWRA tunnel project is slated to start in 2027 and will continue for 13 years.
• The Linden Street/Chambers Street project will replace single dwelling units with four
story, multiple unit buildings.
• The Avery property on Highland Ave is adding 186 residential units

Disclosure Explanation
The study will suggest strategies for meeting long-term needs and aligning community risk with
appropriate resources. Recommended approaches will offer short, mid, and long-term
implementation timeframes. These strategies will assist in making informed decisions regarding

=== Page 238 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department BUILDING DEPARTMENT
Department Mission
The Building Department administers and enforces the Massachusetts State Building, Plumbing,
Gas, Electrical, Mechanical, Sheet Metal and Energy Codes as well as the Town’s Zoning Bylaw and
portions of the General Bylaws including Stormwater and Signs.
On the Horizon
The department continues to work with the State’s new energy codes. Additionally, there are new
regulations regarding ADUs (Accessory Dwelling Units) and MBTA Zoning which will result in
additional workload. We continue to adjust to the newly adopted 10th edition of the State Building
Code and we are trying to rebuild/re-establish our Periodic Inspection process for approximately
160 businesses in the town.
Budget Statement
Our budget reflects a minor increase in expenses of $3,640. This allows for anticipated increases
in memberships for our professional organizations, monthly trainings for inspectors, a $1250
increase in clothing allowance for staff members to help maintain a professional appearance for
front facing employees and an increase of $800 to the vehicle budget to allow for regular car
washes to help maintain our vehicles. We are requesting a seasonal employee for 4 months at a
cost of $4,485 to help scan plans stored at 470 Dedham Ave. We had a 25% return in expenses
mostly due to $20,000 which is reserved for outside consultants if the need arises for review of
complex Fire Alarm and Fire Sprinkler systems on potential projects. We had a 7% return in salaries
due to unfilled positions. We remain short-handed and unable to fully restore the work involved in
safety inspections on certain buildings (approx. 160) as mandated by the State Building Code.
Accomplishments and Activities
Over the past 2 years, the Department has undergone significant personnel changes in that the
Building Commissioner, Assistant Building Commissioner, 2 Local Building Inspectors and 2
Plumbing and Gas Inspectors are new to the Department. We have been continuously training new
personnel in all the varied processes involved in building inspections and related tasks. The Building
Commissioner has been involved in meetings over a 15-month period and has aided in successfully
drafting revisions of the Stormwater Bylaw. We have seen completion of a large renovation project
at the Coca Cola facility and the construction of the new Children’s Hospital is nearly complete.
Residential home construction remains vibrant. We have made significant updates to our electronic
permitting system making it more user friendly for both staff and applicants.

Description and Explanation
There are thousands of plan sets stored at 470 Dedham Ave. This building is slated to be
demolished. Staff currently scans plans when time is available, but it is a large task overall.
This position would be responsible to scan plans during the stated hours on the DSR4S and to
inventory the overall project. This position would put a good dent in the number of stored
plans and would give a better idea of how much more effort will be needed to complete this
task prior to demolition of the building.

Section 3 - 178

=== Page 247 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Building Design & Construction
Department Mission
The Building Design and Construction Department (BD&CD) mission is to provide comprehensive
project management support and oversight services to the Permanent Public Building
Committee, advise and support the Town Manager, on municipal facility renovation, construction,
planning and operational needs on building renovation/construction projects having a total
project cost of $500,000 or more. BD&CD functions as the Employee Owner’s Project Manager
(OPM) for large town building projects. The overall goal of the department is to provide a high
level of project management services while completing renovation and construction projects on
time and within budget. The Department strives to investigate and resolve issues that arise in
the design, permitting, construction and commissioning of buildings as quickly and prudently as
possible. The BD&CD works to provide comprehensive project management and oversight on all
the municipal renovation/construction projects it is charged to complete.
On the Horizon
The Department is fully engaged in all the following PPBC design & construction projects:
1) The renovation of the Emery Grover Building for the School Administration at 1330 Highland
Ave (currently in the punch list/ warranty phase),
2) Rooftop Unit Replacement at the Broadmeadow Elementary School, (5-RTUs & ASHP
construction completed summer 2025, currently in punch-list/ 2-year warranty phase).
3) MSBA Feasibility Study Phase for the Pollard Middle School renovation, (PDP-Sept 2025, PSR-

7) The construction of the lighting and sound Theater System Upgrades (TSU) to the Newman
Elementary School auditorium. (Construction-summer 2026 completed by Oct. 2026)
8) Design and construction of renovations for the Center at the Heights (CATH) to include the
Kitchen, vestibule addition, fitness center expansion and relocation of the Game Room.

The Department coordinates with many other Town Departments during the design and
construction phases of each project. This includes - Town Manager, School Department, School
Principals & staff, Library, Health Department & CATH, all divisions of DPW, Building Maintenance
Division, Planning, Conservation, Park & Recreation, Finance Department, IT, Town Clerk, Police &
Fire, and Building Department. BD&CD also coordinates with all the respective “User Group” and
“Permitting” Boards and Committees including: PPBC, School Committee, Select Board, Library
Trustees, Council on Aging, Planning Board, Conservation Commission, Park & Recreation
Commission, and Finance Committee. As the Employee OPM’s for the Town we are also responsible
for the selection process in hiring and coordinating with the design teams, OPM consultants, and
contractors on each project and coordinating with Massachusetts authorities like the Massachusetts
School Building Authority (MSBA).
Budget Statement
The Department budget has increased by $5,456 or 1.37% in the Salary & Wages line item over
the last fiscal year, which includes 25% funding for the full year of the second Senior Project
Manager position (the remaining 75% funding comes from capital projects), projected cost of
living increases and/or anticipated step raises. The deduction of the 75% Senior Project Manager
salary allocation to capital project budgets is included in the DSR2 line 1.e. PRD1 Budget
Adjustments. The expense line item in DRS2B line #6 Total Expense, stays the same as last
fiscal year’s approved amount.
The department allocates project management time to certain capital projects once they are in the
detailed design and construction stage which leaves an uncommitted budget balance for FY2026
in the Salary & Wages line item. The amount of $110,614 or 29.7% in the Salary & Wages line
remained uncommitted in FY2025. Also, the department took longer than expected to complete
the hiring process of the new Sr. Project Manager during FY2025. The additional Senior Project
Manager position was filled in April 2025, and it took a month for the on-boarding process.
The Department also underspent the anticipated Expense budget by $9,229 or 43.5%. This was
due to several factors including: the AIA conference was in Boston this year reducing travel
expenses, some required continuing education classes were on-line, the new Sr. PM telephone and
communication expenses and other expenses commenced late in FY2025. While all members of
the department are MCPPO certified, continuing education is focused on a three-year renewal cycle
and will be ramping up during FY2026-7.
Accomplishments and Activities
The Emery Grover Building construction was completed. The gut renovation of this 126-year-
old, National Register Historic building was completed within budget allowing the School
Administration staff to return from the Hillside School temporary offices back to Emery Grover on
October 1, 2024. Punch list, energy rebate and warranty items are continuing through 2025.
The Eliot Elementary School Rooftop Unit (RTU) Replacement project was completed during
the summer of 2024, including four RTUs and the addition of Air Source Heat Pumps (ASHP) to
improve the energy efficiency of the building. Punch list and seasonal commissioning will continue
through August 2026.

Sunita Williams Elementary School- Solar Array
SMART payments average around $22,000 per year for ten years. The solar array also saves on
electrical bills by providing about one-third of the electrical power demand to the building each
year. The solar array has generated 779MWh since Oct. 2019, offsetting 467 tons of CO2.
The department personnel working as the Town’s OPM has realized over $953,900 in savings for
the Town over the last five years based on over $53.2M in construction spending. The savings is
based on the customary OPM fee of 3.5 - 5% charged by third party project management
consultants acting for the Town as the obligatory OPM on construction projects over $1.5M
pursuant to state law compared to Department payroll contributions being spent on Department
personnel.

=== Page 264 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
Department Mission
The Department of Public Works (DPW) is a comprehensive agency comprising nine divisions.
Seven of these divisions are funded through the General Fund, while the remaining two—Water
and Sewer—operate as independent Enterprise Funds. Divisional Breakdown: Within the General
Fund, the DPW has three Support Divisions that provide essential services to all nine divisions,
irrespective of their funding source: Administration, Engineering, and Fleet. Additionally, there
are four Operations Divisions funded by the General Fund: Building Maintenance, Highway, Parks
& Forestry, and Recycling & Solid Waste.
Core Mission and Vision
The Needham DPW strives to maintain and improve the built environment to ensure Needham
remains a healthy, safe, and enjoyable place to live. The built environment includes all municipal
assets, such as roads, bridges, sidewalks, sewer and water systems, stormwater infrastructure,
trash disposal, recycling, parks, trees, fields, and all School and Town buildings.
On the Horizon
The Department of Public Works (DPW) Fiscal Year 2027 budget request is designed to support
our core mission while proactively addressing several significant operational and market
challenges on the horizon. The focus for FY 2027 will be on building institutional resiliency and
ensuring continuity of high-quality services to the Town, while prioritizing Safety, Infrastructure
Preservation, and Enhancements to the Built Environment. The Department anticipates facing
its most pressing challenges in three main areas: workforce retention and recruitment, fiscal
pressures from market volatility, and the demands of modernization and compliance.
Workforce and staffing levels remain a critical area of focus, with a significant turnover
anticipated in the next few years due to retirements. This necessitates an accelerated focus on
hiring and succession planning to grow internal candidates. The most competitive challenge is
attracting and retaining skilled talent who can afford to live locally, particularly when competing
against the prevailing wage rates of the surrounding metropolitan area. This challenge is acute
in specialized areas such as HVAC maintenance, licensed water treatment and distribution
professionals, and skilled construction positions. To increase competitiveness, the Department
implemented a License Incentive Stipend program and anticipates that additional incentives and
improvements to the work environment will be necessary to overcome regional workforce
shortages.
The Department is navigating an environment of unprecedented fiscal constraints driven by
external market forces. The most pressing fiscal challenge continues to be the significant
increase in the cost for materials and labor for outside services. This includes a notable rise in
contract prices for services like roadway treatments and sidewalk improvements. Trade
uncertainty and tariffs are compounding the issue, putting pressure on engineering and
construction firms. This high demand has led to both increased prices and project delays for the
Town. The Recycling and Solid Waste Division anticipates rising trash costs due to the region’s
strained capacity to process trash and unpredictable recycling markets, necessitating the pursuit
of all practical diversion strategies to offset management expenses.
DPW is undertaking major initiatives to modernize operations and meet evolving regulatory and
public expectations, which require significant resource allocation. The Department will complete
the phased implementation of a new, consolidated work order and asset management system
(Confirm) across all operational divisions in FY 2027. This modernization is vital for shifting from
Section 3 - 196

=== Page 265 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
a reactive to a proactive maintenance model, but requires full adoption and training across the
Department. A major departmental goal for FY 2027 is to formally begin the process of seeking
Accreditation as an Agency of the American Public Works Association (APWA). This will provide a
rigorous benchmark against industry standards but requires sustained investment in the Safety
Management System, full OSHA compliance, and professional documentation.
DPW continues to think innovatively about how services are delivered. This requires engaging
residents and stakeholders in the process to ensure that there is buy-in when improvements are
made to the built environment such as traffic calming measures, reducing permeable surfaces,
enhancing walkability and safety, improving trash service delivery, and providing solutions for
stormwater quality and quantity. This requires more communication in the form of site visits,
educational sessions, working with local media, and reporting to appropriate committees on
current and planned upcoming work.
The budget presented for Fiscal Year 2027 is a proactive strategy designed to navigate
significant operational and fiscal challenges while securing the Department's future resiliency.
Our base budget request (DSR2) focuses on ensuring the continuity of essential services by
funding proactive and reactive daily maintenance across all DPW divisions. This commitment
ensures residents continue to receive reliable service for their roads, water, sewer, solid waste,
and public facilities. Our Service Enhancement Requests (DSR4s) directly address current
shortcomings in meeting community needs.
This budget is built on the Public Works leadership's belief that Needham residents expect high-
quality infrastructure that supports an exceptional quality of life. This includes:
• Well-engineered and constructed roads, sidewalks, and bridges.
• Safe drinking water and reliable sewer systems.
• Improved stormwater management.
• Beautiful and well-maintained parks, fields, and trees.
• Access to sustainable waste removal and recycling options.
• Well-operated, sustainable Town and School buildings.
Administration
The Administration Division is committed to providing clear, consistent, and reliable
administrative support across key operational areas, including budget management, personnel,
accounts payable, procurement, and customer service. Our goal is to support the operational
divisions of DPW by ensuring that administrative processes are efficient, transparent, and
responsive to their needs.
We will continue to explore and implement improvements through technological solutions and
process streamlining to enhance overall efficiency. Ongoing staff training and a focus on
succession planning will remain a priority to support employee development, knowledge
transfer, and long-term retention.
Building Maintenance
The Building Maintenance Division is committed to delivering reliable, consistent, and efficient
operations across all Town and School facilities, with an emphasis on occupant comfort, safety,
and long-term asset preservation. The Division continues to navigate a range of operational
Section 3 - 197

=== Page 266 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
challenges, including restricted year-round building access, extended lead times for critical
materials and deliverables, and varying performance reliability among contracted vendors.
Despite these constraints, the Division remains focused on executing a robust portfolio of capital
and maintenance projects in the upcoming fiscal year.
To support its operational goals, the Division leverages data and analytics from Brightly (Work
Order/Asset Management System) to inform a proactive preventative maintenance strategy
across municipal and educational buildings. This data-driven approach enhances decision-
making capabilities related to resource allocation, budget forecasting, and long-term
infrastructure planning.
A key strategic priority remains the advancement of sustainability initiatives through targeted
infrastructure upgrades. The Division is systematically replacing obsolete or inefficient systems
with high-efficiency technologies and the electrification of equipment, such as high-efficiency
condensing boilers, electric water heaters, air-source heat pumps, and LED lighting systems.
These efforts contribute to reduced energy consumption, lower operational costs, and support
the Town’s broader environmental goals of achieving net zero emissions.
To ensure service delivery remains aligned with growing facility demands, the Division will
collect and analyze operational data to assess staffing requirements across both custodial and
skilled trades personnel. This assessment will inform future hiring strategies and optimize
resource deployment across all buildings.
Furthermore, the Division is committed to continuing its collaboration with the Building Design
and Construction Department. This partnership aims to improve the efficiency and quality of
new construction and renovation projects, ensuring a seamless transition from construction to
ongoing maintenance operations.
Engineering
The Engineering Division is committed to fostering a welcoming and inclusive environment for
all team members, partners, and stakeholders. Our collective goal is to ensure that our projects
not only meet technical standards but also enhance the quality of life for the communities we
serve. To improve traffic flow and safety, we are undertaking several street and intersection
upgrades such as continuing the design of Marked Tree Road and the intersection improvements
at Central and Great Plain Avenue.
A significant focus of our infrastructure efforts this coming year will be the reconstruction of
aging sewer interceptor lines along RTE 128. This involves the replacement of deteriorated pipes
with larger diameter and durable materials that will handle current and future wastewater flows.
These upgrades are essential to prevent sewer overflows, improve water quality, and protect
public health.
In addition to immediate improvements, the Engineering Division is dedicated to long-term
planning. We are actively preparing master plans for our drainage systems for quality and
capacity. These comprehensive plans address current deficiencies and anticipate future needs,
ensuring that our infrastructure can support ongoing development and population growth. The
Engineering Division will also continue providing inspectional services for the multi-area gas and
underground electrical replacements proposed by Eversource.
Section 3 - 198

=== Page 267 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
Fleet
The Fleet Division aims to provide green, elite, vehicle service. Fleet is responsible for the
acquisition, repair, maintenance, and disposal of all department-owned vehicles, and they
ensure the department’s vehicles and related equipment are available, safe, and dependable so
Town agencies can deliver essential municipal services. Operationally, Fleet continues to
experience challenges with delays in the arrival of replacement vehicle deliveries and with repair
parts availability issues. As a result, Fleet continues to focus heavily on providing proactive
maintenance and effective utilization of the Department’s refurbishment budget to minimize
potential negative impacts to operations. The Division is actively providing training preparation
for ASE (Automotive Service Excellence) certifications for the fleet technicians. Fleet continues
to utilize an online auction vendor to dispose of surplus vehicles and equipment which provides
a significant increase in returns to the Town when compared to traditional “trade-in” methods.
The Division manages the Town’s fuel depot, supplying 69,590 gallons of unleaded gasoline and
37,535 gallons of diesel annually. Fleet continues to utilize B20 biodiesel seasonally to
supplement our petroleum-based diesel fuel usage on Town owned diesel-powered vehicles.
Biodiesel is produced from renewable resources, emits fewer greenhouse gases, and is
biodegradable. In line with the Town's commitment to sustainability and green initiatives, Fleet
Services manages the Town's electric vehicle charging stations. The Town's EV charging
infrastructure has expanded to include 10 EV charging stations. During the past twelve months,
the Town’s electric vehicle chargers dispensed 95,958 kWh of energy to both public and town
electric vehicles. Since its inception the Town’s electric vehicle charger network has dispensed
323,109 kWh of energy, which has a carbon offset equivalent to planting 8,285 trees and letting
them grow for 10 years.
Highway
The Highway Division remains committed to its efforts to construct and upgrade surface
infrastructure that meets the Town’s goals for safe multimodal travel. The Division will continue
to pursue state and federal funding opportunities for the reconstruction of Highland Avenue
from Great Plain Avenue to Webster Street, with progress being made towards inclusion in the
Transportation Improvement Program (TIP). The Division is also playing a role in the relaunched
design process for the Great Plain Avenue Corridor project in Needham Center. Highway plans
to incorporate Complete Streets standards in its regular roadway projects as much as possible,
focusing on improvements to bicycle and pedestrian safety, new traffic markings, and traffic
calming measures. The Division is also developing plans to better coordinate traffic signals
through equipment upgrades with the latest detection and phasing technology.
There are also challenges to consider. The Highway Division continues to experience difficulties
in scheduling and coordinating street and sidewalk improvement work due to supply chain
issues and a high demand for contractor services. This has required the team to adjust
schedules throughout the construction season to ensure project completion, and it is expected
that this will be the case in the upcoming year. The Highway Division also oversees snow and
ice operations for the Town, and the steady decrease in the number of contractors participating
in the snow program region-wide means that the Division must continuously adapt snow
operations planning to meet the Town’s standards for safe travel during winter weather events.
Section 3 - 199

=== Page 268 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
Parks and Forestry
The Parks and Forestry Division aims to maintain the “front yard of Needham” as welcoming,
safe, accessible, and aesthetically pleasing. The Division will continue to maintain the trees,
parks, fields, school grounds, and forests of the Town, ensuring safe and enjoyable options for
outdoor activities. Parks and Forestry looks forward to completing multiple major capital
projects, including wrapping up the final touches on the new McLeod softball field, completing
the renovation of Asa Small Little League field, refreshing the off-leash dog park, and
completing the Claxton Field renovation. Parks and Forestry will continue to partner with the
Park and Recreation Department on many projects and programs.
In addition, the Division will partner with counterparts on school grounds to improve their
conditions and act as a supporting agency to accomplish new priorities. The Division has been
facing some challenges regarding the changing climate and will need to continue to look at ways
to combat these challenges. Climate change will continue to challenge Forestry operations as a
diversity of pests advance in the region, causing harm to tree species that make up a significant
portion of Needham’s canopy, such as ash trees. Parks and Forestry will continue to look at
ways to adapt to these changing conditions. Lastly, the Division will continue to work with town
residents and stakeholders to help address our place in a sustainable Needham.
Recycling and Solid Waste
The Recycling and Solid Waste Division aims to keep Needham clean and green by providing
efficient, safe, sustainable, progressive, and practical solid waste disposal and recycling. During
this year, the Recycling & Solid Waste Division will be working on implementing
recommendations from the Service Delivery Study for trash and recycling for the Town. This
report will provide the basis for decision making going forward for the RSW Division. The RTS
will continue to work on deploying Bigbelly trash and recycling containers throughout town and
eliminate the use of the standard blue and white containers where appropriate. These units
improve cleanliness and discourage illegal dumping. The RTS will continue working with the
School Nutrition Services Staff and Building Maintenance Division to strengthen and expand the
food-waste diversion program offered at the schools. The food waste diversion programs will be
further promoted through Town outreach to expand resident drop off at the RTS. Needham, like
all Massachusetts communities, will be seeing rising trash costs as the region’s capacity to
process trash becomes strained. All practical diversion strategies will be pursued to offset the
costs of trash management.
Budget Statement
Salaries & Wages increasing $223,281, 1.79%
Expenses & Services increasing $442,709 4.55%
Operating Capital decreasing ($48,120), -63.22%
Total Budget Submission* increasing $617,870 2.78%
*not including snow & ice
Salaries
Only two group within DPW have established COLAs via negotiation which are NIPEA at 2.5% at
the beginning of FY27 with an additional 0.5% in January 2027 and ITWA at 3%. For the purposes
of the budget all BCTIA and non-represented staff have no COLA in their payroll adjustments.

=== Page 269 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
Most divisions saw a modest increase between 1% and 4%, mainly due to the incentive and
stipend pay. The standout was Engineering with a 20% increase in base and overtime pay.
Administration had a notable decrease of 15% largely from salary adjustments. Across the
organization, regular salaries are mostly stable and the bonus type pay of stipends, incentives,
and overtime is trending up. In FY26 incentive and stipend pay was included in the salary line.
For budgetary practices, these needed to be removed and added in their own line.
Administration increased overtime to accommodate hours needed for committee minute taking.
This is being absorbed in the DPW rather than the OTM. BMD saw an overtime increase due to
the number of staff out in a given week, the addition of annual building inspections that have
been lacking over the last number of years, and the increased number of non-billable permits.
The Engineering increase was due to the increase in site supervision for CIP and Utility projects.
Fleet has added managing auctions of surplus vehicles and pre-bid inspections on Saturdays. RSW
had a slight increase due to the need for trash collections in warmer months with increased
outdoor dining. The Overtime Budget has increased by $102,184, 9%.
The base salary for temporary help has remained essentially flat with no meaningful change.
Expenses and Services
Administration
The primary change in the Administration budget is related to the implementation of the
department wide consolidated work order system and the integration to our residential portal and
software licenses with an increase of $35,125. Additional expenses are related to increases in
legal advertisements due to the rise in auctioned vehicles and notices to the public. With a full
staff, training needs increased as well as the need for wireless communications and devices.
Net increase: $42,624.00, 21.65%
Building Maintenance

primarily due to contract increases and increases due to current spending trends. The Custodial

increased by 17%, though only $1,547, and this again was due to contract increases. Gasoline
was a decrease due to the migration to Electric Vehicles, hence the decrease in consumption.
Net increase $142,968, 3.22%

The Parks and Forestry Division’s budget is increasing primarily based on contract price increases.
There is an increase in the need for professional and technical services as the Town investigates
the creation and implementation of a tree by-law and consulting on irrigation connectivity. Other
increases include supporting the increase in clay prices as with the increase in inclement weather,
the need for quick dry clay has gone up. Additional increases include the number and prices of
nursery stock for private setback plantings.
