Section 3 - 210

=== Page 279 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
The Parks and Forestry Division receives supplemental funding from fees collected by the Park &
Recreation Department. This covers additional work that the Division does to maintain the field
in safe and well-maintained order for user groups. The anticipated transfer in FY2027 is $150,000.
Without these funds cultural practices would need to be cut back on certain fields and the playing
conditions would decrease.
Accomplishments and Activities
Administration
Over the past year, the organization has experienced several staffing transitions, including two
internal promotions—one Management Analyst and one Administrative Analyst—as well as the
hiring of two Administrative Specialists and a new Director of Administration. These changes
have presented both challenges and opportunities for the organization. To enhance overall
efficiency and effectiveness, the Administrative Division is implementing a new Work Order
system that will be integrated with SeeClickFix. Additionally, the Division is developing a short-
term solution for Time and Attendance tracking. It is also actively evaluating options for a new
ERP system and planning for its future integration.
Administration continues to address the need for adequate staff facilities through Department of
Public Works (DPW) projects currently progressing through the capital planning process. At the
same time, the Division remains focused on managing increasing expectations and service
demands with limited resources.
Building Maintenance
The Building Maintenance Division completed many significant repair and upgrade projects
throughout the town including roof repairs, elevator repairs, life safety repairs and upgrades,
exterior lighting repairs and upgrades, to name a few. BMD worked closely with BD&C on the
installation of four new fully electric Roof Top Units at the Eliot and added an Air Source Heat
Pump to use during the shoulder months for re-heat purposes, which reduces the need to use
the gas fired boilers. The Division focused time on repairs to masonry concerns at the Rosemary
Recreation Center, the Public Safety Complex, Memorial Park, and the Library which needed
significant work to address cracked sidewalks and walkways. A significant portion of Building
Maintenance’s focus continued to be on completing HVAC repairs and upgrades at almost every
building in Town and the Division will continue to work with the Town’s Sustainability Manager
on all projects to find ways to reduce the use of gas appliances.
Engineering
The Engineering Division achieved a highly productive FY 2025, significantly accelerating its
capital plan thanks to supplemental federal ARPA funding. This funding allowed the Division to
complete major water, sewer, and stormwater projects including Reservoir sediment removal,
Walker Pond outlet control reconstruction, Rosemary Lake outlet repair, stormwater upgrades at
Walker Gordon Field, and the first phase of the Route 128 interceptor sewer rehabilitation.
The Division also completed the Greendale-to-Concord drainage improvements. Beyond
construction, Engineering provided extensive inspection for major projects for the South Street
16-inch water main replacement and NHS Tennis Courts. Significant regulatory oversight was
provided, inspecting 433 street permits, reviewing 347 building plans, 12 project reviews for
Planning Board, 22 project reviews for ZBA, and completing 19 traffic studies with 9 traffic
regulations.
Section 3 - 211

=== Page 280 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
Fleet
The Fleet Division completed a repurposing of surplus unit 29 into a dedicated Snow Fighter.
The repurposed vehicle, reassigned as unit 8029, had its original body and jetter equipment
removed, a new custom body was fabricated in house by fleet staff and a heavy duty 14’
snowplow was installed. Fleet added a new Assistant Fleet Supervisor position. This new
position has provided required support to shop operations which is improving efficiencies. 40
GPS devices were installed in the operational fleet. The Division activated an additional electric
vehicle charging station at the Emery Grover Building and now manages 10 chargers with 20
service ports providing charging for both Town and public owned vehicles. Fleet performed the
following top 5 service tasks over the past 12 months: 184 PM Services, 141 State Vehicle
Inspections, 72 Tire/Wheel Services, and 71 Snowplow Repairs.
Highway
The Highway Division rehabilitated approximately 11 lane miles of roadway. Paved streets
included Brookside Road, Rolling Lane, Forest St (Central Avenue to Rolling Lane), Rosemary
Street (MBTA tracks to Parish Road), Tillotson Road, Damon Road, Tanglewood Road, Hollow
Ridge Road, Pythias Circle, Washburn Avenue, Marshall Street, Gage Street, Pheasant Road,
Mallard Rd, Winfield Road, Ware Road, Noyes Street (Great Plain Avenue to Central Avenue),
Farnham Circle, Warren Street (Great Plain Avenue to Kingsbury Street), Lawton Road, Old
Farm Road, Longacre Road, and Charles River Street (Grove Street to Town of Dover). Most of
these rehabilitation projects were preceded by the installation and upgrade of ADA-compliant
handicap ramps, with approximately 80 of these ramps being added over the course of the two
partial construction seasons. Another 14 lane miles of roadway were surface treated to extend
the streets’ life cycles and delay the need for rehabilitation, and over 3,000 feet of sidewalk was
repaired on Forest Street, Laurel Drive, and across several ramp compliance and smaller work
order projects.
The Highway Division also geometrically reconstructed the intersection of Forest Street and
Brookside Road to calm traffic speeds and enhance pedestrian safety near Volante Farms. This
intersection project reduced the open pavement with berm extensions that will help address
stormwater runoff, and force traffic to slow down when making turns around the corner. The
Division also continued its work to convert traffic signal detection from outdated magnetic loops
and outdated camera equipment to modern overhead cameras, upgrading this equipment to
four different intersections, including in the downtown area. Finally, Traffic installed several
additional flashing signs and Rectangular Rapid Flashing Beacon (RRFB) crosswalk systems,
including on Harris Avenue at Pinewood Road, Chestnut Street at Junction Street, Central
Avenue at Cedar Street, and Chestnut Street at Emerson Road. These improvements enhance
pedestrian visibility and driver awareness.
Parks and Forestry
The Parks and Forestry Division had a productive and collaborative year, completing a wide
range of projects to enhance Needham’s parks, athletic fields, and public spaces. Major park
projects included the DeFazio Park fence replacement and the Claxton Park renovation, as well
as re-hanging backstop netting and upgrading the irrigation system at Memorial Park—funded in
part through outside grants. The Division partnered with Parks & Recreation to renovate nine
Little League infields and the Walker-Gordon infield, and completed landscape improvements
around the Avery Field memorial rock. At Asa Small Baseball Field, a new synthetic turf batting
Section 3 - 212

=== Page 281 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Department of Public Works – General Fund
cage was installed, and the High Rock multipurpose field was leveled and renovated through
fraze mowing and seeding. The Forestry team oversaw the start of the town-wide street tree
inventory, replaced downtown street trees in partnership with Hartney Greymont, and
scheduled healthcare treatments to protect legacy trees at Newman and Memorial Park. The
Division collaborated with Eversource along Webster Street to remove invasive trees and replant
native species. Lastly, Forestry hosted a regional aerial bucket truck training for staff and area
professionals. In celebration of Arbor Day, the Division partnered with second graders, the High
School Environmental Club, Eversource, and community volunteers for plantings and cleanups
at Noyes Park, Rail Trail, Riverside Park, High School, and Broadmeadow Elementary School.
The team also supported the Needham High School Greater Boston Project to install a rain
garden at the PSAB building, furthering the Town’s commitment to sustainability and
environmental education. Parks and Forestry worked closely with the Engineering Division on
the High School tennis court reconstruction, South Street water main improvements, and the
Amity Path extension and resurfacing projects. Four new benches were installed at Cricket Field,
Amity Path, Heights Common, and Mitchell School in partnership with NCRTF. Throughout the
year, staff responded to more than 50 public SeeClickFix requests and numerous emergency
tree calls, demonstrating the Division’s responsiveness and commitment to public service.
Overall, the Parks and Forestry Division’s efforts reflect a strong commitment to maintaining
safe, sustainable, and beautiful public spaces through collaboration, environmental stewardship,
and community engagement.
Recycling and Solid Waste
The RTS Division processed 8,645 tons of trash, 1,941 tons of household recycling, over 1,000
car tires, and 1,236 mattresses. Trash tonnage decreases were the result of adjusted
commercial scale fees that made it less desirable for haulers to bring out-of-town trash to the
Needham transfer station. Notable achievements for the Division included outreach efforts to
conduct a town-wide survey for the Service Delivery Study. 2,318 households responded to the
RTS survey which will provide a solid basis for finalizing the report. MA DEP grant funds were
utilized to pay for mailings and additional analysis needed to complete the report.
RTS continued to work with Needham School’s Nutrition Services on food waste diversion and
reducing trash volumes at the schools. 384 tons of food waste were collected from Needham
Schools and the RTS. That number is a slight increase over last year, but it shows evidence of
coming to a plateau. To further reduce trash, the RTS supported the school’s roll-out program
for reusable trays at cafeterias, providing on-site guidance for students at the start of that
program. Food waste diversion was also made available at this year’s Harvest Fair at Greene’s
Field. The RTS continued with two paper shredding events this year, to safeguard resident
information and still capture the paper for recycling. The RTS division ran a successful
Household Hazardous Waste Day event in October. RTS staff provided trash collection services
in support of four town-wide cleanup events sponsored by the Needham Revitalization Trust
Fund, the Charles River Conservancy, and Needham’s Park and Recreation Division (spring & fall
cleanups). The RTS also partnered with Public Health and the High School’s S.A.L.S.A.
(substance abuse prevention group) to help collect and recycle empty vaping devices for safe
processing.
A new employee trailer was installed at the transfer station. RTS staff methodically demolished
the old trailer, carefully extracting all the metal siding and framing for scrap metal recycling.
The new trailer was occupied in July of 2025.

Description and Explanation
This request is for an additional Park Ranger within the Parks and Forestry Division. The
addition of the Park Ranger will enhance the existing Ranger’s significant impact on reducing
litter, vandalism, and improper use of fields throughout the town. Additionally, this will
provide much-needed assistance to the existing Park Ranger, reducing the need for
supplemental help from other divisions and allowing them to return to their core
responsibilities.
For example, currently the Director of Park & Recreation and the Superintendent of Parks &
Forestry cover emergency calls whenever the Park Ranger is not on duty. The addition of a
new Park Ranger will allow for staggered scheduling to cover critical days when the existing
Ranger is not scheduled, such as Sundays and Mondays. This will provide a consistent and
stable presence in the parks throughout the entire week thereby adding to the enforcement of
park rules and regulations, and a reduction of litter and vandalism.
One of the most valuable services the existing Park Ranger has provided is educating the
public on the use and permitting of the Town’s fields. This has resulted in users calling in to
make sure they are going through the proper approval channels and adherence to Town
policies.
Section 3 - 229

=== Page 298 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request

Department of Public Works – Fiscal
Department 2027
General Fund Year
Title Park Ranger Priority 1
The additional Ranger would allow for the expansion of public education and further raise
awareness regarding the responsible use of these facilities with wider-reaching oversight and
visibility.
As with the existing position, the additional Park Ranger would be tasked with monitoring
Needham’s parks, open spaces, and trails, providing safety support to visitors, educating the
public regarding pertinent rules and regulations (including permitting), assisting with daily
maintenance and stewardship, and serving as a positive presence and resource to those using
these facilities.
The Park Ranger is responsible for:
• Patrolling parks and recreation areas both on foot and in clearly marked Town vehicles to
promote public safety, ensure park cleanliness, protect the Town’s capital assets from
vandalism, and inform and educate patrons about facility rules and regulations. In addition to
regular park visitors, this includes monitoring the various programs and permitted activities
by athletic groups and other parties.
• Interacting/engaging with park visitors Town-wide by providing support and acting as a
knowledge resource regarding park facilities and policies. They may provide staff oversight at
park events and programs.
• Performing routine maintenance and care of all grounds, pathways, picnic areas, public
restrooms, pavilions, and other facilities in the parks, open spaces, horticultural areas, and
school grounds throughout the Town.
• Performing maintenance and improvement projects at park trails.
• Reporting safety concerns, incidents, and maintenance needs to DPW, including the need for
additional trash pick-ups and vandalism cleanups and repairs.
• Utilizing the Town’s work order system for asset management
This request is in support of the Select Board goal #3 “Promotes and sustains a secure, clean,
and attractive place to live, work and play.” This position will check in on proper permitting for
our fields and outdoor spaces and ensure they are not being used without authorization.
Permitting generates revenue for the Town.

Description and Explanation
This request proposes upgrading all seventeen (17) Town irrigation systems to cloud-based
controllers with wireless management capability. The project includes a one-time investment of
$20,000 for hardware upgrades, $10,000 for installation-training and a recurring annual cost of
$2,000 for cellular data plans and software subscription to support connectivity.
Transitioning to a cloud-based system will provide the Department of Public Works with real-
time, remote control of all irrigation zones across Town properties. This will enable staff to
monitor usage, make adjustments instantly, and respond to changing weather conditions without
needing on-site visits.
Current irrigation systems at the fields must be manually adjusted, which increases
overwatering, water waste, and non-compliance with state or regional drought restrictions. This
exposes the Town to higher water costs, enforcement actions during drought conditions, and
reputational concerns related to sustainability. In addition, the lack of real-time alerts and
remote management creates operational inefficiencies and delays in responding to system
failures, which can lead to turf and landscape damage and higher long-term repair costs.
Approving this request reduces those risks by ensuring water use is efficient, compliant, and
Section 3 - 233

Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Department of Public Works – Fiscal
Department 2027
General Fund Year
Title Cloud Connected Irrigation Controls Priority 2
responsive to changing weather conditions.
The benefits of this upgrade include:
• Water Conservation: Automated weather-based adjustments will prevent overwatering
and reduce unnecessary irrigation, saving thousands of gallons annually.
• Operational Efficiency: Remote access eliminates staff travel for manual adjustments,
improving response time.
• Sustainability: Optimized water usage supports Town-wide conservation goals and
reduces strain on municipal water supplies.
• Improved Field and Landscape Health: More precise irrigation scheduling ensures turf,
athletic fields, and landscaped areas receive the right amount of water when needed.
This investment will modernize the Town’s irrigation infrastructure, reduce water consumption,
and promote long-term sustainability, while providing operational savings over time. This request
aligns with Select Board Goal #7 to ensure that “Needham is a sustainable, thriving, and
equitable community that…preserves and responsibly uses the earth’s resources.”

Description and Explanation
This request is for an additional Public Works Craftsworker within the Highway Division,
specifically to support Traffic Maintenance operations. The primary role of this added position
would be to assist the only currently dedicated Traffic Maintenance staff—the Public Works
Technician—in their wide range of responsibilities. These include the installation, maintenance,
and repair of traffic signs, traffic signals, and parking meters, as well as the supervision of all
pavement markings operations.
The demand for these services has expanded in recent years as the Town has continued to
add more maintenance-intensive flashing signs and pedestrian and bicycle pavement
markings. The Highway Division’s role in the parking meter program has also evolved to
include collecting, bagging, and tagging the meter revenue, all of which takes hours of time
away from other tasks. Especially with the increasing number of traffic control work orders
and improvement requests from the Transportation Safety Committee (TSC), this is a large
workload for one person. Some of these jobs also involve large or heavy signs and equipment,
requiring two people to perform the work safely. The Traffic Craftsworker would provide
valuable support and backup to our existing Traffic staff member, allowing the Highway
Division to complete more tasks and cover multiple responsibilities simultaneously. As the
team looks to the future and anticipates role changes and retirements, it is crucial to establish
Section 3 - 235

=== Page 304 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Department of Public Works – Fiscal
Department 2027
General Fund Year
Title Public Works Craftsworker Priority 3
a pathway for staff to receive more advanced traffic control and traffic signal training, and this
position would be one of the ways to build that knowledge base and succession plan.
One of the other key roles of the Traffic Craftsworker would be to substitute for the existing
Craftsworker when they are unavailable, assist them with more complicated skilled work, or to
join the rest of the team when larger projects require the entire Division’s help. The Highway
Division currently operates with 11 unionized staff who are responsible for the maintenance of
260 lane miles of roadway, 140 linear miles of sidewalk, and 246 miles of grass, curbing, and
parking lots. More infrastructure requiring maintenance is built each year, mostly by
contractors who require oversight and inspection. The construction and maintenance workload
has continued to grow while Highway has been unable to offset this demand with additional
in-house staff. The additional Craftsworker could be called in to operate extra equipment or
provide additional supervision over contractor crews to make sure the jobs get done on time
and to Town specifications. This is similar to how the existing Craftsworker flexes between
their duties based on need.
As a final and critical benefit, the Traffic Craftsworker would also be a contributing member of
the Snow and Ice Program. During major snow events, the Town depends on all DPW staff
being available for snow removal, especially with the continued downward trend of contractor
support. Though union staff are limited in the amount of vacation they can take during the
winter months, there are several times where backfilling, or route reassignment is necessary.
The new Craftsworker would be an additional CDL driver, vital during snow events to keep as
many snow fighting units deployed as possible.
This request is in support of the Select Board Goal #5 “Safe,” specifically the item “Fosters a
climate of safety in homes, businesses, neighborhoods, streets, sidewalks, bike lanes, schools,
and public places.” The Traffic Craftsworker would expand the capacity of the Division to
address traffic safety concerns while also supporting the effort to clear snow and ice from the
public ways and ensure that they are safe for all users.
This position has been requested as an HMEO or Craftworker for the past several years to assist
in bringing the Highway Staffing back up to meet the demands of the operation.

Description and Explanation
A key management system will allow for the centralized access management of the
department’s primary fleet and vehicle pool which will improve vehicle security, prevent
unauthorized access, improve vehicle key and key-fobs management, and provide key
performance indicator data for improved efficiency and utilization in right sizing the
department’s fleet.
General Summary
Implementing a fleet key management solution streamlines all aspects of fleet operations with
powerful, automated controls over scheduling and key transactions, and on-the-ground
insight into fleet utilization so the Town can optimize how operations vehicles and equipment
are utilized. Some of these benefits include:
• Utilization reporting: The system will provide valuable data on the usage of all vehicles,
allowing for better decision-making and resource allocation.
• Vehicle information data collection: All necessary information about each vehicle will be
stored in one centralized location, making it easy to access for maintenance and
tracking purposes.
• Vehicle issue tracking: Any issues or maintenance needs can be quickly recorded and
Section 3 - 238

=== Page 307 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Department of Public Works – Fiscal
Department 2027
General Fund Year
Title Fleet Key Management System Priority 4
addressed through the system, ensuring the safety and reliability of our vehicles.
• Pool vehicle scheduling and calendar with phone app functionality: Users can easily
schedule and reserve pool vehicles through a convenient phone app, making it simpler
to manage vehicle usage.
• Vehicle assignment customization: The system allows for customization of vehicle
assignments based on user needs, ensuring that the right vehicle is always available
for the right purpose.
• Pool vehicle booking versatility: Users can book pool vehicles for a variety of purposes,
from official town business to personal use, making the pool more versatile and
accessible.
• On-demand bookings: If a vehicle is needed last minute, users can make on-demand
bookings through the system, ensuring that all needs are met in a timely manner.
• Five types of reports: The system will generate five types of reports, including user,
vehicle, utilization, transactions, and capacity reports, providing valuable insights into
vehicle usage and performance.
• Notifications for unauthorized or suspicious use: Any suspicious or unauthorized vehicle
use will trigger notifications, allowing for quick action to be taken to address any
issues.
Overall, implementing a key management system for the Department of Public Work’s
primary fleet and vehicle pool would greatly benefit our community by improving efficiency of
use for vehicles, increasing accountability, and ensuring the proper maintenance and
utilization of our vehicles.
This request aligns with Select Board Goal #7 that Needham “Combats the climate crisis;
Prioritizes sustainability, including transitioning from fossil fuels to clean, renewable energy.”
Improvements to the efficiency of Town vehicle usage will help to ensure that pool vehicles are
used as effectively as possible and provide information regarding overall fleet utilization so the
Town can optimize how operations vehicles and equipment are utilized.

Description and Explanation
In 2024, the Engineering Division of Public Works decided to fill a full-time position of
Engineering Aide through a cooperative education program (co-op) through a local university
specializing in Engineering. This yielded a positive experience in the Spring of 2024 where that
student was able to be exposed to the world of Civil Engineering from the municipal
perspective, and the Engineering Division was enriched with additional support and a student
with new ideas and perspectives.
It has been difficult in both the private and the public sector to find applicants in the field of
Civil Engineering and providing these college and graduate students with the opportunity to
learn this field, while providing critical support to the Town, can help to develop our future
workforce while supplementing the fast-paced work of our Engineering office.
At this time the Engineering Division would like to continue to fill the role of Engineering Aide
with a full-time co-op student to assist with keeping up with the needs of the division. This
project aligns with Select Board Goal #6 that the Town “identifies and implements strategies to
hire, support and retain diverse staff at every level of the organization.”

Title Appropriate Funds from Outside Sources
Department of Public
Fiscal Year 2027 Department
Works – General Fund
Funding Amount TBD Funding Source Misc.
Article Information
Text of Proposed Article: “To see if the Town will vote to appropriate funds from [TBD] in the
sum of [$XX,XXX] for the purpose of [TBD], said sum to be spent under the direction of the
Town Manager, and take any other action relative thereto.”
The Town often receives monies from outside entities, such as Eversource or through the
Planning Board process, to spend for a specific public works infrastructure purpose. In order to
be able to spend these funds, Town Meeting must appropriate the received sum for the intended
purpose. The ability for the Town to receive these funds often relies on the Town committing to
spending the funds on the intended purpose.
The exact payer, sum, and purpose of the funds will be determined prior to Town Meeting, as it
will vary.

Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Fleet Refurbishment Program
Department of Public
Fiscal Year 2027 Department
Works – General Fund
Funding Amount 150,000 Funding Source General Fund
Article Information
“To see if the Town will vote to raise and/or transfer and appropriate the sum of $150,000 for
the purpose of funding the Fleet Refurbishment Program, said sum to be spent under the
direction of the Town Manager, and to meet this appropriation that $150,000 be transferred from
Free Cash; or take any other action relative thereto.”
In FY2018, the Fleet Division implemented a refurbishment program for Fleet assets and related
components. The goal of this program is to extend the life cycles of the vehicles, increase
operational safety, and eventually reduce reactive maintenance with longer term planning. The
Fleet Refurbishment funding requests are spread out to allow the Division time to plan multiple
repairs at once, follow proper procurement procedures, and have the work completed efficiently.
Refurbishment work on the DPW's fleet includes corrosion abatement, treatment, and refinishing,
replacing corroded chassis, air brake tanks, and brake valves, rebuilding primary components,
replacing suspension systems and brakes, and updating lighting and reflective striping. Spending
funds to refurbish the existing fleet helps to extend life cycles. While this has this been an
ongoing practice of the DPW, current market conditions are creating a growing need to extend a
vehicle's lifecycle longer than initially anticipated, as replacement vehicles are taking an
unprecedented amount of time to be delivered. The Fleet Division must invest additional
resources into the planned maintenance of their equipment in order to ensure continued
operations.
Disclosures YES NO

9. Is this required by a court or other jurisdictional order? X

and maintenance on DPW vehicles and equipment. It is requested every other year.
8. This program will continue as additional fleet repair and maintenance projects on DPW
vehicles and equipment will be identified as necessary every year. However, the specific
maintenance items completed under this request will be performed as needed, and as a result,
will vary from year to year.

direction of the Town Manager, and to meet this appropriation that $1,000,000 be transferred
from Free Cash; or take any other action relative thereto.”
The Facilities Maintenance Program includes the annual maintenance of public buildings used
throughout Town and by the School Department, including, but not limited to, asbestos
abatement, HVAC work, painting, and other repairs and necessary upgrades. In 2020, the School
Facilities Master Plan was released, identifying a number of major repairs needed in the school
buildings. DPW is requesting $1,000,000 to continue to address the recommended repairs in the
Master Plan as well as other planned projects necessary to the continued operation of Town
buildings.
Also included under this article are planned maintenance programs that use a rotating annual
schedule by building, such as duct cleaning at the Eliot, Hillside and Library, and wood floor
refinishing at the High School. Additional projects to be completed in FY2027 include exterior
window cleaning on several buildings and schools, refinishing of the CATH deck, High Rock
foundation access improvements, HVAC split systems replacement at several locations, bathroom
renovations at the Daley building, DPW administration office upgrades, and roof repairs at the
High School and Hillside.

Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Resurfacing for Parking Lot Leased to Public Library
Department of Public
Fiscal Year 2027 Department
Works – General Fund
Funding Amount 85,000 Funding Source General Fund
Article Information
“To see if the Town will vote to raise and/or transfer and appropriate the sum of $85,000 for the
purpose of funding the contractually obligated resurfacing of the parking lot leased from the
Christ Episcopal Church to the Needham Free Public Library, said sum to be spent under the
direction of the Town Manager, and to meet this appropriation that funds be transferred from
Free Cash; or take any other action relative thereto.”
The Christ Episcopal Church of Needham has leased the shared use of their parking lot on
Highland Avenue to the Needham Public Library for many years to supplement the Library’s
limited parking in the lower lot on Rosemary Street. The most recent 10-year lease agreement,
negotiated and signed in 2015, contained a clause stating that the Town of Needham “will
overlay the Licensed Premises before December 31, 2025.” The Town is currently engaged in the
negotiation process for a new lease agreement starting in 2026, but must also fulfill the terms of
the 2015 agreement. The Highway Division inspected the parking lot and determined that an
asphalt overlay as described in the lease agreement is not feasible without negatively impacting
the sidewalk ramps, driveway aprons, and drainage in the lot and necessitating additional
construction. Due to these factors, a mill and pave is the only feasible option to satisfy the
Town’s obligations under the 2015 lease as stipulated.
The proposed resurfacing project would involve milling and repaving 1.5" of the asphalt surface,
the repair and adjustment of metal castings, and the replacement of the existing pavement
markings.

Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Special Financial Warrant Article Request
DSR5
Title Swale Rain Garden Program
Department of Public
Fiscal Year 2027 Department
Works – General Fund
Funding Amount 120,000 Funding Source General Fund
Article Information
“To see if the Town will vote to raise and/or transfer and appropriate the sum of $120,000 for
the purpose of funding the Swale Rain Garden Program, said sum to be spent under the direction
of the Town Manager, and to meet this appropriation that $120,000 be transferred from Free
Cash; or take any other action relative thereto.”
The proposed project involves implementing stormwater quality and quantity improvements
focused on public roadways that partner with adjacent property owners. Designs will consist of
establishing 8-foot-wide vegetative buffer strips within the Town's Right-of-Way and on portions
of private properties where appropriate. These strips will feature native wildflowers, tall grasses,
and select trees, are designed to significantly interrupt stormwater flow and reduce stormwater
related flooding, encourage infiltration, and remove nutrients through natural soil and plant
activity, enhancing overall water quality in support of the Town’s phosphorous reduction goals
and NPDES permit requirements. A preliminary analysis of the drainage areas will ensure the
buffers are placed where they will have the greatest impact to manage stormwater runoff,
improve water quality, increase infiltration, and provide ecological and aesthetic benefits through
nature-based solutions. Concept plans have already been prepared along Charles River Road,
and in the Walker Pond watershed vicinity. These solutions will be strategically placed to
intercept and infiltrate stormwater runoff from surrounding areas [e.g., road and drive ways,
rooftop downspouts, parking lot, sloped lawn], preventing runoff from overwhelming nearby
storm drains or causing erosion. We are seeking match grant opportunities for funding the
program. It is estimated per project this would cost $4,500 – $12,000 depending on size,
complexity, and in-kind labor and materials required.
Disclosures YES NO

9. Is this required by a court or other jurisdictional order? X
10. Is this a personnel related request? X
11. Is this a local option acceptance request? X
12. Is this in support of a goal of a Board or Committee? X
All “YES” responses must be explained Below
Disclosure Explanation
3. This is potentially a matching grant funding request. With many applicable grant programs,
we are anticipating that this project will be a strong contender for these programs.
6. This is a pilot program to determine its success in stormwater management.
8. This program has the potential to span multiple years depending on its success in interrupting
stormwater flow, reducing stormwater related flooding, and improving water quality.

DSR5
Title Swale Rain Garden Program
12. This program will support enhancements to water quality by improving stormwater runoff
through nutrient treatment via nature-based solutions in support of the Town’s phosphorous
reduction goals and federal NPDES permit requirements. In addition to helping to improve water
quality and mitigate stormwater related flooding, these nature-based solutions will include
native plantings that will help to provide habitat for local pollinators, supporting local
ecosystems. This program is in support of Select Board Goal #7 that “Needham is a sustainable,
thriving, and equitable community that… preserves and responsibly uses the earth’s resources;
and cares for ecosystems.”

DSR5
Title Time Clock Hardware Upgrades
Department of Public
Fiscal Year 2027 Department
Works – General Fund
Funding Amount 95,250 Funding Source General Fund
Article Information
“To see if the Town will vote to raise and/or transfer and appropriate funds in the sum of
$95,250 for the purpose of upgrading the time clock system hardware, said sum to be spent
under the direction of the Town Manager, and to meet this appropriation that $95,250 be
transferred from Free Cash; or take any other action relative thereto.”
The Town seeks to upgrade the existing time clocks that have reached their end of life with new
units that support the implementation of the Town’s current time clock system. The current
clocks will be out of production as of December 31, 2025, and support for the existing units will
end on March 31, 2027. Delaying the project may impact the operation of the current clocks as
the company will no longer address software bugs or compliance changes, and parts will not be
available to replace or repair damaged clocks.

Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Municipal Parking Program
Purpose
The Town operates and maintains several municipal lots in and around the business districts for
customer, visitor, and employee parking. The Town issues parking permits to local businesses for
their employees at a fee. The Town maintains and enforces parking regulations in these lots.
Municipal parking lots include Lincoln/Chestnut Street, Eaton Square, Mark Lee, Dedham Avenue,
Chapel Street, and Kimball Lot.
Budget Statement
The Municipal Parking budget is utilized by the Town to seal pavement markings, repair fences,
and maintain the landscaping in the municipally-owned parking lots. The funding is also used to
pay lease fees for lots owned by outside organizations by utilized by the Town, such as the MBTA
parking lot.
Budget Changes
Salaries and Wages increasing $1,547, 5.32%.
Expenses and Services increasing $4,990, 3.34%.
Total Budget Submission increasing $6,537, 3.66%.
Salaries
There are no changes to the overtime program for the municipal parking services. Increases in
this budget are reflective of the base salary increases in the NIPEA collective bargaining
agreement.
Expenses and Services
The increases seen in these expense lines are from the annual lease costs for all three rented
parking lots.

=== Page 341 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Health & Human Services
Department Mission
The Needham Department of Health & Human Services (HHS) provides programs and
services that support and enhance the quality of life in Needham. HHS includes the following
divisions: Aging Services, Public Health, Veterans’ Services, and Youth & Family Services. Its
mission is to protect, preserve, and promote the health, wellness, and social and emotional well-
being of all Needham residents
* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * ** * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
Aging Services Division
Mission:
The mission of the Aging Services Division is to respond to the needs of Needham’s older residents
by providing a welcoming, inclusive, and safe environment with programs, services, and resources
that enhance their quality of life and provide opportunities of growth. The Center at the Heights
serves as a focal point for supporting aging in the community.
On the Horizon:
The Aging Services Division continues to offer a wide variety of programs and services to older
adults and their families through both in-person and hybrid platforms. Services include daily meals
(both at CATH and delivered to residents’ homes); health benefits counseling, creative and social
classes; special events and trips; case management and mental health counseling services;
transportation; entertainment; information and referral services, educational programs; a state-
of-the-art fitness center; health and wellness programming; and volunteering opportunities. The
Aging Services Division provides the resources and supports in Needham that are essential to
healthy aging.
Though the effects of COVID-19 created an initial decrease in attendance at the CATH, the desire
for in-person and hybrid programming is growing each year. The Aging Services Division
Programming team expertly offers a wide variety of programs ranging from health and wellness
to recreational. Over the last three fiscal years, the attendance numbers in the CATH building have
grown, and hybrid programming continues to hold its place as well. The Programming Department
offers approximately 20-25 programs a day, always including one special event unique to that day.
The cost for quality programming continues to increase and therefore the Aging Services Division
is asking for additional funds to support programming. Active and accessible engagement is a
critical component to healthy aging and priority to the Aging Services Division.
Social isolation and its impact on health aging continues to drive efforts at Aging Services.
Psychosocial supports including a short-term mental health counseling program. The short-term
mental health counseling program has been a major success. Last FY, there were 370 counseling
sessions provided by the social work department (an increase from 288 the preceding FY).
Understanding the current mental health crisis in the state of Massachusetts and the need for
qualified clinicians, makes this service priority for the Aging Services Division. Included in this
budget submission is a DSR-4 request to increase funding to expand the hours of the part-time
Clinician position. Though mental health counseling is a vital and well utilized service, it is more

Another critical component to healthy aging that remains a priority in the coming fiscal year for
the Aging Services Division is accessible and affordable transportation. The Aging Services
Division received grants from the Massachusetts Council on Aging Association as well as
MassDOT to continue a vital medical transportation program which expands upon existing
community services to provide rides to older adults to the Boston area hospitals. Understanding
the need for more on-demand, affordable and accessible transportation, the Aging Services
Division switched from utilizing taxi/livery services and in-house vehicles to rideshare services.
Through this service, 265 individuals were able to receive medical transportation at no cost. The
Aging Services seeks to continue to expand this model and incorporate more funds into the
operating budget to support it, as the grant climate is making this program harder to sustain.
The breadth of services provided by the Aging Services Division illuminates an ongoing
commitment to supporting health aging for Needham residents. The Aging Services Division is
committed to continuing to meet the needs of older adults in the community and services as a
beacon for supporting goals related to healthy aging.
Budget Statement:
This year’s budget submission for Aging Services Division reflects reductions in two spending
categories, reflective of the move towards more computer-based work and a decreasing need for
paper mailings and office supplies. Spending increase requests are reflective of increased service
and programming offerings, as well as overall utilization of the CATH. A decrease in anticipated
grant funding for transportation supports the need for increased operating budget funding for
emergency medical transportation not otherwise available through current community resources.
A DSR4 submission is also included to meet the growing mental health and support needs in the
community.

Social Services:
• The Social Services Unit continued to provide case management, information and referral
services, and counseling to older adults in Needham. Social Workers offer in-person
individual and group support services, both in the office as well as in the homes of
homebound older adults. In FY25, the Social Work Unit served 379 individuals and
logged 4,676 appointments, an increase of nearly 400 appointments from the prior fiscal
year.
Transportation:
• The Aging Services Division Transportation Department provides essential rides to older
adults in Needham to and from the Center, on shopping excursions, and trips. Utilizing
two additional grants (Executive Office of Aging and Independence & MassDOT) continued
its’ medical taxi ride program, offering free transportation to medical appointments within
a 12-mile radius for older adults in Needham. 265 rides medical rides were provided at no
cost to Needham residents.
• 4,663 rides were provided directly through Council on Aging vehicles to 221 Needham
residents. These rides include trips to and from the Council on Aging, the grocery store,
on recreational trips, and various other outings.
SHINE:
• In FY25, SHINE provided education to 1,505 attendees at 50 group events, a 20% increase
from last year. SHINE logged 8,916 contacts with beneficiaries, a 4.2% increase from last
year.
• From July-December 2024, SHINE calculated a savings of $3,375,905 in cost savings for
702 Medicare beneficiaries who changed their plan per SHINE’s recommendations.
On the Horizon
Public Health Division
Mission:
The Needham Public Health Division is empowered through the Needham Board of Health by the
Massachusetts General Laws to enforce state and local public health and environmental
regulations.
The mission of the Division is to prevent disease, promote health, and protect the public health
and the social well-being of Needham residents, especially those who are most vulnerable. Public
Health staff work toward fulfilling this mission through collaboration with state and local agencies
and community partners. The work largely consists of promoting health practices based on
research and evidence, enforcing local and state regulations, and advocating for policy and
regulatory changes that promote health and well-being.
One the Horizon:
Public health staff completed a busy year in FY2025, and a number of critical plans and reports
were developed and finalized last year including the a four-town Strategic Plan for sharing public
health services, a report on steps to modernize one-quarter of the Board of Health’s outdated

Updates were also made to a number of protocols and standard operating procedures.
As in previous years, some of the key needs and issues that are increasing and are forecasted to
continue to increase include continuing vaccination clinics (and requests for new vaccinations like
RSV); acquiring funding to address ongoing and emerging concerns about domestic violence,
hoarding, and homelessness; and mental health and substance use prevention. Some of the
ongoing needs for the public health division include reviewing, and updating policies, procedures,
and regulations and pursuing public health accreditation with a site visit scheduled for early
December 2025.
Budget Statement:
This year’s base budget submission for the Public Health Division reflects increases in some lines
to accurately reflect the costs of certain services and equipment which have escalated rapidly in
the last three years. The largest actual increase is requested for substantially elevated software
license costs, an area in which expenditures have exceeded budgets by nearly 50% the past two
fiscal years.

Accomplishments & Activities:
Environmental Health -
• The highest priority of the Environmental Health Unit is to protect the public’s health through
implementation and enforcement of State and Federal regulations and local Board of Health
ordinances to ensure compliance with environmental health and safety standards.
• The Environmental Health team monitors and regulates a wide range of business
establishments, facilities and activities, including, but not limited to: food service establishments;
tobacco retail vendors; Medical Marijuana Treatment Center; Biotech companies; residential
housing; construction activities (demolition, septic system and well installations); waste hauling;
bodywork; public and semi-public swimming pools; and many other activities. We review online
permit applications and issue permits through the Town’s ViewPoint Cloud permitting website.
These permit reviews have increased as building projects, including septic system installations,

have ramped back up again. We are also seeing an uptick in pest nuisance complaints
throughout town, which we are investigating and sending out pest control information and
working in conjunction with owners and pest control operators as issues arise, on a case-by-case

• In 2025, the Public Health Division Environmental Health Team was once again awarded a
combined base and travel/training grant from the National Environmental Health Association
(NEHA) and U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), specifically from the FDA Retail Flexible
Funding Model (RFFM) Grant Program, to adopt and maintain additional FDA Voluntary National
Retail Food Regulatory Program Standards and to allow staff to attend conferences.
• The Environmental Health Team has been very successful in receiving grant money from the
FDA for the past seven years. The FDA grants continue to be a critical source of funding which
allows the Division to continue to host mandatory forum trainings which have trained more than
80% of Needham’s 130+ food service establishments on the current Food Code requirements
and the most frequently cited Food Code violations documented during inspections. In the four
upcoming trainings in November, we are focusing on our revised Food Safety Excellence
Program, which encompasses the newly revised Board of Health approved Food Code
Enforcement Policy and Food Establishment Inspection Report Scoring initiative, and we will also
provide up to date information about safe food handling practices and requirements. Training our
permitted food establishment owners and management staff on an annual basis has proved to be
a significant step towards improving food safety compliance in Needham and we hope to expand
upon these free trainings in the future.
Accreditation -
During FY25, the Needham Public Health Division focused on applying for full public health
accreditation. The application was completed and submitted in mid-December 2024. We
received feedback and made improvements to our application. The next step is a site visit from
the accreditors in early December 2025.
The accreditation process has identified some areas, such as quality improvement, performance
management, and workforce development, that will require ongoing work. If the Division
achieves accreditation, Needham will apply for reaccreditation in five years.
The Traveling Meals Program -
The successful Traveling Meals program continued to provide meals to the most vulnerable
home-bound population in town, especially those Needham residents living alone with cognitive
or physical limitations which impair meal preparation abilities. The program provided an average
of 857 meals delivered to 53 clients per month in FY25. We have up to 28 volunteers delivering
meals for nine months out of the year and an average of four paid part-time staff working during
the summer months to continue providing the nutritionally balanced two-meal packages year-
round to our clients.
Nursing -
During FY 2025 the Public Health Nursing team continued to provide influenza and COVID-19
vaccinations as needed and continued to adjust to the changing climate. The Public Health
Nursing team is working to provide the Foundational Public Health Services that the

Department Information
DSR1
Department Health & Human Services
Massachusetts Department of Public Health is working to role out to each town and city, as levels
of services to provide to every resident in Massachusetts. The Public Health Nurses continue to
pursue grant-funding to support the development of a mother-baby check-in program for every
new mother in Needham.
FY 2025 continued to see increased community participation in activities such as vaccination
clinics, and increased utilization of substance use prevention, harm reduction, and mental health
resources. There continues to be an increased need for financial assistance, challenges in food
access, and other educational needs. In FY25, Public Health Nursing continued the momentum
towards certifying Needham as a HEARTSafe Community, which requires establishing proactive
steps to protect residents from sudden cardiac arrest. These efforts have provided residents with
many opportunities to learn CPR and enabled residents to identify Automated External
Defibrillators (AEDs) throughout town.
Epidemiology -
The data analyst/epidemiologist has been able to provide data and in-depth analyses to help
inform the Public Health team’s work. Making data-informed decisions has allowed the Public
Health team to better inform the community and find new ways to reach the town’s most
vulnerable populations and increase the response to public health concerns. In FY25, the data
analyst/epidemiologist worked with the Environmental Health team to streamline reporting of rat
sighting data and create an educational website on rodent prevention for residents and
community members which displays the reported rat sighting data in real time. This provides up-
to-date information for residents and allows the Environmental Health team to perform targeted
interventions. The data analyst/epidemiologist has been working across departments with teams
such as the Schools, Sustainability Manager, and the Fire Department on projects that include
sustainability, climate change, informational data development, and data sharing needs.
Substance Use Prevention -
The Substance Use Prevention Teams continue to provide services and education to Needham
residents and its shared services partners. Some of the key services include using the MetroWest
Adolescent Health Survey data to create targeted educational campaigns and prevention efforts
to help decrease substance use in adolescents. Peer Recovery Coaching and community outreach
has been another valuable resource in FY 2025 for residents who are curious about recovery and
families who would like to help loved ones with recovery. Program development and opioid
education and harm reduction, including Narcan distribution and education, have been other
large pieces of the Substance Use Prevention team’s work.
Public Health Preparedness -
In FY 2025, the Public Health Emergency Preparedness Team worked to review and update
existing emergency operation plans as well as develop new plans. They have worked to provide
education and training to the Medical Reserve Corp and to develop emergency materials that are
culturally appropriate, including availability in languages for all Needham residents.
Budget Statement
Veterans Services Division
Section 3 - 281

=== Page 350 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Health & Human Services
Mission:
The mission of the Veterans Services Division is to provide services determined by Massachusetts
law including: the administration of benefits for veterans and their families who meet eligibility
criteria; the care of veterans’ graves, ensuring the burial of indigent veterans and their eligible
family members; the ceremonial observance of national and state holidays dedicated to veterans
and patriotic purposes; to take such actions as may be necessary to ensure the well-being of
Needham’s veterans; and to actively pursue available federal benefits for veterans and their
families.
Needham is a member of the West Suburban Veterans District, which was established in fiscal
year 2010. The District also includes Wayland, Wellesley, Weston, and Westwood. The district
includes a Veterans Director (Dan O’Neil) and a Deputy Director (Dave Campisano) to work with
each community.
On the Horizon:
Partnerships and Outreach continue to grow within the Veteran Services Division, enhancing
the Division’s ability to provide necessary services to not only low-income Veterans and their
families, but also to assist widows and family members with VA applications and to provide
resources to assist in the care of their loved ones. One such partnership that is growing rapidly
is the joined forces with SSVF (Supportive Services for Veteran Families). The core Concepts of
SSVF are Housing First, Crisis Response and Choice. “Choice” refers to the “powerful
engagement tool and one that is critical to the success of the Housing First model. Empowered
participants are more likely to remain involved in activities after their placement, particularly if
these activities have been mutually identified, with their case managers, as supporting their
housing stability”.
Budget Statement:
As a member of the West Suburban Veterans District (WSVD), Needham receives dedicated
support for the administration of benefits and services to veterans and their families. The
vast majority of the Veterans Services budget is consumed with those two cost categories
(veterans’ benefits and district administration costs), and funding for Memorial Day
ceremony, flags and grave markers, Purple Heart Day Ceremony, Veterans Day
remembrance and Vietnam Veterans Day. Some of the funds spent on veterans’ benefits and
other services are eligible for up to 75% reimbursement from the Commonwealth.
This budget was developed based on the best estimate of the FY 2027 WSVD assessment,
the surplus that was available in FY 2025 from the two primary funding lines (Governmental
Charges and Veterans’ Benefits) and the projected spending levels in FY 2025. Overall, the
budget shows an increase in Governmental Charges from $77,500 to $85,000. While a
$7,500 increase may seem large, the fees for the District have been increasing significantly;
there was a 10% increase between FY 2024 and FY 2025 dues, and then an 8% increase
between FY 2025 and FY 2026. A $7,500 increase in the budget for FY 2027 would, if that
were the amount of the district fee charged, be only a 4% increase.

Accomplishments and Activities
Youth & Family Services Division
Mission:
To empower youth and families with accessible, equitable mental health care, enriched
programs, and vigorous advocacy. Committed to breaking down stigma and obstacles to care,
cultivating a community in which every individual—no matter the background—can flourish.
Anchored in the identification and addressing of unmet needs, the full spectrum of mental health
challenges receives comprehensive support. Through collaboration, education, and outreach,
voices are heard and their concerns addressed; interests are elevated and collective wellbeing
made a genuine community priority. Participation in committees and coalitions enhances public
awareness, strengthens education, and ensures inclusive involvement in shaping mental health
solutions. Diverse perspectives and robust partnerships champion a future where every person is
truly seen, heard, and valued.
On the Horizon:
Over recent years, there has been a marked rise in mental health needs among young people,
amplified by stressors such as the COVID-19 pandemic, social isolation, educational disruptions,
and economic instability. The demand for mental health support among youth continues to
intensify, and the coming period offers a critical window to expand, adapt, and deepen
programming. The Massachusetts Association of Health Plans explains, “There is a long-standing
Section 3 - 283

=== Page 352 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Health & Human Services
crisis in children’s behavioral health in Massachusetts. Prepandemic research studies indicate
that approximately one in five children and adolescents experienced a behavioral disorder each
year and showed major increases in certain mental health symptoms, including depressive
symptoms and suicidal ideation. Massachusetts isn’t alone; this is a nationwide crisis. According
to the American Academy of Pediatrics, rates of childhood mental health concerns were already
on the rise over the past decade. However, the COVID-19 pandemic along with the issue of racial
inequality has dramatically impacted and changed children’s worlds across the country. The
pandemic exacerbated the issue and took an unprecedented toll on their mental health and
intensified challenges in accessing care.”
More specific to Needham, According to the MetroWest Adolescent Health Survey, 35% of high
school students and 23% of middle school students report feeling nervous, anxious, or on edge
at least half the days in the past 2 weeks (prior to the survey) and 28% of high school students
and 16% of middle school students report feeling unable to stop or control worrying. 22% of
high school youth and 13% of middle school youth reported depressive symptoms over the past
12 months, and 12% of high school youth and 8% of middle school youth reported engaging in
intentional self-injury in the past 12 months. 9% of high school youth and 12% of middle school
youth seriously considered suicide in the past 12 months; and 2% of high school youth and 3%
of middle school youth attempted suicide in this time. At the same time, demand for services
has increased, while access remains limited due to ongoing challenges such as workforce
shortages, insurance barriers, and stigma — all of which continue to delay or prevent many
young people from receiving timely diagnoses and care.
Given these realities, our efforts must prioritize new and enhanced program structures that
widen access, increase capacity for mental health supports, and deepen community connection.
To serve more youth efficiently and effectively, the growth of group modalities and structured
clinical programs is essential. Rather than one-to-one therapy alone, offering more
evidence-based group interventions allows a broader reach: for example, psychoeducational
groups (social skills, anxiety management, emotional regulation), process groups (peer support
around grief, identity, transitions), and targeted intervention groups (e.g. depression support,
self-harm recovery, resilience building). These groups can help reduce waitlists and give a
“stepped care” pathway for youth whose needs fall between universal and intensive clinical care.
Where appropriate, using modular group curricula (e.g. dialectical behavior therapy skills groups,
cognitive-behavioral therapy group modules) can ensure fidelity and consistency.
Another promising pathway Youth & Family Services is implementing is brief, structured clinical
programs (e.g. short series of sessions, modular therapies) that offer time-limited, high intensity
intervention prior to making recommendations for ongoing treatment. This can allow more youth
to benefit from therapeutic engagement sooner.
To lower barriers and align services with youth in their natural environments, Youth & Family
Services will prioritize expanding community-based programming and community visibility. This
includes delivering group and psychoeducational sessions in accessible spaces such as libraries
and schools. Youth & Family Services will also organize pop-up mental health workshops and
drop-in hours at trusted community hubs. By bringing programming into the places youth
already spend time, Youth & Family Services aims to reduce transportation and scheduling
obstacles, normalize help-seeking behaviors, and encourage earlier engagement.
Section 3 - 284

=== Page 353 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Health & Human Services
To reduce stigma, increase awareness, and build community readiness, Youth & Family Services
will continue to work on and grow comprehensive education and outreach strategy. A recurring
"Mindful Community Speaker Series" brings together mental health experts, youth with lived
experience, educators, and clinicians to speak on topics such as anxiety, trauma, identity
development, and digital wellbeing. This initiative is in its third year of operation and brings
together community partners such as the Needham Community Council and the Becca Schmill
Foundation.
In parallel, Youth & Family Services will lead a broader community education campaign that
includes webinars, parent and caregiver workshops, curated social media content, and practical
resource toolkits. These efforts will be done in partnership with local schools, libraries, and
community organizations, and are designed to elevate awareness of early warning signs,
promote emotional literacy, and normalize seeking help.
Expanding training opportunities is essential to building a mental health-informed community.
Youth & Family Services will continue to scale Youth Mental Health First Aid training to educators,
coaches, youth mentors, religious leaders, parents, and community partners. With a local trainer
network in place, Youth & Family Services will provide ongoing access to these courses to
strengthen community response. In addition, teen Mental Health First Aid will continue in a
partnership with the Needham Health and Wellness Department at Needham High School. This
curriculum is trained in all 10th grade classes and requires approximately 90 hours of training at
the school.
Internally, YFS will invest in staff development to meet growing clinical demand and complexity.
Training will focus on key topics such as trauma-informed care, suicide risk assessment, de-
escalation strategies, safety planning, co-occurring disorders, dialectical behavior therapy (DBT)
skills, and modular or transdiagnostic treatment approaches. Ongoing consultation, peer
supervision, and learning communities will help staff stay supported and effective in higher-
acuity cases.
Through these strategic investments, Youth & Family Services will proactively expand access,
foster early intervention, and embed mental health support within the fabric of the community.
This approach ensures that as demand continues to grow, the system can respond with
flexibility, equity, and sustainability—ensuring youth and families receive support that is timely,
trusted, and effective.
Budget Statement:
This year’s base budget submission for the Youth & Family Services Division reflects a modest
increase in overall spending. We are recommending a reallocation of $11,000 from the
Professional and Technical line item to Professional and Technical – Licensed Professional, as this
more accurately reflects the intended use of these funds.
Additionally, we propose a $1,000 increase in the Professional and Technical – Seminars and
Training line to allow staff to attend high-quality trainings. Ongoing professional development is
essential in the mental health field, where evolving needs and emerging best practices require
staff to stay current in effective treatment approaches.
The Professional and Technical – Licensed Professional line is also increasing to accommodate
both the $11,000 reallocation mentioned above. Furthermore, Human Resources has advised

that one part-time staff member should be reclassified as a vendor. As a result, we are
recommending that $5,000 be moved from the Part-Time Salary line to this category.
We are also seeking a modest increase in programming funds of $1,000, due to both rising
material costs and a higher volume of programs being delivered by staff. In addition, we are
requesting a $500 increase to the cell phone budget to fully cover staff usage.
Lastly, after reviewing historical conference expenses, we’ve determined that a $500 reduction in
this area is appropriate, as past allocations have exceeded actual spending.
Overall, the proposed changes represent a modest budget increase that reflects both the rising
cost of essential services and our commitment to providing high-quality, community-based
mental health support.

Accomplishments and activities:
Over the past year, Youth & Family Services has continued its commitment to providing high-
quality clinical support, community outreach, and enrichment programs. Our initiatives are
designed to meet the evolving needs of the community and make a meaningful impact at both
individual and collective levels.
• One of the core priorities of our division is to ensure that Needham residents have access
to direct clinical services, including individual, family, and group therapy. Over the past
year, Youth & Family Services has significantly expanded its group therapy offerings and
has also increased the availability of therapy services provided within the community,
beyond the traditional office setting. These efforts have helped meet growing demand and
reduce barriers to access. The division played a vital role in responding to a range of
critical issues affecting Needham youth, including suicide, self-injury, grief, depression,
and anxiety. In addition, staff have offered a support group for parents who need
support on various topics.
• There has been a big focus on community-based programming that has excelled this past
year. The team has provided immediate clinical support, facilitated open community
conversations, and implemented crisis management strategies to address these urgent
needs. Partnerships with local organizations have strengthened our ability to provide
comprehensive care. On an individual level, crisis intervention and case management
services have grown considerably. Our staff has supported countless residents
experiencing challenges such as housing instability, domestic violence, trauma, and
mental health concerns—connecting them not only with clinical support but with a broad
network of community resources.
• Youth & Family Services continues to lead several impactful youth programs such as Peer
Tutor Program and the VIP Peer Mentor Program. The Volunteers Around Needham
program continues to grow and expand this year, offering youth volunteer opportunities
throughout the year, including during school breaks. A partnership with Newbridge on the
Charles, a senior living facility, has been especially successful, with youth volunteers
participating in orientation sessions and ongoing service activities.
• Some community events and initiatives include Needham Unplugged was a tremendous
success again this year. This collaborative effort—led by Youth & Family Services, the
Charles River YMCA, Park & Recreation, the Needham Free Public Library, and Needham
Public Schools— to create a calendar of activities in the month of March that encourage
people to disconnect from electronics and spend more time enhancing face-to-face
interaction and overall wellness. In addition, Y&FS continues to host two annual Family
Section 3 - 287

=== Page 356 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Health & Human Services
Nights, providing community members an opportunity to connect with each other and
with staff in a fun, family-friendly environment.
• The Patrick and Particia Forde Good Person Memorial Award was awarded to this year’s
nominee, as this is an opportunity to recognize a community member who significantly
gave back to the Needham community in unique ways. This year’s recipient was James
Murphy. James has served as Scoutmaster of Scouts BSA Troop 13 in Needham since
2011, offering outstanding leadership and mentorship to youth and families. He first
stepped into a leadership role in Newton following the sudden passing of a Scoutmaster,
continuing for a decade before becoming Troop 13’s Scoutmaster. Known for his patient,
empowering style, James has guided hundreds of Scouts in developing life skills, with
over 20 earning the Eagle Scout rank under his leadership. He rarely misses overnight
trips, uses vacation time for week-long camps, and encourages parent involvement
through his deep knowledge and steady commitment. Beyond Scouting, James engages
Troop 13 in numerous community service projects and plays a key role in supporting
Eagle Scout initiatives. His efforts helped launch Needham’s first female-linked troop,
expanding opportunities for local youth. James exemplifies the values of trust, service,
and leadership, making a lasting impact on both Scouts and the wider Needham
community.
• Youth & Family Services staff continue to serve as certified trainers in Youth Mental
Health First Aid (YMHFA), providing essential education to community members on
recognizing and responding to youth in mental health crises. Interest in this training
continues to rise in response to growing mental health needs. This year, several staff
members taught teen Mental Health First Aid (tMHFA) for the second year. This
curriculum is taught directly to high school students to help them support peers facing
mental health challenges. Our staff provided 90 hours of instruction to ensure all
Needham High School sophomores received this training, reducing stigma and
improving access to support. We are committed to continuing this partnership.
• Youth & Family Services, in partnership with the Needham Community Council and the
Becca Schmill Foundation, launched the Mindful Community Speaker Series: Talking Mental
Health Together to support community mental health and address the needs of families.
Events included the screening of Screenagers, an award-winning documentary about youth
and technology, which featured a staff-led discussion with positive feedback. Deborah
Farmer Kris presented Emotions 101 at Broadmeadow Elementary, offering practical
strategies for helping K–6 children build emotional awareness and resilience. Author
Michelle Icard spoke to over 100 parents, caregivers, and school staff about essential
conversations to have with adolescents before high school. A companion book club
discussion of her book Fourteen Talks Before Age Fourteen was also held at the Needham
Free Public Library. All events were well-attended and received overwhelmingly positive
responses.
• Y&FS facilitates a local employment program that connects youth with flexible job and
community service opportunities—including lawn care, snow shoveling, babysitting, and
other odd jobs—while also helping residents find reliable assistance for household tasks.
• Youth & Family Services maintains membership in several community groups including
the Youth Resource Network and the Community Crisis Intervention Team. Other groups
include the Domestic Violence Action Committee, the Homelessness Prevention
Committee, the Online Safety Coalition, the Community Health Resource Group,
International Overdose Awareness Day Committee, Chapter 84, and the Substance
Prevention Alliance of Needham, where Division staff sit on the steering committee and
action committees.
