Description and Explanation
Clinicians serve an essential and comprehensive role in the Aging Services Division. This role includes
providing free mental health counseling, case management, information, and referral services to older
adults and their networks in Needham. These services are provided at the CATH, in the resident’s home, or
via remote platforms determined by the given individual’s needs. Aging Services Clinicians also assist in
program development, facilitating supportive and educational groups, and engaging in ongoing community
outreach. The Needham Council on Aging Clinicians have also partnered with the state as a SNAP intake
site. It is essential, given our state’s current mental health crisis, to continue to provide free high quality
mental health and supportive services to older adults in Needham in addition to the services currently
offered.
One of the long-term impacts of COVID-19 was the increased need for mental health services for older
adults. The Needham Council on Aging was able to use ARPA funds to hire a part-time Clinician in the Social
Work Department in May of 2022 to increase the breadth of mental health and supportive services in
community. As part of the FY25 budget process, this position, which proved to be of great value, was
transitioned to the operating budget. Due to the success of this position and the level of need for service in
the community, the Aging Services Division is now seeking to increase the funding to transition its’ 19.5-
hour Clinician position in the Aging Services Division to a 30-hour per week position through the operating
budget.
Section 3 - 300

=== Page 369 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Health and Human Services
Fiscal
Department Department / Aging Services FY 2027
Year
Division
Title Expansion of Clinician Hours Priority 1
Through the initial expansion of the Social Work Department in the Aging Services Division beginning in
2022, the breadth of resident support and wellness programming provided has been expanded. The
average daily attendance at the CATH has increased from approximately 167 people per day in FY24 to 192
in FY25, to 228 people this fiscal year so far (these figures do not include those utilizing remote
programming). The more individuals utilizing Aging Services programming, the more requests will be
received for additional wrap around supports. Growth in overall attendance has likely been a factor in
increased requestions for social work services, such as mental health counseling. Table 1 shows the growth
of the mental health counseling program at the Needham Council on Aging over the last three fiscal years.
The Department has reached capacity in the number of individuals that we are able to serve, requiring the
department to maintain a waitlist. However, the number of interactions with each recipient has increased
due to a lack of clinicians available in the community to refer recipients to upon completion of the short-
term counseling program.
Referring to Table 2, the data indicates an increased number of social work interactions with residents over
the last 3 fiscal years. Though the number of individuals served has decreased slightly, the time spent with
recipient’s has increased which indicates that the population needs are becoming increasingly complex. An
additional explanation for this data relates recent efforts to expand the Aging Services Division’s free short-
term mental health counseling program. Mental health counseling continues to grow in popularity at the
Needham Council on Aging but is a more time-consuming service to provide and manage. Case
management and information and referral calls are less predictable in time requirements. Though many
case management scenarios are complex, there are also many contacts in which there is an exchange of
resources, and no further needs are required. Mental health counseling always requires one full hour for a
visit, in addition to time for scheduling and documentation and therefore requires more time to provide.
Increasing the hours of the Aging Services part-time Clinician will allow more clients to be accepted in the
short-term counseling program which often has a waitlist.
Efforts to expand mental health services for older adults in Needham aligns with the Needham Select
Boards 2026-2027 goals. The Select Board list ‘health and socially thriving community’ as their first goal.
The Select Board in includes specific language that states ‘supports positive mental health throughout the
community, including a range of behavioral health services…” Both the mental health counseling as well as
the general case management and information and referral services provided by the Social Work
Department at the Council on Aging address these criteria. The Select Board identified an additional
portion of the goal that states, ‘Provides guidance and support to assist residents as they access the social
safety net including SNAP, Medicaid Enrollment, and other programs.’ Assisting residents in accessing
public and private benefits has remained a high priority of the Aging Services Division. A specific example
illuminating this is that the Aging Services Division has served as a registered SNAP intake site, beginning
during the pandemic. The Social Work Department alone logged over 4,700 interactions with Needham
residents and their families last fiscal year, providing resources, mental health supports and information
and referral services.
There is an ongoing demonstrated need for increased mental health and supportive services for older
adults in Needham, a need which is at heart of the Needham Aging Services Division’s mission. The
growing complexity of individual needs in conjunction with a higher demand for mental health counseling,
which is a more predictably time-consuming support the request for increased Clinician hours. Increasing
the number of hours provided by the part-time Clinician in the Aging Services Division is critical to proving
ongoing support to aging residents.

Description and Explanation
Achieving accreditation for the Public Health Division is a long-standing priority of the
Needham Board of Health (BOH), and this request, or one like it, has been submitted several
times in the past (most recently as part of the FY 2022 budget cycle). Public Health Division
accreditation has been included in the BOH’s goals every year for the last decade, generally
with the goal description of “Pursue Public Health Division accreditation and support the
establishment of a culture of continuous quality improvement.”
Accreditation has been a positive process for previous applicants, fostering and formalizing
those public health departments’ commitment to quality improvement, performance
management, transparency, workforce development, and the capacity to deliver the CDC’s
Ten Essential Public Health Services. An independent national assessment of the Needham
Public Health Division will also serve to engender trust between the Division and the Town’s
residents who can be assured of the quality of the services they receive and the excellence of
the staff providing those services.
The process of achieving public health department accreditation is rigorous and requires
significant staff time and resources to conduct community health assessments, develop

Health Division received a nine-month Accreditation Support Initiative grant of $14,960 from
the National Association of County and City Health Officials (NACCHO) to help advance these
efforts. Additional efforts have continued over the years, supported by staff and interns and
smaller planning grants from state and non-profit funders.
In 2024, the Needham Public Health Division successfully achieved all the requirements and
pre-requisites for accreditation and formally submitted its application in 2025. If successful,
the annual fees of approximately will be $7,500 starting in FY 2027 to maintain accreditation
and to support the costs of re-accreditation (which must be completed every five years).
• This project submission aligns with the goals and objectives of the Board of Health.
• A VERY modest level of support from some Town departments (Building Maintenance,
IT) will be required in advance of the accreditors site visit for things like extra cleanup,
touchup painting, and ensuring that conference room technology is working successfully.
• Public Health Accreditation is not yet a requirement for discretionary grants from state
and federal funders, as well as from non-profit foundations. Yet accreditation is
considered a “bonus” factor in the scoring criteria for new funding streams from public
health focused foundations including de Beaumont, Kresge, and Robert Woods Johnson.

=== Page 376 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Health and Human Services Fiscal FY 2028
Department
Department Year (4 months)
Full-Service Community Nutrition
Title Priority Future Form
Program
Summary of Request
The Needham Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) seeks new funding to meet the
nutritional needs of adults ages 60 and over in Needham by replacing the existing Traveling
Meals and Springwell Community Dining Program with an in-house food service operation run
out of the CATH kitchen, which is slated to be remodeled between now and 2028.
Background
Currently, there are two programs that increase access to affordable and healthy food in the
Needham community: the Springwell Community Dining Program and Traveling Meals.
Springwell is a non-profit that operates a congregate meals program, designed to provide hot,
nutritious meals and socialization opportunities to older adults and individuals with disabilities in
Needham. The Springwell Community Dining Program serves about 20 customers a day, five
days a week, at Needham’s The Center at the Heights (CATH). The Needham Council on Aging
(CoA) currently offers one community dinner a month at the CATH, called the Dinner Bell, where
meals are catered. Typically, about 50 people attend these dinners. The CoA also serves
breakfast on weekday mornings at the CATH.
The Traveling Meals Program serves individuals in Needham who have physical or mental
cognitive issues that prevent them from being able to get their own food, without any age or
income restrictions. Springwell connects customers who are unable to show up in person to the
Springwell Community Dining Program at the CATH to the Traveling Meals program. These meals
are prepared by kitchen staff at the Beth Israel Deaconess Hospital in Needham. As of April
2025, Traveling Meals had 48 clients, with 856 meals produced by the hospital for that month.
Documented Need and Justification for Request
Interviews with leadership from Needham’s Council on Aging (CoA) and the Traveling Meals
Coordinator highlighted several areas of improvement in the Traveling Meals program and
Springwell Community Dining Program. Key concerns included uncertainty about the continued
partnership with the hospital for Traveling Meals and low participation in Springwell’s Community
Dining Program. Additionally, as the Springwell program relies on federal funding, recent and
projected budget cuts at the federal and state levels in 2025 raise concerns about its long-term
sustainability. If funding reductions extend to programs such as Medicare, Medicaid, and SNAP,
the Town of Needham may face increased demand for social services and a rise in food
insecurity. The Community Nutrition Program aims to address these challenges by implementing
an in-house meal program at the CATH that delivers affordable, nutritious food to residents of
the greater Needham community.
What Health Issue Does This Request Address?
Increasing food insecurity among older adults in Needham is a serious public health issue that
requires immediate action.
According to the 2025 MA Healthy Aging Community Profile estimates, 18.2% of residents in
Needham are 65 years or older. The Profile estimates that of Needham residents 60 years or
older, 4.6% have received food stamps in the past year and only 18.9% have five or more
servings of fruits or vegetables per day.
Section 3 - 308

=== Page 377 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Health and Human Services Fiscal FY 2028
Department
Department Year (4 months)
Full-Service Community Nutrition
Title Priority Future Form
Program
A 2018-2019 DHHS Housing Authority Assessment found that 37% of Needham Housing
Authority (NHA) residents were not aware of the Springwell Community Dining Program at the
CATH, highlighting a need to increase awareness of food access programs in Needham.
In the 2022 Needham Healthy Aging Assessment, NPHD surveyed 738 Needham residents ages
60 and older. 16% of respondents reported some degree of difficulty with accessing food in the
past year, which included difficulty with finding the kind of food they wanted, getting
transportation to the grocery store, and having groceries delivered. Additionally, 8% of
respondents reported being unable to prepare their own food as a result of health issues and 6%
reported some degree of experiencing food insecurity, including the inability to afford healthy
meals. 49% of respondents reported feeling worried about one’s own financial security related to
the cost of living. Concern about the cost of living and its effect on financial security was higher
among individuals with lower annual incomes, especially those earning less than $50,000 per
year.
The findings from these assessments demonstrate a need to expand access to affordable,
nutritious meals for Needham residents, especially among those 60 and older. This need can be
addressed by implementing an in-house Community Nutrition Program that integrates and
enhances existing services offered by the Springwell Congregate Meals Program and Traveling
Meals, while strengthening outreach and engagement strategies to ensure long-term
sustainability of the program and its impacts.
Board of Health Goals
This request for funding for a Community Nutrition Program addresses the following Board of
Health goal:
● Expand access to safe and healthy food to members of the community, and invest in
nutrition programs specifically targeted for seniors, the disabled, and those residents with
the most limited resources.
FY28
FY28 Community Nutrition Program Model
This program will offer a Congregate Lunch Program with one meal five days a week, three
breakfasts a week, and one dinner once a month. The program will serve 35 meals per day,
meaning 690 meals will be available to the Needham community per month. These numbers are
based on the amount of meals that are currently served by the Springwell Congregate Lunch
Program and the CoA’s Dinner Bell. The Community Nutrition Program will run based on the
aforedescribed model from March 1, 2028 through June 30, 2028 (Spring of FY28).
FY28 Costs, Funding, and Revenue
The following breakdown of costs are for one-third of the necessary annual costs in FY28
(covering a four month period in the spring), which will then ramp up to 100% funding for a full
12 months in FY29.
Funding Request: Labor
The total requested funding for FY28 is $30,960, which includes wages for both the Head Chef
Section 3 - 309

=== Page 378 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Health and Human Services Fiscal FY 2028
Department
Department Year (4 months)
Full-Service Community Nutrition
Title Priority Future Form
Program
and Community Nutrition Program Coordinator, uniforms, and computers. Hourly pay for
program staff is based on Needham Public Schools’ (NPS) Nutrition Services' pay rates for
cafeteria staff. The Community Nutrition Program Coordinator and Head Chef are paid based on
the hourly rate an elementary cafeteria manager was paid in 2025 classified under Step 1. The
hourly pay for these positions are on the higher end to ensure retention of staff and by
extension, the sustainability of the program. Labor costs for both positions include a 2.25%
Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA) for each additional year between 2025 and 2028.
Revolving Fund
A revolving fund will be established to cover the costs of purchasing ingredients and groceries,
which will later be partially replenished through meal sales. The DHHS anticipates a community
donation of $50,000 to seed this revolving fund, which will cover start-up equipment expenses,
including a point-of-sale system, reusable serving supplies, and small kitchen equipment such as
pots, pans, and cutting boards.
The Community Nutrition Program will join the Needham Public Schools’ Nutrition Services
shared purchasing agreement. The cost of this is to be determined and will be updated once new
information is received. Joining the shared purchasing agreement is a lower-cost alternative to
purchasing ingredients for meals wholesale as an individual program. The Needham Public
Health Division has a working relationship with the Director of Nutrition Services that will make
this possible.
The revolving fund will also cover the replacement asset value (RAV) cost, which is a fund that
accounts for regular maintenance and future parts replacement and repairs. The restaurant
industry standard RAV ranges from 2-5%. At a 5% RAV rate, the total RAV cost amounts to
$2,426 for Spring 2028 for small kitchen equipment.
The total estimated amount covered by the revolving fund in FY28 will be $9,351. The
breakdown for this amount is as follows:
● $657 is the estimated cost of cleaning supplies, disposable table settings, and to-
go containers.
● The total utility costs for running commercial kitchen appliances, including sewage,
electric, and water cost for running commercial kitchen appliances would be
$2,924.
● 1 hour of custodial costs on weekdays, paid at $20 an hour, is $1,600.
● $6,922 is the estimated cost of ingredients used to produce 35 meals per
weekday.
The Town will cover fringe benefits for Community Nutrition Program Staff. Because staff who
work more than 20 hours a week at NPS qualify for benefits, this is also reflected in this
program’s pay model. Benefits costs were determined based on the average benefits cost per
labor hour for state and local government workers according to the U.S. Bureau of Labor
Statistics, which is $14.87.
Revenue
For the purposes of the Community Nutrition Program, a modest 5% revenue model will be
implemented, aligning with restaurant industry standards. A modest anticipated gross revenue

for Spring FY28 is $7,262, generating $337 in profit that can be allocated to make up for other

following the conclusion of FY28.
Non-Financial Pre-Requisites
Volunteers play an important role in making the Traveling Meals and Springwell programs
possible. Drawing from an existing pool of dedicated volunteers from these programs, the
Community Nutrition Program will also benefit from their support, reducing overall program
costs. Volunteer responsibilities may include dishwashing, serving food, and preparing
ingredients.
FY29
FY29 Community Nutrition Program Model
In FY29, the Community Nutrition Program will expand its services by incorporating the functions
previously provided by Traveling Meals. This expansion will introduce a meal delivery option for
community members who are unable to leave their homes, offering lunch and dinner three to
five days a week. The 2028 program served lunch five days a week, three breakfasts a week,
and dinner once a month. With Traveling Meals, which distributed 856 meals in April 2025, the
average number of meals served per weekday is estimated to increase from 25 to 78. The
Community Nutrition Program will operate under this model from July 1, 2028 through June 30,
2029.
FY29 Costs, Funding, and Revenue
The total estimated requested costs for FY29 is $132,266, which includes wages for the Head
Chef, Community Nutrition Program Coordinator, and Food Service Worker, uniforms, and
computers. All wages account for a 2.25% COLA. The anticipated fringe benefit costs are
approximately $63,574 combined for all three roles.
With the increase in meal production in FY29, additional paid staff will be required to keep up
with the demands that come with serving more community members nutritious meals. A part-
time Food Service Worker will be added to the labor model for FY29 at 19.5 hours a week.
Additionally, the weekly hours for both the Head Chef and Program Coordinator will increase
from 30 to 40. With the introduction of the Traveling Meals component, additional volunteers will
also be recruited to support meal delivery efforts, following the model of current volunteers that
make the program possible.
The revolving fund will cover the following costs in FY29, which have been adjusted for a full
fiscal year and an increase in meal production:
● Custodial labor: $1,600
● Utilities: $2,92
● Cleaning supplies, disposable table settings, and to-go containers: $657
● RAV: $2,426
● Ingredients for 78 meals per weekday: $46,301

Description and Explanation
The Health and Human Services Department seeks a new full-time position: Head Chef for the
Community Nutrition Program, based in the Center at the Heights (CATH) kitchen.
The Head Chef’s responsibilities include ordering ingredients and supplies, menu development,
cooking meals, and training and managing volunteers’ work in the kitchen.
The hourly wage for this position is $23.61, based on Needham Public Schools’ (NPS) Nutrition
Services 2025 pay scale for an elementary cafeteria manager at Step 1. This rate was
determined by a 2.25% Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA) calculated for each additional year
between 2025 and 2028.
Estimated annual benefit costs for this position total $23,197, for 30 hours per week at $14.87
per labor hour. For FY28, benefit costs will be $7,732 at one-third of the annual cost given that
the program will only run for four months during the Spring.
FY28 annual cost for Head Chef: $39,664 wages + $23,197 benefits + $1,500 (one-
time purchase of a computer) + $500 (uniform)
FY28 actual cost (1/3 of annual cost): $13,730 + $8,030 benefits + $2,000
(computer and uniform)
FY29 Changes
In FY29, the addition of a Traveling Meals component will increase meal output for the
Community Nutrition Program. Based on the 856 meals distributed by Traveling Meals in April
2025, the projected average number of weekday meals will increase from 35 to 78 for FY29.
This growth will expand the Head Chef’s responsibility load, requiring an increase in hours from
30 to 40 per week. The position’s hourly wage will also rise to $26.07, reflecting a 2.5% COLA.
FY29 benefit costs will be $30,930, for 40 hours per week.
FY29 annual and actual cost for Head Chef: $54,207 wages + $30,930 benefits + $100
(uniform replacement, as needed)

FY29 Changes
In FY29, the addition of a Traveling Meals component will increase meal output for the
Community Nutrition Program. Based on the 856 meals distributed by Traveling Meals in April
2025, the projected average number of weekday meals will increase from 35 to 78 for FY29.
This growth will expand the Program Coordinator’s responsibility load, requiring an increase in
hours from 30 to 40 per week. The position’s hourly wage will also rise to $26.07, reflecting a
2.5% COLA. FY29 benefit costs will be $30,930, for 40 hours per week.
FY29 annual and actual cost for Program Coordinator: $54,207 wages + $30,930 benefits

=== Page 385 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request Supplement
DSR4S
Community Nutrition Program, Future
Position Title Priority
Food Service Worker (FY29) Form
The Health and Human Services Department seeks a new part-time position: Food Service
Worker for the Community Nutrition Program, based in the Center at the Heights (CATH)
kitchen.
In FY29, the addition of a Traveling Meals component will increase meal output for the
Community Nutrition Program. Based on the 856 meals distributed by Traveling Meals in April
2025, the projected average number of weekday meals will increase from 35 to 78 for FY29.
With this growth in meal production, additional paid staff will be required to keep up with the
demands that come with serving more community members nutritious meals. A part-time Food
Service Worker will be added to the labor model for FY29 at 19.5 hours a week. Their
responsibilities include preparing, serving, and delivering meals, supporting the Head Chef and
Program Coordinator, as well as cleaning and dishwashing. This position will not qualify for
benefits.
The hourly wage for this position is based on the approximate median of the pay range for
senior center food service roles in Massachusetts, which is anywhere from $15 to $24 per hour,
depending on experience. The hourly wage for this position is $21.31, based on Needham Public
Schools’ (NPS) Nutrition Services 2025 pay scale for cafeteria staff at Step 4. The Food Service
Worker’s final wage was determined by a 2.25% Cost-of-Living Adjustment (COLA) calculated
for each additional year between 2025 and 2028.
Estimated annual benefit costs for this position total $1,714.
FY29 annual cost for Food Service Worker: $23,852 wages + $1,714 benefits

=== Page 393 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Commission on Disabilities
Purpose
The Needham Commission on Disabilities (NCOD)’s purpose is to promote full integration and
improve accessibility for the disabled population in the Town of Needham. Through advocacy and
special projects and programs, the Commission provides resources in all matters related to
disability issues, promotes awareness, and addresses the needs of persons with disabilities in the
community.
Budget Statement
Commission members remain active in several community initiatives, in accordance with our
mission of awareness, education, advocacy and access. This past year, the committee embarked
on an outreach effort to downtown stores owners/occupants on increasing accessibility, voiced
support for the Trails for All state bill, supported an event for the low vision group in partnership
with the Council on Aging department, piloted a business recognition program for local businesses
in appreciation to their commitment to accessibility and inclusion, helped to promote a local
Disability film series and conducted an accessibility walk of the newly renovated Emery Grover and
reviewed plans for the development at 339 Chestnut St. Most initiatives are accomplished within
the established budget; however, we are increasing our education efforts with the addition of a
featured speaker(s) for public education purposes. The Commission also awards small amount
grants out of the Accessible Parking Fees fund, to community groups, for projects that benefit the
disabled community at large.
Budget Changes
The most significant change to the budget is due to the increase in the committee liaison stipend
from $1,500 to $3,000 per Town policy #427, Extra Committee Liaison. The expense portion of
the budget remains level funded.
Description Purpose Amount
Professional & Technical Education/Training/Programming $500
Office Supplies As needed $100
Other Supplies and Equipment Supplies for events, HP placards, etc. $250
Food & Service Supplies Food for community events $200
Town Liaison Stipend $3,000
Total Request $4,050

=== Page 396 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Historical Commission
Purpose
Serves as an advisory body to the Select Board on topics relative to historic preservation. The
Commission holds public hearings relative to the Town's Historic Building Demolition Delay By-law,
and renders judgement on certain proposed demolitions.
Budget Statement
The Historical Commission is required to hold noticed public hearings relative to the Needham
Historic Building Demolition By-law from time to time. These notices may require mailings and
associated postage costs. In FY2025, the Commission turned back 100% of its appropriation as it
did not hold any public hearings and incurred no expenses.
Budget Changes
There are no changes to the Historical Commission proposed budget for FY2027.
Description Purpose Amount
Communications (534x) Postage $525
Total Request $525

=== Page 399 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Public Library
Department Mission
Mission Statement: The Needham Free Public Library provides access to materials, resources and
information in its endeavor to expand minds, celebrate diversity, embrace differences, and build
community.
Vision Statements: The library will provide and expand resources and information to satisfy
curiosity and will provide opportunities to discover and share new ideas.
The library will be a vibrant and welcoming place for all people to gather, meet, and learn.
The library will create opportunities for programs, services and outreach by building partnerships
with institutions such as schools, businesses, and organizations.
On the Horizon
As we look toward Fiscal Year 2027, the Library is poised for continued growth and transformation,
building on the successes of the previous year. The rise in visitors and circulation in FY2025—with
a 5.23% increase in total visitors (277,878 compared to 264,074 in FY2024)—signals an ongoing
commitment to serving the needs of our community. With total checkouts also increasing to
595,828, up from 557,056, it is clear that patrons are not only returning but engaging more deeply
with Library resources. Similarly, the increase in programs—from 541 in FY2024 to 709 in FY2025,
with attendance reaching 21,249—underscores the community’s growing interest in diverse and
enriching offerings.
Looking ahead, the Library’s five-year Strategic Plan remains our guiding framework, ensuring that
every step we take in FY2027 aligns with our long-term vision of providing opportunities to meet
the needs of the Needham community. The momentum gained from recent progress will serve us
well as we continue implementing the initiatives outlined in our Annual Action Plan, developed in
close collaboration with Library staff and approved by the Board of Library Trustees. The Library’s
FY2025 Action Plan included 39 priority items while the FY2026 Action Plan includes 23 priority
items. Several important FY2026 priorities include establishing Customer Service Standards for all
Needham Free Public Library employees, completing a staffing analysis to guide Library
Administration in identifying unmet needs and service improvements, developing a proactive
community outreach plan, and creating a comprehensive communications plan. This vital work will
continue across the six critical goals of the FY2023-2027 Strategic Plan and staff will continue to
meet the growing daily needs of patrons across our collections, programs, and services. In the
second half of FY2027, the Library will also develop a process and timeline for the creation of the
FY2028–2032 Strategic Plan.
The completion and inauguration of the expanded Teen Room in the Spring of 2026 will mark a
milestone achievement, one that will continue to inspire and serve our growing teen population in
FY2027. With this vibrant new space in place, Library staff will remain focused on delivering
innovative programs that foster creativity, learning, and social connection. Building on the
successful collaboration with the Town of Needham’s Building Design and Construction Department
team for Phase 1 (Teen Room renovation), we anticipate a seamless transition into Phase 2 of our
multi-year/multi-phase renovation of the Library’s interior spaces. In FY2027, we look forward to
Phase 2 and the exciting opportunity to design a new Children’s Room.
With continued momentum, the renovation of the Children’s Room is set to be the Library’s next
major focus in enhancing Library services and spaces. While the Library remains open during
Section 3 - 331

=== Page 400 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Public Library
construction, our commitment to maintaining a welcoming and functional environment for all
patrons remains unwavering. Through careful planning and preparation, we are committed to
ensuring that the Library continues to serve as a hub of activity and engagement, even during
periods of disruption. These renovations are not just about updating physical spaces, but about
creating a more dynamic Library that can better serve our growing community for years to come.
Full-time employee salaries are contributing to additional costs in FY2027 as all of these staff
members are receiving step increases; none of our full-time staff have reached the top step of
their respective salary grade. This alone, outside of any potential cost of living adjustment for non-
represented Town employees in FY2027, represents a 1.62% increase over FY2026.
After careful review of our departmental scheduling needs for part-time staff, our FY2027 budget
request meets the standards of excellence Needham patrons have come to expect while ensuring
an efficient outlay of staffing resources. This review has resulted in a slight decrease of 1.80% in
comparison to FY2026 in our salary request for part-time staff.
As of late October 2025, we are seeking to fill three positions including a full-time Circulation
Services Supervisor, a full-time Technical Services Assistant and a part-time Children’s Reference
Librarian. Although the new hires for these full-time positions will be hired at a lower step, the
FY2027 salary request includes the salary levels of the former employees.
The Library’s FY2027 budget request includes three DSR4 forms. The first DSR4 form is a request
to hire a part-time Reference Librarian/Young Adult Specialist to help staff the new Teen Room on
Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday evenings as well as every Saturday. The second DSR4 form
requests to move the annual cost to maintain the Library’s website, which had more than 388,336
hits in FY2025, to the Town budget. This annual maintenance agreement includes the hosting of
the Library’s website, maintenance and upgrading of the website including custom plug-ins, and
30 hours of service time to support the Library’s website. In June 2021, the Library’s new website
launched thanks to funding from the Library Foundation of Needham and the Friends of Needham
Public Library. Since then the annual expense to maintain the Library’s website has been paid out
of a library trust fund (late FY2022), an unfunded line item in the Library’s operating budget
(5300/Library Professional & Technical Services in FY2024) and the Library’s Permanent Donation
Fund (FY2025). The Library’s website is a much-used and critical resource for the community to
access droves of information about the work we do. Shifting this operational expense to our Town
budget in an intentional way will allow us to continue to use other resources to innovate on behalf
of our patrons. The third DSR form requests to move the annual cost of Libstaffer, the Library’s
staff scheduling and management tool, to the Town budget. Purpose-built for libraries, LibStaffer
has streamlined staff scheduling, improved internal communication, and enhanced operational
efficiency. During FY2024, the Library implemented LibStaffer, a scheduling and management tool
developed by Springshare, using funds from Library State Aid. Given its effectiveness, the Library
renewed the subscription in FY2025 and FY2026, again using Library State Aid funds. As a public
library providing consistent service coverage, fair staff scheduling, and improving patron service
is essential. LibStaffer helps ensure our critical service points are always staffed appropriately.
Automated scheduling minimizes the chance of unstaffed hours, last-minute scrambles or
miscommunication, which directly affect our patrons. With 48 full-time and part-time staff covering
weekend and evening shifts, this tool effectively manages these scheduling complexities within a
single platform. It helps prevent service disruptions and saves administrative time, which can be
redirected toward community outreach and other patron support programs.
Budget Statement

In FY2025, the Library had a salary and wages budget return of $164,636.20 (8.5%). Due to
several resignations, the Library was without an Assistant Library Director for six months and
without a department supervisor for five months, in addition to a full-time Children’s Librarian and
eight other part-time staff vacancies. In total, there were 11 vacant positions over the course of
ten months. The three full-time vacancies were filled at lower pay steps than budgeted, as the
budget had been set at the rates of the employees who previously held those positions.
While essential functions continued throughout FY2025, the absence of three full-time staff
members affected salary and wage expenditures and resulted in an 8.5% budget return. Due to
these gaps in key roles, several priority items from the trustee-approved FY2025 Action Plan could
not be completed and have now been incorporated into the FY2026 Action Plan. We do not
anticipate a budget return of 3% or more in FY2026.
Accomplishments and Activities
Section 3 - 334

=== Page 403 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Public Library
The Library made substantial progress toward its goals and objectives during a busy year that saw
continued growth in visitors, materials checked out, and program attendance. The total number of
children’s materials checked out increased to 253,900, up from 248,227. This year’s summer
reading program also saw the highest participation in recent years, with more than 1,000 children
taking part.
The Library also worked to fill 11 position vacancies—representing 23% of its total workforce—
including key leadership positions such as the Library Assistant Director and Reference Supervisor
over the course of five to six months.
Major accomplishments during FY2025 include the completion of design work for the renovation
and expansion of the Teen Room and obtaining project approval at the May 2025 Annual Town
Meeting. Construction began in September 2025 and is expected to be completed in early spring
2026. This next phase sets the stage for the remaining three phases of the Library’s long-term
renovation plan. While maintaining a functional and welcoming space during construction will
present challenges, Library staff are committed to careful preparation and clear communication to
ensure a positive experience for all patrons throughout this exciting period of transformation.
The FY2025 Action Plan contained 39 priority items, and the Library achieved significant progress
on these goals despite staffing challenges. Highlights include the completion of onboarding
documentation for new employees and enhanced cross-training opportunities among library
departments. Library staff also strengthened the Library’s community presence through several
outreach initiatives, including the installation of a StoryWalk® on the Town’s rail trail, collaboration
with the Needham History Center and Museum, digitization of Needham High School yearbooks,
and reorganization of the Genealogy and Local History section of the Library’s website.
To better align acquisitions with patron demand, the Library developed a plan to reduce wait times
for popular print and digital materials. Several collection shifts were also completed to improve
accessibility, create a more cohesive collection, ensure materials remain current and in demand,
and prepare for the new Teen Room renovation project.

Description and Explanation
This funding request was submitted for the FY2025 and FY2026 operating budgets. It was not
approved. It is being re-submitted in order to allow the Library to hire a part-time Reference
Librarian/Young Adult Specialist to support the staffing needs of the new 4,000-square-foot
Teen Room scheduled to open in the Spring of 2026. This request aligns with Objective 1:
“Improve service, space, outreach, and resources for Emerging Adults” under Goal 3: “Offer a
user experience catered to the specific wants and needs of the Needham community” as
outlined in the Board of Library Trustees-approved FY2023-2027 Strategic Plan. This funding
request also acts in support of the Select Board’s Goal #1: Healthy and Socially Thriving for
FY20265-2027 as stated in “Cultivates a wide range of recreational, cultural, educational, civic,
and social opportunities for all socioeconomic and age groups,” “Supports positive mental
health throughout the community” and “Fosters inclusion, diversity, and equity in order to
become a culturally responsive, anti-racist, and anti-biased community.” As Phase 1, which is
quintupling the size of the Teen Room, is set for construction to be completed in Spring 2026, if
funding is approved at Annual Town Meeting in Spring 2026, this staffing need is timely and
critical to the success of the new Teen Room.
Currently, we see upwards of 100 teens visiting the Library after high school lets out across the
street. This population is served by a full-time Reference Librarian/Young Adult Specialist who

Part-Time Reference Librarian/Young
Title Priority 1
Adult Specialist
spends 40% of her time working as a Reference Librarian. During her off-desk time, she
concentrates on collection development for teens (selection and curation, weeding, and
marketing), participating in off-site outreach in schools, and developing, managing, and
running programming for teens; and by a part-time Reference Librarian/Young Adult Specialist
who works nine hours per week. This new part-time Reference Librarian/Young Adult Specialist,
who would work three evenings and on Saturdays would provide expanded opportunities for
teen programming and the delivery of library services. We expect the renovated and expanded
Teen Room to attract even more teens to the Library, especially in the evenings and on the
weekend. The new Teen Room will have a staff desk and the ability to provide a staff member
at that desk during the evenings and on Saturdays is crucial to properly serving our teen
patrons and provide staff oversight of an exciting, new space that the Town will have invested
millions of dollars in renovating and expanding.
If approved, this position would be recurring in future operating budgets.

marketing), participating in off-site outreach in schools, and developing, managing, and

Teen Room to attract even more teens to the Library, especially in the evenings and on the
weekend. The new Teen Room will have a staff desk and the ability to provide a staff member
at that desk during the evenings and on Saturdays is crucial to properly serving our teen
patrons and provide staff oversight of an exciting, new space that the Town will have invested
millions of dollars in renovating and expanding.

Description and Explanation
The request to incorporate the annual expense related to the hosting and maintenance of the
Needham Free Public Library’s website into its FY2027 operating budget is in support of the
Library’s Strategic Plan for FY2022-2027 approved by the Board of Library Trustees and its
related FY2025 Annual Action Plan. Specifically, this expense supports the following goals and
their related objectives of the Strategic Plan: Goal 2: Increase visibility as a critical community
partner, known and valued by everyone in Needham with Objective 1: Grow the Library’s
visibility and position as the heart of the community and Goal 3: Offer a user experience
catered to the specific wants and needs of the Needham community with Objective 4:
Strengthen the Library’s branding and marketing. Specifically, this expense supports the
following Priority Action Items in the FY2026 Action Plan: “Develop a proactive community
outreach plan with flexible internal procedures to reach patrons beyond the Library building”
and “Create and implement a holistic communications plan for the Library that includes all
manners by which the Library communicates with patrons and residents.”
We seek to move the annual cost to maintain the Library’s website, which had more than
388,336 hits in FY2025, to our Town budget. This annual maintenance agreement includes the
hosting of the Library’s website, maintenance and upgrading of the website including custom
plug-ins, and 30 hours of service time to support the Library’s website. In June 2021, the
Section 3 - 344

=== Page 413 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Fiscal
Department Public Library FY2027
Year
Title Website Hosting & Maintenance Priority 2
Library’s new website launched thanks to funding from the Library Foundation of Needham and
the Friends of Needham Public Library. Since then the annual expense to maintain the Library’s
website has been paid out of a library trust fund (late FY2022), an unfunded line item in the
Library’s operating budget (5300/Library Professional & Technical Services in FY2024) and the
Library’s Permanent Donation Fund (FY2025). The Library’s website is a much-used and critical
resource for the community to access droves of information about the work we do. Shifting this
operational expense to our Town budget in an intentional way will allow us to continue to use
other resources to innovate on behalf of our patrons.
If approved, this expense would be recurring in future operating budget requests.

Description and Explanation
The Needham Free Public Library requests that the annual expense for a staff scheduling and
management tool be incorporated into its FY2027 operating budget. This request supports the
Library’s Strategic Plan for FY2023–2027 and its related FY2026 Annual Action Plan, as approved
by the Board of Library Trustees. Specifically, this expense aligns with:
● Strategic Plan Goal 1: Provide a seamless, efficient, equitable, and outstanding user
experience. Objective 3: Ensure staff are appropriately allocated throughout the
organization
● FY2026 Action Plan Item: Assess and improve workflow and efficiencies across
departments, continuing efforts initiated in FY2024 under Goal 1, Objective 3.
During FY2024, the Library implemented LibStaffer, a scheduling and management tool
developed by Springshare, using funds paid out of Library State Aid. Purpose-built for libraries,
LibStaffer has streamlined staff scheduling, improved internal communication, and enhanced
operational efficiency. Given its effectiveness, the Library renewed the subscription in FY2025
and FY2026, again using Library State Aid funds.
Section 3 - 346

=== Page 415 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Performance Improvement Funding Request
DSR4
Fiscal
Department Public Library FY2027
Year
Title Staff Scheduling & Management Tool Priority 3
As a public library providing consistent service coverage, fair staff scheduling, and improving
patron service is essential. LibStaffer helps ensure our critical service points are always staffed
appropriately. Automated scheduling minimizes the chance of unstaffed hours, last-minute
scrambles or miscommunication, which directly affect our patrons. With 48 full-time and part-
time staff covering weekend and evening shifts, this tool effectively manages these scheduling
complexities within a single platform. It helps prevent service disruptions and saves
administrative time, which can be redirected toward community outreach and other patron
support programs.
Moving this recurring expense to the Town budget is a proactive step to free up Library State Aid
to support innovation and new services for patrons.
If approved, this expense would be recurring in future operating budget requests.
V2027

=== Page 420 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===

Department Information
DSR1
Department Park and Recreation
Department Mission
The Park and Recreation Department’s major responsibilities include providing diverse, inclusive,
and equitable year-round programming and leisure services for residents of all ages;
administering all Town athletic fields, including scheduling, and permitting functions; managing
Town playgrounds; operating and managing the Pools at the Rosemary Recreation Complex;
providing seasonal employment and volunteer opportunities; administration of all Town trails;
and providing support to community organizations.
The Park and Recreation Commission is a five-person elected board. The Commission has
jurisdiction over approximately 300 acres of land, including the Town Forest. They set policies
related to recreation facilities and programs, including the use of all athletic fields, except Memorial
Park. Working with the Director, the Commission provides long-range planning for recreation
facilities and services.
On the Horizon
We continue to march towards upgrading and renovating our 18 playgrounds in Needham to not
only be ADA compliant, but to move to universally inclusiveness within each playground.
Additionally, our Capital Improvement Plan reflects our drive to ensure equality among facilities
including equitable field availability for all youth sports.
We continue to add additional options to our plans such as a skate park, pump track, pickleball
courts and disc golf within the next 5-10 years.
Due to the success of our playground assessment, the Department and Commission are looking
into an Athletic Field Assessment to determine the exact needs of each of our thirty-five (35) fields.
In addition to a field assessment and playground projects, the Department will be working on
projects around town such as the Claxton Field Renovations, Needham High School Tennis Court
renovations, Rosemary Pool Shade Upgrades & various playground renovations
Seasonal, Temporary & Part-time Staff Salaries – In order to hire and maintain quality and capable
staff, we have updated the seasonal salary scale to increase our starting wages across all part-
time and temporary staff:
Schedule C Wages – Effective July 1, 2025:

=== Page 421 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Park and Recreation
Budget Statement
In FY2025, the department turned back to the town over 17% of our operating budget. About
11.5% of this turnback is salaries. We were understaffed for about six months as the department
went through a re-organization. We moved from having an Administrative Specialist to an
Administrative Analyst. During the search for the Administrative Analyst, we were down to just
four staff members. The other 5.5% return was due to using our Revolving Fund for expenses that
should have been coming out of the Revolving Fund, but in past years had come out of the
Operating Budget. For those items, I have adjusted the budget accordingly.
The Park and Recreation Commission and Department review program fees at least three times a
year, adjusting fees as appropriate, with the intent of having most programs be self-sustaining.
The fees for the 2024 pool season were updated and approved in December of 2023. Most of the
department’s programs are in the Revolving Fund, but the major summer programs are all
funded through the Operating Budget and the revenue is deposited into the General Fund. The
pool rates for the 2025 season will be voted on in December 2024.
Revolving Fund: The programs held in the Fall, Winter, and Spring, as well as some small summer
programs, are operated through the fee-generated Revolving Fund (53D). The Revolving Fund
also includes revenue and expenses related to the Field Maintenance Fee, Court Badge Fee,
Memorial Park Lights Fee, DeFazio Park Lights Fee, Claxton Lights Fee, and the Carleton Pavilion
Fee.
Gift Fund: The Park and Recreation Commission has a Gift Fund for a variety of projects. The
majority of the funds hold donations for the Arts in the Parks concert series and the Children’s
Theatre programs. The donation of $20,000 from the estate of Harold J. A. Street is also in the
fund, with a balance of approximately $9,000.
The following information outlines the dollar changes to specific budget lines:

Accomplishments and Activities
Programmatically, the Department continues to collaborate with three school departments, the
Post-Grad program, Extended School Year (ESY) and the Summer Bridges program. Our Post-
Grads interned with us 2-3 days a week at the pool and in the office doing cleaning, shredding
and general office organization. ESY kids attended their scheduled classes during the mornings
and met up with our staff for fun-filled afternoons. At Summer Bridges, children attend reading
and math classes in the morning and then were able to come outside and join our summer
camps in the afternoons. All programs were a huge success and we are continuing with our Post-
Grad interns through the school-year.

=== Page 433 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Memorial Park Trustees
Purpose
In accordance with Part 5 section 33 of the Town of Needham Charter, the land known as the
Memorial Park in the Town of Needham shall continue to be under the jurisdiction of the Trustees
of Memorial Park. There are five elected Trustees, each for a term of three years, plus the Chair
of the Select Board, or designee. Memorial Park includes the memorial garden with an adjacent
gazebo, a natural grass 90’ baseball diamond, a synthetic multi-purpose field, a synthetic 60’ turf
diamond and a pedestrian walking path circling the park. All fields are lighted. The Trustees host
events on Memorial Day, Veteran’s Day and Purple Heart Day.
Budget Statement
The primary operating costs for the Memorial Park fieldhouse and the athletic fields are in the
Department of Public Works (DPW) operating budget, primarily in the Building Maintenance and
Parks and Forestry divisions, with a small portion covered by the Park and Recreation Department.
The primary expenses related to the building include electricity and heat. The electrical costs are
under the Needham Electric, Light and Gas Program budget, and other expenses including cleaning
and maintenance are covered by DPW. In recent years, the Trustees of Memorial Park have voted
to use their budget for the purchase of American and POW flags for the flagpoles as the Park as
well as some small projects which benefit the park.
Budget Changes
Flag pricing continues to increase each year. Last year the budget was increased so as to not rely
as much on the Park and Recreation department to make up the gap of funds vs needs.
Additionally, there are more small projects including repairs and maintenance needs at the Park.
This increase will help to alleviate the continued reliance on the Park and Recreation department’s
budget.
Description Purpose Amount
Other Supplies and Equipment American and POW flags, Signage $1,250
Repairs and Maintenance Bench and garden repairs $1,000
Total Request $2,250
V2027

=== Page 436 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Needham Arts & Culture Council (NCAC)
Purpose
The purpose of the Needham Council for Arts and Culture (NCAC) is to support community-based
projects and activities in the performing and visual arts, humanities, and interpretive sciences to
benefit the residents of Needham.
Budget Statement
The NCAC requests funding in the amount of $18,300 to support local arts and culture
programming and sustain permanent, publicly funded art in Needham. These funds are intended
for to be spent across three major categories: grants to local arts and culture organizations, public
art installations, and sustainment of existing public art installations. Each aspect of the requested
budget works towards implementing the long-range Arts and Culture Plan.
Grants: The cornerstone of the NCAC’s work and the primary area intended use for this funding is
to provide local arts and cultural institutions grants to support their work in our community. All
grants directly benefit Needham through events, public art, and community art projects such as
Needham Open Studios, performing arts courses at the Center at the Heights, and hosting
Revolutionary War reenactors on the Town Common. Lectures, concerts, story time crafts, and
performances like these bring the community together. It is especially important, now more than
ever, to nurture community connection and build on the incredible success that the NCAC has
accomplished in the past few years.
Public Art Installations: The NCAC commissioned and funded the placement of public murals in
Needham Heights and Needham Center in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Both murals were funded
by ARPA funds, which are no longer available. Moving forward, the NCAC seeks to continue funding
public art installation with a portion of its Town-appropriated funds, while still continuously seeking
alternate funding sources to support public art installations.
Budget Changes
Description Purpose Amount
NCAC Annual Funding Funds grants, public art installations, and $18,300
sustainment of existing public art
installations
Total Request $18,300

All “YES” responses above must be explained in the narrative below
Description and Explanation
The NCAC seeks an additional $8,050 in FY2027. $7,000 is intended to reduce a funding gap
between qualifying grant applications and available resources. $1,050 is intended to sustain
existing public art installations. Due to the NCAC’s continued impact and successful outreach to the
arts and cultural community, grant applications have risen 24% from FY2024 to FY2025. Over these
two fiscal years, the NCAC faced a funding gap of 26% between qualifying funds requested by grantees
and the grants the NCAC could allocate. This gap would have been larger in FY2025, if not for the use
of (now exhausted) ARPA funds to support a public art installation and the subsequent shift of those
funds to grants. In addition to funds appropriated by Town Meeting, the Massachusetts Cultural Council
(MCC) distributes funding to NCAC annually. For the last two years, this funding has been $9,400. In
the 2025 MCC funding cycle, the NCAC encumbered the entire sum for grants.
Sustainment of Existing Public Art Installations: After installation, public art is occasionally
damaged or faces minor degradation during its intended life. To maintain the integrity and quality
of installed public art, the NCAC seeks to use funds to pay the artist (or their designee) for such
small repairs, a best practice documented in peer communities.

Section 3 - 371

=== Page 440 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Sewer Enterprise
Department Mission
The Sewer Division of the Department of Public Works is responsible for maintaining the Sanitary
Sewer System (Sewer) and the Storm Sewer System (Drains), as well as all related programs
and infrastructure.
On the Horizon
The overall budget On the Horizon statement can be found under the Department of Public Works.
The Sewer Division continued to see operational challenges in the current employment market
and delays caused by the increase in material costs and the supply chain shortages.
The Water, Sewer, and Drains Division saw a lot of staff turnover, causing the Division to re-
evaluate their operational processes and needs. The Sewer Division of the Department of Public
Works continued to perform the daily maintenance tasks, both proactive and reactive, to keep
our sewer and drain systems operating efficiently.
The items below detail additional and specific work that the division will be undertaking in the
upcoming fiscal year.
The Sewer Division continues to perform the daily maintenance tasks, both proactive and reactive
to keep our sewer and drain systems operating, while also focusing on meeting the stormwater
requirements that went into effect with the Municipal Separate Stormwater Systems (MS4) permit
in July 2018. This involves the cleaning and maintenance of brooks and culverts as well as catch
basin cleaning. The Division will also continue to perform the regularly scheduled flushing
programs, inspect sewer and drain lines with the CCTV camera truck, and thoroughly clean all
sewer station wet wells on a routine basis. The Division will continue to maintain and improve
their sewer flow monitoring system that monitors for inflow and infiltration throughout town and
at all ten sewer pump stations. The Sewer & Drain Division continues to recruit, hire and train
new staff as well as providing existing staff with training. We continue to combat staffing
shortages, which have been the most challenging within the Water, Sewer, and Drains Divisions.
The Town will continue to implement new billing and collections software upgrades over the next
year.
Budget Statement
Salaries & Wages increasing $14,628, 1.15%
Expenses & Services increasing $18,991, 2.1%
Operating Capital flat $0, 0%
Total Budget Submission* increasing $33,619, 1.45%
Salaries
Only one group within DPW has established COLAs via negotiation which is NIPEA set at 2.5% at
the beginning of FY27 with an additional 0.5% in January 2027. For the purposes of the budget
all non-represented staff have no COLA in their payroll adjustments.
Across the organization, regular salaries are mostly stable and the bonus type pay of stipends,
incentives and overtime is trending up. In FY26 incentive and stipend pay was included in the
salary line. For budgetary practices, these needed to be removed and added in its own line, hence
the internal shift in lines.

Highlighted Changes
Sewer Maintenance - 4440: FY27 budget is primarily stable with modest growth. Increases
primarily due to inflationary cost increases, contract obligations and being fully staffed. The
largest percentage increase was Certification & Licenses and Clothing. The largest dollar increase
was Sewer Flow Meter Monitoring and Diesel. Cost reductions were in Unleaded Gasoline and
Seminars.
Sewer Pump Stations - 4443: Energy and maintenance are the key cost drivers, consistent with
rising utility and contractor costs.
Drains - 4901: This division remains stable with minor adjustments. The 29% drop in fuel costs,
balancing small increases in contracted services
Drains Division
• The Drains division’s budget remains largely stable with a few small adjustments:
• Diesel fuel decreases by $319 (-29.2%), reflecting reduced fuel use or efficiency gains.
• Seminars and training fall by $250 (-10%), as the department is now fully staffed and
requires fewer additional sessions.
• CCTV truck supplies decrease slightly by $10 (0%), consistent with historic spending
levels.

=== Page 442 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Sewer Enterprise
• Debris disposal for sweeping increases by $503 (+1.5%), and catch basin debris disposal
rises by $1,525 (+3.5%), both due to contract escalations.
• Street sweeping services increase by $1,248 (+3.5%), also from contractual rate
adjustments.
• Vactor truck parts rise by $53 (+4%), a small increase reflecting typical supply cost
changes.
Sewer Division
• The Sewer division shows more notable growth in certain categories:
• Unleaded gasoline decreases by $241 (-1.9%), indicating stable or reduced
consumption.
• Diesel fuel increases by $1,064 (+9.1%), likely due to rate adjustments or usage
differences.
• Electricity rises by $1,755 (+1.3%), and natural gas jumps by $2,459 (+28.5%),
reflecting higher utility costs.
• Generator maintenance increases by $122 (+3.5%), electrical on-call services by $482
(+4%), and mechanical on-call services by $2,408 (+4%), all tied to contract renewals.
• Wet well cleaning grows by $1,750 (+7%), and alarm/sprinkler maintenance by $41
(+8.8%), both showing moderate contractual increases.
• Pump inspections increase by $1,500 (+50%), as the number of inspections rises from
two to three annually.
• MWRA sulfide testing grows by $391 (+4%), and sewer flow meter monitoring increases
by $2,560 (+4%), both from standard contract escalations.
• Clothing rises by $1,000 (+50%) and certifications/licenses by $200 (+50%), due to the
division being fully staffed.
• Asphalt increases by $922 (+6%), reflecting material contract adjustments.
• CCTV truck supplies and vactor truck parts increase slightly by $10 (0%) and $53
(+4%), respectively, consistent with historic usage.
• Seminars and training fall by $250 (-10%), again reflecting fewer additional training
needs.
Operating Capital
Pump Replacement Program
The Sewer Division will be purchasing a new replacement pump as back up for the pumps in our
sewer stations.
Overall Operating outlook: budget growth is controlled and inflationary, not expansionary;
increases reflect cost-of-living adjustments across the operational expenses. The primary drivers
are maintenance, purchased service costs and rising energy. The offset factors are the decreases
fuel expenditures.

Accomplishments and Activities
The Sewer Division performed regularly scheduled maintenance tasks in addition to larger
projects, and unanticipated projects for the benefit of the public. The Division also responded to
work order requests initiated through the Town’s online reporting tool, SeeClickFix.
The Sewer Division performed regularly scheduled cleaning of stormwater drain catch basins
through-out town. This involved removal and proper disposal of sediment from the stormwater
drain system.
During FY2025, the Sewer Division repaired or replaced failing stormwater catch basins and
associated drain pipes. The division also performed pipeline point repairs on the sewer and drain
lines.
The Division also CCTV’d approximately 81,000 feet of sewer pipe, approximately 69,000 feet of
storm drainpipe, and flushed/jetted approximately 200,000 feet of sewer and drainpipes within
the Town’s roadways and easements. The Division monitors and maintains the requirements
under our MWRA sewer discharge permit by performing scheduled routine flushing and cleaning
of the sewer lines.
Using the CCTV truck in conjunction with the Jet/Vactor/Flush truck allowed the Division to
investigate the sewer and drain lines to proactively locate any defects, potential blockages and
make any necessary repairs prior to the completion of scheduled road surface improvements.

=== Page 455 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Water Enterprise
Department Mission
The Water Division of the Department of Public Works is responsible for maintaining the
infrastructure associated with the water delivery system and the development and production of
the water supply in the Charles River Well Field, as well as supplemental water from the
Massachusetts Water Resources Authority (MWRA).
On the Horizon
The overall budget On the Horizon statement can be found under the Department of Public Works.
The Water Division has seen operational challenges and delays caused by the increase in material
costs and the supply chain shortages. The Water, Sewer, and Drains Division saw a lot of staff
turnover, causing the Division to re-evaluate their operational processes and needs.
The Water Division of the Department of Public Works continues to perform daily operational
maintenance tasks, both proactive and reactive to keep our water, sewer and drain systems
operating efficiently. The items below detail additional and specific work that the division will be
undertaking in the upcoming fiscal year.
The Water Division will continue the maintenance, improvement, and repair of the overall water
distribution system, including but not limited to replacing/repairing fire hydrants, water services,
water mains, water gate valves, and water meters. The division will continue their testing and
inspection programs which look at all commercial, municipal, and industrial cross-connection and
backflow prevention devices. The Water Division completes annual water distribution leak detection
services, proactively locating leaks and repairing them before they become a water break or a
source of Un-Accounted for Water (UAW). The Massachusetts Department of Environmental
Protection (MA DEP) developed their Primary Drinking Water regulations regarding PFAS (per- and
polyfluoroalkyl substances) and require monthly sampling protocols. Due to the hard work of the
Water Division, Needham’s PFAS levels are consistently below the Maximum Contaminant Level
(MCL) of 20 ppt. Needham’s averages are approximately 8 to 11 ppt, which is below the (MCL) 20
ppt. The Water Division is currently doing monthly PFAS sampling.
There continues to be a shortage of those in the water industry with the appropriate distribution
and treatment licenses. The Water Division will continue to evaluate ways to increase licensures
of existing staff. The Division continues recruiting new staff with the necessary licensures in an
attempt to combat the workforce shortages seen in the DPW, which have been prominent in the
Water, Sewer & Drain Divisions. The Town will continue to implement new billing and collections
software upgrades over the next year.
Budget Statement
Salaries & Wages increasing $8,823, 0.5%
Expenses & Services increasing $34,464, 2.02%
Operating Capital -$31,984, -68.07%
Total Budget Submission $11,303, 0.32%
Salaries
The only groups with COLAs via negotiation are NIPEA at 2.5% at the beginning of FY27 with an
additional 0.5% in January 2027 and ITWA at 3%. For the purposes of the budget all non-
represented staff have no COLA in their payroll adjustments.
Across the organization, regular salaries are mostly stable and the bonus type pay of stipends,

Highlighted Changes
The FY27 budget represents a modest overall increase for inflation, contract updates and
operational needs. Across both 4500 and 4501, with the most significant drivers being Repairs &
Maintenance and Communications increases. Overall, the Water Division saw a growth of (2.02%)
modest and appears well-contained.
Detail Breakdown:
Largest Increases
The most notable budget increases in FY27 reflect inflation adjustments, and corrections to
previously under-budgeted items.
• Postage – CCR & Misc shows the largest single increase, rising by $23,000, or 460%. This sharp
jump reflects a correction for prior under-budgeting, ensuring adequate funds for regulatory
mailings and community correspondence.
Section 3 - 388

=== Page 457 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Water Enterprise
• Mechanical & Electrical On-Call Services increased by $25,000, representing the correction in
accounting for the FY27 budget. This services provides flexibility to address urgent
maintenance and specialized repair needs that were previously absorbed elsewhere.
• Trench Restoration rose by $10,000, a 33.3% increase, aligning the budget with historical
spending averages.
• Well Redevelopment increased by $8,600, or 20.8%, based on the department’s three-year
spending average.
• Laboratory Chemicals (Reagents) increased by $3,000, a 60% rise
• Natural Gas increased by $4,357, or 7.9%, consistent with recent market energy cost trends.
• Several treatment chemicals (including sodium hydroxide, phosphate, and sodium
hypochlorite) rose between 3.0% and 3.1%, representing standard inflationary adjustments
for recurring consumables.
Largest Decreases
The largest decreases are concentrated in maintenance and supply categories, driven by reduced
needs, and budget realignments to prior-year averages.
• Fluoride Pumps decreased by $5,500, a 100% reduction, as no purchases are anticipated for
FY27.
• Tank Inspections (Dunster and Bird Hill) dropped by $4,500, or 60%, since major cleanings
occur only every 3–5 years per Massachusetts DEP guidelines.
• Professional Association Memberships (Dept 4501) fell by $1,800, a 60% decrease, following a
reassessment of necessary memberships and prior spending trends.
• Leak Detection Services decreased by $5,000, or 16.7%, based on the prior three-year
average, reflecting lower anticipated contract needs.
• Vehicle Supplies, Tires, and Batteries declined by $3,000, or 37.5%, also realigned with actual
historical usage.
• Electricity saw a moderate decrease of $13,736, or 2.6%, likely reflecting efficiency measures
or lower projected consumption.
• Additional smaller reductions include certifications and licenses (down $300, or 37.5%) and
gate valves and repair sleeves (down $4,685, or 12.4%), both tied to recalibration based on
recent expenditure patterns.
Funding for only a ride-on lawn mower under operating capital in FY27 as compared to $46,984 in
capital in FY26 caused a decrease offsetting the overall budget.

Accomplishments and Activities
The Water Division performed regular operational and maintenance tasks in addition to larger
projects as well as impromptu projects for the benefit of the public. The Division also responded
to work order requests initiated through the Town’s online reporting tool, SeeClickFix.
The Water Division staff repaired, removed or replaced various fire hydrants that weren’t
functioning correctly, or were damaged or leaking. Staff also performed numerous water leak
repairs during normal business hours and emergency water repairs after-business hours. Staff
replaced various size inoperable water gate valves and removed lead water services and replaced
them with new copper services when encountered during these repairs. Staff also performed water
main improvements on Tower Ave extension between Mitchell School and Lindberg Ave by
replacing approximately 250 feet of the existing small diameter failing steel water line with a new
6-inch diameter Ductile Iron water main, adding a new fire hydrant and new copper water service
connections to the properties for improved water and fire protection service in the area.
The Water Division continues to replace and install new water meters, responds to customer and

=== Page 460 | Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget ===
Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget
Department Information
DSR1
Department Water Enterprise
resident calls for leaks, and performs daily water quality testing. Staff continued testing all
backflow protection devices and surveyed facilities to ensure cross-connection compliance.
Additionally, during FY2025 the DPW’s Water, Sewer & Drains Division hired a contractor to remove
and replace approximately 59 water service pipes throughout town in a proactive manner to
prepare for the new 2024 EPA’s/DEP’s Lead Copper Rule (LCR) Regulation implementation on
October 16, 2024. The Division also hired Weston & Sampson to redevelop one of our three water
wells at the Charles River Water Treatment Facility. This redevelopment consists of reconditioning
the well and associated pumping equipment for better well (water) production and pumping
efficiencies.

Budget Request
Short Form
Budget Community Preservation Committee
Purpose
The Community Preservation Act (CPA) M.G.L. c. 44B, allows Massachusetts cities and towns to
raise monies through a surcharge of up to 3% of the real estate tax levy on real property. These
funds may then be used to acquire, create and preserve open space; acquire, rehabilitate, restore
and preserve historic resources; acquire, create, preserve and support community housing; and
acquire, create, rehabilitate, restore and preserve land for recreational use. Community housing
and open space may only be rehabilitated or restored if purchased with Community Preservation
funds. The legislation was updated in July 2012. Needham voted to accept the legislation in
November 2004, with a 2% surcharge. The Community Preservation Committee (CPC) is appointed
to make recommendations for use of the Community Preservation Fund.
The Community Preservation Committee develops and maintains an annual five-year lookout on
projects, with assistance from the Town Manager and staff.
To receive Community Preservation funding, proponents must first apply, and the Community
Preservation Committee must then determine if the proposed project is eligible for CPA funding.
Eligibility determinations are based upon guidance provided by the Community Preservation
Coalition and Town Counsel, and as authorized by the Community Preservation Act. Once a project
is deemed eligible, the proponent must answer the Committee's due diligence questions and
present the full scope of the project to the Committee. The proponent will also present the project
to the public during the Committee's public hearing. At the public hearing, the Committee
encourages feedback from the community in support or non-support of the projects under review.
The Committee also consults with the Select Board and the Finance Committee on proposed
projects prior to making a final recommendation for funding to Town Meeting.
Budget Statement
The Massachusetts Community Preservation Act permits up to 5% of annual revenues to be spent
on administrative and operating expenses. Although the Committee does not typically use the full
amount each year, it does set aside $82,000 of annual revenues to be prepared for unknown
expenses. This is well under the maximum of 5%. At the end of each fiscal year, unused
administrative funds are returned to the Community Preservation Fund Free Cash.
Since the Committee was established, it has primarily utilized the administrative budget for
personnel expenses, office supplies, and communication. Funds are also reserved for the
Committee to investigate and research proposed projects to determine their viability for funding,
which has at times included retention of a consultant to advise on highly technical and/or
specialized aspects of a proposed project.
The Director of Administration in the Department of Public Works has been appointed by the Town
Manager to serve as staff liaison to the Committee. The Committee's administrative budget
typically pays for staff support, including an Administrative Coordinator and use of Town Counsel.
The Needham Community Preservation Committee is a member of the Massachusetts Community
Preservation Coalition and pays annual membership dues. From time to time, the Committee
consults with Coalition staff on issues related to project applications. Coalition staff have also
provided Committee members with education and training regarding CPA administration.
The May 2025 Annual Town Meeting authorized $6,560,895 through Community Preservation
funds to support six projects. The Needham History Center and Museum’s Collections Storage
Upgrades project was determined to be eligible as Historic Preservation and was funded through
Section 3 - 402

Fiscal Year 2027 Proposed Budget

Short Form
Budget Community Preservation Committee
the Historic Resources Reserve ($46,712). The Needham Conservation Department’s Trail Signage
Improvements project was determined to be eligible as Recreation and was funded through CPA
Free Cash ($104,000). The Charles River Center’s East Militia Heights Development project was
